meta.title: Korean Labor Standards Calculators — Workmate
meta.description: Annual leave, weekly rest pay, severance, minimum wage — calculators based on Korea's Labor Standards Act with step-by-step explanations and legal citations.

laborCalcHub.heading: Tenure, leave, and pay in one place
laborCalcHub.intro: Built on Korea's Labor Standards Act §55 and §60 plus the Severance Act. Each tool shows the inputs and the legal calculation step by step.

annual-leave.title: Annual Leave Calculator
annual-leave.description: Enter your hire date — get accrued leave days plus unused-leave compensation. Handles sub-1-year monthly accrual and 1-year-plus increments.

weekly-rest-pay.title: Weekly Rest Pay
weekly-rest-pay.description: Weekly holiday allowance for employees working 15+ hours per week, from hourly wage and weekly hours.

severance.title: Severance Pay
severance.description: Severance for 1+ year tenure: 30 days of average wage × years served, based on your last 3 months' pay.

min-wage-monthly.title: Min Wage → Monthly
min-wage-monthly.description: Converts the 2026 minimum wage to monthly pay (209 hours) and compares annual salary and take-home.

meta.title: Korean Severance Pay Calculator (Avg vs Ordinary Wage)
meta.description: Hire date, resign date, last 3 months' salary → average daily wage + ordinary wage comparison → severance auto. Optional bonus + unused-leave additions.

severanceTool.heading: Severance Pay Calculator
severanceTool.intro: Average daily wage × 30 × (service days ÷ 365). Higher of average or ordinary daily wage applies. Optional 3/12 portion of annual bonus and unused-leave pay.

hints.recentThreeMonthsSalary: Base + fixed allowances summed over 3 months
hints.annualBonus: Regular and definite bonuses only (3/12 added)
hints.annualLeavePay: Last 1 year's unused leave pay (3/12 added)
hints.monthlyOrdinaryWage: Used if ordinary daily wage > average

result.heading: Result
result.empty: Enter dates and wages.
result.notQualifiedBody: Tenure {days} days — under 365 days = no severance (Worker Retirement Benefits Act §4).

steps.heading: Steps
steps.averageWage: Average = (3-month wage {threeMonthSalary} + bonus 3/12 {bonusPortion} + leave 3/12 {leavePayPortion}) ÷ 90 days = daily {averageDailyWage}

errors.unknown: An error occurred during calculation.

severanceTool.source: Worker Retirement Benefits Act §8 (severance system). Average wage calculation can vary by company and industry. Some companies pay severance for under-1-year service.

law.title: Eligibility
law.body: 1+ year continuous service AND average 15+ hours per week (4-week avg). Triggered regardless of voluntary or involuntary termination. Applies to workplaces under 5 too (since 2010-12-01).

average.title: Average wage
average.body: Last 3 months' total wage ÷ days in that period (typically 90). Regular bonus = 1/4 of annual; unused-leave pay = 1/4 of last year's, both added in.

dc.title: DC vs DB
dc.body: DC (defined contribution): 1/12 monthly wage accumulated each year. This calculator assumes DB (defined benefit). DC final amount depends on investment returns.

validation.salaryPositive: Please enter the last 3 months' wage.
validation.hireBeforeResign: Hire date must be before resign date.

meta.title: Weekly Rest Pay Calculator (Korea, LSA §55)
meta.description: Enter hourly or monthly wage → weekly, monthly, annual rest pay. Part-time prorated (15+ hours/week) automatically per Labor Standards Act §55.

weeklyRestPayTool.heading: Weekly Rest Pay Calculator
weeklyRestPayTool.intro: Paid weekly rest day pay for workers attending all scheduled days. Under 15 hours/week = no rest pay; 15-40 hours = prorated. Hourly or monthly input.

result.heading: Result
result.empty: Enter wage and weekly hours.
result.notQualifiedBody: Weekly {hours}h — under 15h/week is not eligible for weekly rest pay (LSA Enforcement Decree §30).

errors.unknown: An error occurred during calculation.

weeklyRestPayTool.source: Labor Standards Act §55, Enforcement Decree §30. Company rules and collective agreements may differ.

law.title: LSA §55
law.body: Workers attending all scheduled work days in a week receive 1 paid rest day. 'Full attendance' = no absences. Holidays don't count as absences.

formula.title: Part-time proration
formula.body: Part-time rest pay = (weekly hours ÷ 40) × 8 hours × hourly wage. Capped at 8 hours. 30h/week = 6h pay, 20h/week = 4h pay.

exception.title: Not eligible
exception.body: Under 15 hours/week, weeks without full attendance, daily-hire workers (in principle). Applies to workplaces with under 5 employees too. Employer-caused absences may count as attendance.

validation.wageRequired: Please enter hourly or monthly wage.
validation.weeklyHoursPositive: Weekly hours must be greater than 0.

meta.title: Hourly → Monthly Salary Converter (Korea, with weekly rest)
meta.description: Convert hourly wage to monthly salary and annual income using Korea's standard 209-hour basis (40-hour week + weekly rest pay). Part-time prorated.

minWageTool.heading: Hourly → Monthly / Annual
minWageTool.intro: Convert hourly wage to monthly salary using Korea's 209-hour standard (40h/week + weekly rest pay). Part-time hours auto-prorated.

hints.hourlyWage: 0 = use min wage for selected year
hints.includeWeeklyRest: Workers with 15+ weekly hours qualify for weekly rest pay

result.heading: Result
result.empty: Enter hourly wage and weekly hours, then convert.

errors.unknown: An error occurred during conversion.

minWageTool.source: Labor Standards Act §55 (weekly rest day). 209 hours is the administrative standard for monthly working hours. Actual pay may differ per company rules.

formula.title: Where does 209 hours come from?
formula.body: 40 weekly work hours + 8 weekly-rest hours = 48 weekly. 52 weeks ÷ 12 ≈ 4.345 weeks/month. 48 × 4.345 ≈ 208.56 → administratively rounded to 209.

partTime.title: Part-time proration
partTime.body: Under 15 weekly hours → no weekly rest pay. 15-40 hours → weekly rest pay = (weekly hours / 5) hours per week, capped at 8.

minWage.title: Minimum wage decision
minWage.body: Announced yearly by Aug 5 for the following year. 2025 = 10,030 KRW / 2026 = 10,320 KRW (+2.9%, MOEL Notice No. 2025-47).

meta.title: Korean VAT Calculator — Extract & Compute
meta.description: Split tax-inclusive prices into supply + VAT, add VAT to ex-tax amounts, compute filing tax for general taxpayers, and apply industry-specific value-added rates for simplified taxpayers.

vatTool.heading: VAT Calculator
vatTool.intro: Extract supply value and VAT from a tax-inclusive price, or compute filing tax for general / simplified Korean taxpayers — including industry value-added rates.

amount.add: Pre-VAT supply value to add VAT to

result.heading: Result
result.empty: Enter an amount and click Calculate.

errors.unknown: An error occurred during calculation.

vatTool.source: Source: VAT Act §30 (10% rate), §63 (simplified taxpayer rates). For reference only — actual filing requires deductible input verification and exempt-sales segregation.

law.title: VAT Act §30
law.body: 10% on supply of goods and services. Supply value = total ÷ 1.1. Businesses file by netting sales tax against input tax.

simple.title: Simplified taxpayer
simple.body: Prior-year sales under KRW 104M. Industry value-added rate (15-40%) is applied, then 10% VAT. No mandatory tax invoice issuance.

deadline.title: Filing deadlines
deadline.body: General: H1 (Jan-Jun) by Jul 25, H2 (Jul-Dec) by Jan 25 next year. Simplified: once a year by Jan 25. Provisional filings due quarterly on the 25th.

meta.title: Annual Leave Calculator — Korea (LSA §60)
meta.description: Enter your hire date and salary — get accrued annual leave days and unused-leave compensation per Korea's Labor Standards Act §60. Handles sub-1-year accrual and 25-day cap.

annualLeaveTool.heading: Annual Leave Calculator
annualLeaveTool.intro: Hire date, salary, unused days — get accrued leave and unused-leave compensation in one go. Sub-1-year monthly accrual and 2-year increments are auto-handled.

hints.referenceDate: Defaults to today. Use for forward simulation.
hints.dailyOrdinaryWage: If you've already calculated daily wage
hints.unusedDays: Days to convert into compensation (0 = accrual only)

result.heading: Result
result.empty: Enter your hire date and click Calculate.

steps.heading: Steps
steps.monthlyAccrual: Sub-1-year — 1 day per month worked: {totalMonths} months → {daysAccrued} days (cap {cap})
steps.annualIncrement: Increment — +1 day every {interval} years, {increments} applied → {total} days total
steps.wageDerive: Monthly wage {monthly} KRW ÷ 209 × 8 = daily wage {daily} KRW
steps.lowAttendance: Attendance < 80% — only 1 day per fully-attended month (LSA §60 ②)

warnings.heading: Notes
warnings.futureHireDate: Hire date is in the future. Treat the result as a simulation.
warnings.lowAttendance: Attendance < 80% accrues only 1 day per fully-attended month. Confirm with HR for precise numbers.
warnings.missingWage: Enter monthly or daily wage for compensation calculation.
warnings.negativeUnused: Unused days must be 0 or greater.

errors.unknown: An error occurred during calculation.

annualLeaveTool.source: Source: Labor Standards Act §60 (Annual Paid Leave). Daily ordinary wage = monthly ÷ 209 × 8 per administrative interpretation. For reference only — collective agreements and work rules at your workplace take precedence.

law.title: LSA §60
law.body: 15 days of paid leave per year for ≥ 80% attendance. Sub-1-year or < 80% gets 1 day per fully-attended month. From year 3, +1 day every 2 years; cap 25 days.

compensation.title: Unused-leave pay
compensation.body: Unused days are paid as daily ordinary wage × unused count. Daily wage = monthly ÷ 209 × 8 (base + fixed allowances).

exception.title: Workplaces under 5
exception.body: Workplaces with fewer than 5 regular employees are exempt from annual leave provisions (LSA §11). Collective agreements may override.

validation.hireAfterReference: Hire date cannot be later than reference date.
validation.unusedDaysNegative: Unused days must be 0 or greater.

steps.minSection: Step 2: Minimum cross-section by drop — A_min = ({coefficient} × {distance} × {current}) / (1000 × {drop}) = {result}mm²
steps.derating: Step 3: Derating factors — {insulation} insulation, ambient {ambient}°C → Ct={ct}, {circuits} circuits → Cg={cg} (total {total})
steps.selected: Step 5: KS standard cross-section — both drop (≥{minSection}mm²) and ampacity (base ≥{requiredAmpacity}A) satisfied → {chosen}mm² ({material} {insulation} table {baseAmpacity}A)

warnings.parallelRecommended: Cross-section {chosen}mm² is impractical as a single cable. Consider parallel circuits (2 or more in parallel).

errors.noStandardMatch: No KS standard cross-section satisfies both ampacity and voltage drop. Review your inputs (ambient/circuits/conductor) or consider parallel cabling.

steps.totalR: Step 3: Total R — layers {layers} + surface {surface} = {result} m²·K/W

brokerage-fee.overview: This calculator finds the legal maximum brokerage fee for Korean real estate transactions — the amount set by Annex 1 of the Enforcement Rules of the Licensed Real Estate Agents Act (revised and effective 19 October 2021). The 2021 revision replaced the old flat '0.9% above ₩900M' structure with a tiered system that significantly lowered rates on high-value properties. For sales, the cap ranges from 0.4% to 0.7% depending on the price bracket; for rentals (jeonse and monthly), it ranges from 0.3% to 0.6%. Low-bracket transactions also have hard fee ceilings (₩200K–₩800K). For monthly rent (wolse), the tool first converts to a 'transaction amount' using the statutory formula (deposit + rent × 100, or × 70 if that total is under ₩50M), then applies the rental rate table. The result is the legal maximum — the actual fee is negotiable.
useCases: Estimating the brokerage fee before signing an apartment purchase contract
Checking whether an agent's quoted fee is within the legal cap for a jeonse lease
Comparing how different deposit/monthly-rent combinations affect the brokerage cost
Understanding the legal baseline before negotiating the fee down
Calculating total out-of-pocket cost with or without VAT

howTo.title: How to use (3 steps)
steps: Select the transaction type: sale, jeonse, or monthly rent (wolse).
Enter the sale price, jeonse deposit, or (for monthly) the deposit and monthly rent. The monthly-rent conversion is automatic.
Toggle VAT if needed, then click Calculate. You'll see the applicable rate, any cap, and the final fee.

formula.title: Formula
formula.body:
Sale / jeonse: fee = min(transaction amount × max rate, cap)

Monthly rent conversion: amount = deposit + rent × 100
If that total < ₩50M: amount = deposit + rent × 70
→ Apply the rental rate table to the converted amount

VAT: fee × 10% (only if the agency is a general VAT taxpayer)

items.title: ₩500M apartment purchase
items.body:
₩500M falls in the sale bracket ₩200M–₩900M → max rate 0.4%, no cap.
₩500M × 0.4% = ₩2,000,000.
With VAT: ₩2,200,000.
Negotiating below ₩2M is common and perfectly legal.

items.title: ₩300M jeonse
items.body:
₩300M falls in the rental bracket ₩100M–₩600M → max rate 0.3%, no cap.
₩300M × 0.3% = ₩900,000.
With VAT: ₩990,000.

items.title: ₩10M deposit + ₩500K/month wolse
items.body:
Converted amount: ₩10M + ₩500K × 100 = ₩60M (≥ ₩50M → use × 100).
₩60M falls in the rental bracket ₩50M–₩100M → max rate 0.4%, cap ₩300K.
₩60M × 0.4% = ₩240K < ₩300K cap → cap doesn't apply. Fee: ₩240,000.

faq.question: Do both buyer and seller (or both landlord and tenant) pay the fee?
faq.answer: Yes. Each party pays their own agent separately. Even if one agent handles both sides (dual agency), each side pays independently.

faq.question: Can I negotiate below the legal maximum?
faq.answer: Yes. The statutory rate is a ceiling — paying above it is illegal, but agreeing to less is fine and common. Settle on a figure before signing and have it written into the contract.

faq.question: Is VAT always added on top?
faq.answer: No. VAT applies only if the agency is a 'general VAT taxpayer' (annual sales ≥ ₩80M). Agencies below that threshold (simplified taxpayers) cannot charge VAT. Confirm the agency's tax status before signing.

faq.question: How do I know whether to use × 70 or × 100 for monthly rent?
faq.answer: First compute deposit + rent × 100. If that figure is ₩50M or more, use it as-is (multiplier = 100). If it's below ₩50M, recompute as deposit + rent × 70. This rule is written into Annex 1 of the Enforcement Rules.

faq.question: Is the fee based on the actual contract price or the assessed value?
faq.answer: The actual contract price (the price written in the sale or lease agreement), not the assessed or market value. Enter the real transaction amount.

faq.question: Can local governments set different rates?
faq.answer: Yes. Cities and provinces may subdivide brackets above ₩900M or set lower rates by ordinance. This calculator reflects only the national legal maximum — check the local ordinance for your area before signing.

cautions: This tool shows the legal maximum fee. The actual brokerage fee is negotiable and may be lower.
Local (city/province) ordinances may subdivide brackets above ₩900M — check your area before signing.
VAT applicability depends on the agency's tax status (general vs. simplified taxpayer). Confirm before signing.
This is a reference tool, not legal or tax advice. Verify with a licensed agent or legal professional before completing any transaction.

gift-tax.overview: Korean gift tax (증여세) is paid by the recipient (donee) when receiving assets for free. Under the Inheritance and Gift Tax Act §53, a deduction is allowed based on the relationship between donor and donee — up to KRW 600M for spouses, KRW 50M for adult lineal descendants, KRW 20M for minor lineal descendants, and KRW 10M for other relatives — cumulated over a 10-year window. Progressive rates from 10% (up to KRW 100M) to 50% (over KRW 3B) apply under §56. Filing voluntarily within 3 months earns an additional 3% credit on the calculated tax. This calculator instantly outputs the deduction, tax base, calculated tax, self-report credit, and final tax payable. Note: property valuation, generation-skipping surcharge (30%), and penalty taxes are not included.
useCases: Estimate gift tax before parents transfer cash KRW 50M to an adult child
Calculate spousal gift tax after KRW 600M deduction on property transfer
Simulate additional gift tax when prior gifts exist within the 10-year window
Check minor child stock gift tax using the KRW 20M deduction limit
Compare payable tax with and without the 3% self-report credit

howTo.title: How to use (3 steps)
steps: Enter the gift amount — cash value, appraised property value, or stock market value.
Select the donor relationship. The deduction is applied automatically based on the relationship chosen.
Enter any prior gifts received from the same donor within the past 10 years (0 if none), check the self-report box, and the payable tax appears instantly.

formula.title: Formula
formula.body:
Tax Base = Gift Amount − Relationship Deduction − Prior 10-year Gifts

Calculated Tax = Tax Base × Rate − Progressive Deduction

[Rate table]
  Up to KRW 100M : 10% (deduction: 0)
  Up to KRW 500M : 20% (deduction: 10M)
  Up to KRW 1B   : 30% (deduction: 60M)
  Up to KRW 3B   : 40% (deduction: 160M)
  Over KRW 3B    : 50% (deduction: 460M)

Final Tax = Self-report → Calculated Tax × 0.97
           No report  → Calculated Tax (no credit)

items.title: Example 1: Parent gifts KRW 100M to adult child
items.body: Gift amount KRW 100M, relationship 'Ancestor → adult descendant'. Deduction: KRW 50M. Tax base: KRW 50M. Calculated tax: 50M × 10% = KRW 5M. With self-report credit: 5M × 0.97 = KRW 4.85M payable.

items.title: Example 2: Spouse gifts KRW 1B
items.body: Gift amount KRW 1B, relationship 'Spouse'. Deduction: KRW 600M. Tax base: KRW 400M. Calculated tax: 400M × 20% − 10M = KRW 70M. With self-report credit: 70M × 0.97 = KRW 67.9M payable.

items.title: Example 3: Additional KRW 30M gift after prior KRW 30M from same parent
items.body: The adult child already received KRW 30M from the same parent 5 years ago. Remaining deduction: 50M − 30M = KRW 20M. Tax base: 30M − 20M = KRW 10M. Calculated tax: 10M × 10% = KRW 1M. With self-report: KRW 970,000 payable.

faq.question: Who pays Korean gift tax — the giver or the receiver?
faq.answer: The recipient (donee) pays. If the donor pays on behalf of the recipient, that payment itself is treated as an additional gift and taxed accordingly.

faq.question: What does '10-year aggregation' mean?
faq.answer: Gifts from the same donor are aggregated over a 10-year window when applying the deduction limit. For example, if you received KRW 30M from a parent 5 years ago, only KRW 20M of the KRW 50M adult-descendant deduction remains for a new gift.

faq.question: When is the filing deadline?
faq.answer: You must file and pay within 3 months from the last day of the month in which the gift occurred. Filing on time earns the 3% self-report credit. Late filing incurs a 20% non-filing penalty plus daily interest.

faq.question: How is property valued for gift tax purposes?
faq.answer: The market value (recent comparable transactions) is used as the primary valuation. When market value is unavailable, supplemental methods such as the published standard price (기준시가) are used. Enter your best estimate into the calculator.

faq.question: What is the generation-skipping surcharge?
faq.answer: If grandparents skip the parent generation and gift directly to grandchildren, a 30% surcharge is added to the calculated tax. This calculator does not include that surcharge — consult a tax accountant if this applies.

cautions: This calculator is for reference only and has no legal effect. Actual tax payable may differ.
Property valuation, generation-skipping surcharge (30%), and penalty taxes are not reflected.
If prior 10-year gifts already exceed the deduction limit, no further deduction is available.
Missing the 3-month filing deadline forfeits the 3% self-report credit and triggers penalty taxes.

prepayment-penalty.overview: The prepayment penalty calculator uses the sliding-scale formula from Korean bank standard lending terms (여신거래기본약관) to automatically compute the fee charged when you repay a loan in full or in part before its maturity date. The sliding scale means the longer you have held the loan, the lower the penalty: for a typical 1.2% rate with a 3-year exemption period, repaying immediately after origination incurs the full 1.2%, while repaying after 1 year costs about 0.8%, after 2 years about 0.4%, and after 3 years the fee is waived entirely. Enter the prepayment principal, penalty rate, elapsed months, and exemption period to instantly see the penalty amount, remaining days, and exemption status. This tool is ideal for anyone considering refinancing (대환대출) or planning an early repayment after receiving a lump sum.
useCases: Check the prepayment penalty before refinancing (switching banks or loan products)
Decide the optimal timing for early repayment when a bonus or property sale proceeds arrive
See how many days remain until the penalty exemption kicks in (usually 3 years)
Compare the actual penalty amount across different bank penalty rates
Plan renewal timelines for jeonse loans, mortgage loans, or personal credit loans

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Enter the prepayment principal — the full remaining balance for full repayment, or the partial amount you plan to repay.
Enter the penalty rate (%). Check your loan agreement or bank app; typical range is 0.5–2%.
Enter the elapsed period in months — the number of months since the loan origination date.
Enter the exemption period in years. Most Korean banks use 3 years; check your agreement.
Read the result: penalty amount, remaining days until exemption, and effective rate.

formula.title: Sliding-scale formula
formula.body:
Penalty = principal × rate × (remaining days / exemption period days)

Remaining days = max(0, exemption days − elapsed days)
Exemption period days = exemption years × 365
Elapsed days = elapsed months × 30.4  (simplified: 1 month = 30.4 days)

Example: ₩100M principal, 1.2% rate, 12 months elapsed, 3-year exemption
→ Elapsed days = 12 × 30.4 = 364.8
→ Remaining days = 1,095 − 364.8 = 730.2
→ Penalty = 100,000,000 × 0.012 × (730.2 / 1,095) ≈ ₩800,219

items.title: Immediate repayment (0 months elapsed)
items.body: Principal ₩100M, rate 1.2%, 3-year exemption. Remaining days = 1,095. Penalty = ₩1,200,000 (full rate). This is the worst-case scenario for switching loans — waiting until the exemption period reduces the cost significantly.

items.title: Repayment after 1 year (12 months elapsed)
items.body: Principal ₩100M, rate 1.2%, 3-year exemption. Elapsed days = 364.8, remaining days = 730.2. Penalty ≈ ₩800,219 — about 33% less than immediate repayment. The sliding scale reduces the burden as time passes.

items.title: Repayment after 3+ years — fully exempt
items.body: Principal ₩100M, rate 1.2%, 37+ months elapsed, 3-year exemption. Remaining days = 0. Penalty = ₩0. Under standard Korean bank lending terms, once the exemption period (usually 3 years) has passed, the prepayment penalty is fully waived.

faq.question: What is a loan prepayment penalty?
faq.answer: A prepayment penalty (중도상환수수료) is a fee charged by the bank when you repay a loan before its maturity date. Because the bank loses expected interest income, it compensates by charging a percentage of the repaid amount. The fee typically slides down to zero over 3 years.

faq.question: Where can I find my penalty rate?
faq.answer: Check your loan agreement (약정서), the loan details screen in your bank's app, or call the bank's customer center. Rates typically range from 0.5% to 2%. Internet-only banks like Kakao Bank or Toss Bank often charge no prepayment penalty.

faq.question: Is the penalty really zero after 3 years?
faq.answer: Under the standard Korean bank lending terms (여신거래기본약관), the prepayment penalty is waived once 3 years (1,095 days) have elapsed since origination. However, some products may use different exemption periods — always verify in your specific loan agreement.

faq.question: Why might the calculator differ from my bank's actual fee?
faq.answer: This calculator simplifies elapsed days as months × 30.4. Your bank calculates exact calendar days from origination to repayment. Some products may also use different methods (e.g., deducting from the remaining principal). Always confirm the exact fee with your bank before repaying.

faq.question: How do I compare the penalty against the savings from refinancing?
faq.answer: Compare the prepayment penalty against the annual interest savings from the new lower rate. For example, if the penalty is ₩800,000 and the new loan saves ₩1,200,000 per year in interest, you break even in less than a year. Use the Loan Calculator to compare total interest under both loans.

faq.question: How is a partial prepayment penalty calculated?
faq.answer: The penalty applies only to the amount you actually repay early. Enter the partial repayment amount (not the full loan balance) in the principal field to calculate the penalty for that specific amount.

cautions: This calculator uses a simplified model (elapsed days = months × 30.4) and may differ from your bank's exact calculation based on calendar days.
Internet-only banks (Kakao Bank, Toss Bank, etc.) may charge no prepayment penalty — check your agreement.
Fixed-rate and variable-rate loans may have different penalty rates; promotional products may have special conditions.
Always confirm the exact fee with your bank before making an early repayment decision.

unemployment-benefit.overview: The Korean Unemployment Benefit (Guikgubup) Calculator estimates your expected payout based on Employment Insurance Act Articles 46 and 50. Enter your average daily wage before separation, employment insurance subscription period, and age (50+ or disability status) to instantly see the daily benefit amount, benefit days, and total estimated payout. The 2019 revision expanded benefit days across all brackets. For separations on or after 2026-01-01, the daily cap is 68,100 KRW (first increase in seven years) and the floor is 66,048 KRW (2026 minimum wage 10,320 × 80% × 8 hours); 2025 separations use 66,000 / 64,192 KRW. The calculator applies both limits automatically and reflects the preferential schedule for workers aged 50+ or with disabilities. Note: actual eligibility and final amounts are determined by your local Employment Center (Goyong Center) — separation reason, job-seeking activity, and other conditions must also be met.
useCases: Estimate unemployment benefit before resigning to plan living expenses during job search
Compare how benefit days change across different insurance subscription periods
Verify that the minimum wage floor applies when your average wage is low
Convert monthly salary to daily average wage automatically using the monthly input mode
Understand the extra benefit days available for workers aged 50+ or with disabilities

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Select the input mode: 'Daily average wage' (total wages over last 3 months ÷ total calendar days), or 'Monthly average salary' (auto-divided by 30).
Enter your average wage. Include base pay, allowances, and the pro-rated portion of any bonuses earned in the 3-month period.
Enter your employment insurance subscription period in years. For 2 years 6 months, enter 2.5.
Check the box if you are 50 years or older, or have a registered disability — this unlocks the preferential benefit-days column.
Read the daily benefit, benefit days, and total estimated payout in the result panel.

formula.title: Calculation formula (Employment Insurance Act Art. 46·50)
formula.body:
Daily benefit (raw) = Daily average wage × 60%

Daily cap = 68,100 KRW (separation on/after 2026-01-01; 66,000 KRW for 2025 separations)
Daily floor = Min. hourly wage (10,320 KRW) × 0.8 × work hours (8h) = 66,048 KRW (2026)

Daily benefit = clamp(raw, floor, cap)
             = min(68,100, max(66,048, raw))

Total payout = Daily benefit × Benefit days

Benefit days table (revised 2019-10-01):
  < 1 year           → Under 50: 120 days / 50+: 120 days
  1 year – < 3 years → 150 days / 180 days
  3 years – < 5 years → 180 days / 210 days
  5 years – < 10 years → 210 days / 240 days
  10+ years           → 240 days / 270 days

items.title: Example 1 — Regular worker (3 years insured, under 50)
items.body: Daily average wage 120,000 KRW × 60% = 72,000 KRW → exceeds cap (68,100) → daily benefit 68,100 KRW. Benefit days: 180. Total = 68,100 × 180 = 12,258,000 KRW (approx. 12.3M KRW).

items.title: Example 2 — Low-wage worker (2 years insured, under 50)
items.body: Monthly salary 2,400,000 KRW → daily avg 80,000 KRW. 80,000 × 60% = 48,000 KRW → below floor (66,048 KRW) → daily benefit 66,048 KRW. Benefit days: 150. Total = 66,048 × 150 = 9,907,200 KRW (approx. 9.9M KRW).

items.title: Example 3 — Long-tenure senior worker (12 years insured, age 55)
items.body: Daily avg 113,500 KRW × 60% = 68,100 KRW → equals cap. 50+, 10+ years → 270 benefit days. Total = 68,100 × 270 = 18,387,000 KRW (approx. 18.4M KRW).

faq.question: What are the eligibility requirements for unemployment benefit?
faq.answer: ① At least 180 insured days in the 18 months before separation, ② involuntary separation (layoff, contract expiry, etc. — voluntary resignation is generally excluded), ③ willing and able to work but unemployed, ④ actively seeking re-employment. This calculator estimates amounts only; it does not determine eligibility.

faq.question: How is the average daily wage calculated?
faq.answer: Total wages in the 3 months before separation (base pay + allowances + pro-rated bonuses) divided by the total calendar days in that period (approx. 90–92 days). The Employment Center uses payroll records or withholding tax certificates for the official calculation.

faq.question: Can I receive benefits if I resign voluntarily?
faq.answer: Generally no, but exceptions exist for valid reasons: unpaid wages, workplace harassment, impossible commute due to company relocation, or serious health deterioration. You must apply at the local Employment Center and have your reason assessed.

faq.question: What happens if I find a job before the benefit days run out?
faq.answer: You may qualify for the Early Re-employment Allowance (Jogi Jaechwieop Sudang) if you re-employ with significant benefit days remaining. The earlier you find work, the larger the one-time bonus, which incentivizes active job-seeking.

faq.question: Why are the cap (68,100) and floor (66,048) so close together?
faq.answer: The 2019 amendment raised the floor to 80% of the minimum wage, significantly narrowing the range. In 2026 the floor (66,048) would have overtaken the old cap (66,000), so the cap was raised to 68,100 — the first increase in seven years. Most recipients effectively receive either the cap or the floor; only daily wages between roughly 110,080–113,500 KRW fall in between.

faq.question: Does a longer insurance period always mean more benefit days?
faq.answer: Yes. The benefit-days table has five brackets from under 1 year (120 days) up to 10+ years (240 days, or 270 for age 50+). Workers aged 50 or older or with disabilities get an additional 30 days in every bracket compared to the under-50 column.

cautions: This calculator provides estimates only. Actual eligibility, amounts, and benefit days are determined by your local Korean Employment Center (Goyong Center).
Voluntary resignation is generally ineligible. Job-seeking activity, waiting period, and other conditions must be met separately.
The daily cap (68,100 KRW) and floor (66,048 KRW) apply to separations on or after 2026-01-01; 2025 separations use 66,000 / 64,192 KRW. The applicable year is determined by your separation date.
The monthly-to-daily conversion uses ÷30 for simplicity; the official calculation uses the exact calendar days in the 3-month period.

compound-calc.overview: The compound interest calculator covers two modes in one screen: lump-sum (a single principal compounding over time) and recurring (monthly deposits with compounding). The lump-sum mode uses FV = P(1+i)^n; the recurring mode offers annual compounding — matching the Korean bank savings-account convention where each month's deposit earns simple interest proportional to the remaining months, then all interest is capitalized at year-end — or monthly compounding. A period-by-period and year-by-year schedule shows the interest earned, running balance, and cumulative return at every step. Results are pre-tax nominal figures; withholding tax (15.4%) and inflation are not applied.
useCases: Estimate the maturity value of a time deposit or savings account before signing up
Project how much a monthly contribution to a pension savings plan or installment fund will grow over n years
Back-calculate how long it will take (or how much to save per month) to reach a target amount
Compare how much extra monthly vs. annual compounding earns for the same rate and period
Visualize the compounding acceleration effect in the late years using the year-by-year schedule

howTo.title: How to use (3 steps)
steps: Select the tab: 'Lump-sum' or 'Recurring'. Lump-sum needs principal, rate per period, and number of periods. Recurring needs starting amount, monthly contribution, duration (years or months), rate (annual or monthly), and compounding frequency (annual or monthly).
Click 'Calculate' to instantly see the final amount, total invested, total interest, and overall return rate in the summary cards.
Switch between the 'Per-period' and 'Yearly' sub-tabs in the schedule below to trace interest, principal, and balance at every interval.

formula.title: Formula & basis
formula.body:
[Lump-sum]
FV = P × (1 + i)^n
  P = principal
  i = rate per period (% ÷ 100)
  n = number of periods

With periodic contribution (PMT each period-end):
FV = P×(1+i)^n + PMT × [(1+i)^n − 1] / i

[Recurring — annual compounding (Korean savings-account convention)]
Each monthly deposit earns simple interest of annual rate × (months remaining ÷ 12) within the year; the full balance is capitalized at year-end.

[Recurring — monthly compounding]
Balance is multiplied by (1 + monthly rate) every month, where monthly rate = annual rate ÷ 12.

items.title: Example 1: ₩10,000,000 time deposit at 3.5% p.a. for 3 years
items.body: Lump-sum mode: principal ₩10,000,000, rate per period 3.5%, 3 periods (annual). Final amount = 10,000,000 × (1.035)^3 ≈ ₩10,877,289. Total interest ≈ ₩877,289 (8.77%). After Korean withholding tax of 15.4%, net interest is approximately ₩742,000.

items.title: Example 2: ₩500,000/month recurring, 4% p.a. annual compounding, 10 years
items.body: Recurring mode: starting ₩500,000, monthly ₩500,000, 10 years, 4% annual rate, annual compounding. Total invested ₩60,000,000; maturity ≈ ₩73,590,000; total interest ≈ ₩13,590,000 (22.6%). The yearly schedule shows that the 10th year alone generates ~₩2,700,000 in interest — the compounding acceleration effect in action.

items.title: Example 3: Annual vs. monthly compounding on the same principal
items.body: ₩10,000,000 at 5% p.a. for 5 years: annual compounding → ₩12,762,816; monthly compounding → ₩12,833,587. Difference: ₩70,771. The gap widens with higher rates and longer durations, so it is worth checking the compounding frequency stated in a product's terms.

faq.question: Is monthly compounding always better than annual?
faq.answer: Yes, for the same stated annual rate. Monthly compounding capitalizes interest more frequently, so the effective annual rate (EAR) is higher. At 12% p.a., annual compounding gives EAR 12%, while monthly compounding gives EAR ≈ 12.68%. However, some products already advertise an effective monthly-compounded rate, so always read the product terms.

faq.question: What is the 'Korean savings-account convention' for annual compounding?
faq.answer: Korean banks calculate interest on installment savings by crediting each monthly deposit with simple interest proportional to the number of months remaining in the year. The first month's deposit earns 12 months of interest; the last month's deposit earns 1 month. All accumulated interest is then capitalized at year-end (or maturity). The recurring annual-compounding mode in this calculator replicates that method.

faq.question: Is withholding tax (15.4%) automatically deducted?
faq.answer: No. All results are pre-tax nominal amounts. In Korea, interest income is subject to 15.4% withholding (14% income tax + 1.4% local tax). To estimate after-tax interest, multiply the displayed total interest by 0.846.

faq.question: How should I set 'rate per period' and 'periods' in lump-sum mode?
faq.answer: Match the period unit to the compounding frequency. For monthly compounding at 6% p.a. over 2 years: rate per period = 0.5% (6% ÷ 12), periods = 24. For annual compounding over 5 years at 5%: rate per period = 5%, periods = 5.

faq.question: Is there a limit to the number of periods?
faq.answer: Lump-sum mode accepts up to 2,400 periods (200 years at monthly compounding). The per-period schedule table displays up to 600 rows; a notice appears when it is truncated. Recurring mode supports up to 1,200 months (100 years).

faq.question: Why might the result differ from my bank's quoted figure?
faq.answer: This tool does not include withholding tax, transaction fees, inflation, or exchange-rate effects. For market products like funds or ETFs, returns are not fixed. Even for fixed-rate deposits, rounding rules (truncation vs. rounding at each step) can cause a difference of a few won versus the bank's own calculation.

cautions: Pre-tax nominal calculation only — Korean withholding tax of 15.4% is not deducted. Estimate after-tax interest as total interest × 0.846.
Inflation is not factored in. Nominal return and real return differ.
In lump-sum mode, periodic contributions are assumed to be deposited at the end of each period. Products with beginning-of-period deposits will show slightly higher results.
The recurring annual-compounding model follows Korean bank savings-account convention. Minor differences may arise due to each institution's own rounding policy.
Results are for reference only. Confirm actual product terms with your financial institution or the FSS financial product comparison portal before subscribing.

vat-calc.overview: The Korean VAT calculator covers four calculation modes based on Value-Added Tax Act Article 30 (rate: 10%). 'Add VAT' takes a supply value (tax-exclusive) and adds 10% to produce the total price; 'Extract VAT' reverse-calculates the supply value and VAT from a VAT-inclusive amount. 'General taxpayer return' subtracts input tax from output tax to arrive at the net amount payable. 'Simplified taxpayer' applies the industry-specific value-added rates in Enforcement Decree §111 (15% for retail and restaurants up to 40% for finance and other services) to compute the simplified payable amount. All amounts are truncated to the nearest won, matching the NTS (Hometax) filing standard.
useCases: Freelancers / business owners: verify supply value and VAT before issuing a tax invoice
Consumers: back-calculate how much VAT is embedded in a receipt total
General taxpayers: pre-estimate output tax minus input tax before a quarterly VAT return
Simplified taxpayers: simulate the annual payable amount using the applicable industry value-added rate
Exporters / importers: confirm whether a transaction is zero-rated or exempt and separate the taxable amount

howTo.title: How to use (3 steps)
steps: Choose the calculation type: 'Add VAT' (enter the tax-exclusive price), 'Extract VAT' (enter the VAT-inclusive total), 'General taxpayer return' (enter sales total and optional purchase amount), or 'Simplified taxpayer' (enter the sales total and select your industry).
Enter the amount in Korean won. For the general taxpayer mode, entering the optional 'purchase supply value' will automatically compute the deductible input tax.
Click 'Calculate' to see the supply value, VAT, and total (or net payable amount) along with the step-by-step calculation breakdown.

formula.title: Formula & legal basis
formula.body:
[Add VAT (add mode)]
VAT = supply value × 10%  (truncated to nearest won)
Total price = supply value + VAT

[Extract VAT (extract mode)]
Supply value = total price × 10 ÷ 11  (rounded to nearest won)
VAT = total price − supply value

[General taxpayer payable (general mode)]
Output tax = sales total × 10 ÷ 11
Input tax = purchase supply value × 10%
Net payable = output tax − input tax

[Simplified taxpayer payable (simple mode) — VAT Act §63, Enforcement Decree §111]
Value added = sales total × industry value-added rate
Payable = value added × 10%

Industry value-added rates (effective 2021-07-01):
  Retail, restaurants                15%
  Manufacturing, agriculture, courier 20%
  Lodging                            25%
  Construction, transport, IT        30%
  Finance, real-estate rental, other services 40%

※ Legal basis: VAT Act Article 30 (rate 10%)

items.title: Example 1: Freelance service fee of ₩1,100,000 — splitting supply value and VAT
items.body: Extract mode, amount ₩1,100,000. Supply value = 1,100,000 × 10 ÷ 11 = ₩1,000,000; VAT = ₩100,000. The tax invoice should read: supply value ₩1,000,000 / tax ₩100,000 / total ₩1,100,000.

items.title: Example 2: General taxpayer quarterly return — sales ₩22,000,000, purchases ₩5,500,000
Example 3: Restaurant simplified taxpayer, annual sales ₩40,000,000
items.body: Simplified mode, industry 'retail / restaurants (15%)'. Value added = 40,000,000 × 15% = ₩6,000,000. Payable = 6,000,000 × 10% = ₩600,000. Under the general taxpayer regime, output tax would have been roughly ₩3,636,364 (before input tax deduction), illustrating the substantial benefit of simplified taxpayer status.

faq.question: Why does the formula use '×10÷11' instead of '÷1.1' to extract the supply value?
faq.answer: The two are mathematically equivalent, but multiplying by the integer ratio 10/11 avoids floating-point errors. For example, dividing 11,000 by 1.1 can return 9,999.999...998 in some environments instead of the exact 10,000. Using integer numerator and denominator eliminates that rounding artefact before the final truncation step.

faq.question: What is the difference between VAT-exempt and zero-rated?
faq.answer: VAT-exempt transactions (medical, education, unprocessed agricultural products, etc.) charge no VAT and cannot reclaim input tax. Zero-rated transactions (exports, etc.) also charge 0% VAT on sales but are fully entitled to input tax refunds. This calculator only handles standard 10% taxable transactions. Do not use it for exempt or zero-rated supplies.

faq.question: When are Korean VAT returns due?
faq.answer: General taxpayers file twice a year: the first half (Jan–Jun) by 25 July, and the second half (Jul–Dec) by 25 January of the following year. Preliminary returns are filed in April and October but are often payment-only. Simplified taxpayers file once a year by 25 January.

faq.question: What is the sales threshold for simplified taxpayer status?
faq.answer: Since the July 2021 revision, a business with prior-year total supply value below ₩80,000,000 qualifies as a simplified taxpayer. Above that, the business must switch to general taxpayer status from the following year. Real-estate rental and entertainment venues have a lower threshold of ₩48,000,000.

faq.question: Who is required to issue electronic tax invoices?
faq.answer: General taxpayers must issue a (electronic) tax invoice for every taxable supply. Simplified taxpayers with annual supply value of ₩48,000,000 or more must also issue electronic tax invoices. Below ₩48,000,000, a simplified receipt may substitute.

faq.question: Is the VAT return the same as the income tax return?
faq.answer: No. VAT is an indirect tax collected on sales and remitted to the tax authority. Income tax (comprehensive income tax) is a direct tax levied on net profit and is filed every May. Freelancers and business owners must file both separately.

cautions: This calculator handles standard-rated (10%) transactions only. Do not use it for VAT-exempt (medical, education, etc.) or zero-rated (export, etc.) supplies.
All amounts are truncated to the nearest won following the NTS Hometax filing standard. Minor differences may arise from rounding policies used by card companies or individual businesses.
The simplified taxpayer industry value-added rates are based on VAT Enforcement Decree §111 as of 2021-07-01. They are subject to change upon legislative revision.
Results are for reference only. Confirm your actual tax liability via NTS Hometax or a licensed tax accountant.
This tool does not automatically exclude non-deductible input tax items (e.g., entertainment expenses, non-business purchases). Assess deductibility separately before entering your purchase amount.

json-csv.overview: The JSON↔CSV converter is a browser-based utility that transforms JSON arrays into CSV files and CSV files back into JSON arrays, with no data ever sent to a server. In the JSON→CSV direction it defaults to UTF-8 BOM and Windows CRLF line endings — the two settings that prevent Korean (Hangul) characters from appearing as garbage in Korean Windows Excel. In the CSV→JSON direction it auto-detects the delimiter (comma, semicolon, or tab), correctly parses quoted cells that contain commas or embedded newlines per RFC 4180, and optionally converts numeric-looking strings to the number type. Results can be copied to the clipboard or downloaded as a file.
useCases: Convert an API JSON response to a CSV that can be opened directly in Excel or Google Sheets
Convert an Excel-exported CSV to JSON for a database INSERT or REST API request body
Fix Korean character corruption in Excel by enabling BOM + CRLF when exporting CSV
Quickly reformat test data during development or QA without installing any software
Normalize JSON or verify CSV column schema before uploading to Pandas, BigQuery, or similar tools

howTo.title: How to use (3 steps)
steps: Click the direction toggle to select 'JSON → CSV' or 'CSV → JSON'. Sample data switches automatically when you toggle.
Paste or edit the text in the input area. For JSON, the top-level must be an array of objects. For CSV, turn on the 'Header row' option if the first row contains column names.
Review the options (BOM, CRLF, header, number parsing), then click 'Convert'. The output appears on the right — copy it or download as a file. Row and column counts are shown as a summary.

formula.title: Conversion rules & technical basis
formula.body:
[JSON → CSV]
- Column order: keys of the first object in the array
- Cell escaping: cells containing a comma, newline, or double-quote are wrapped in double-quotes; internal double-quotes are escaped as "" (RFC 4180)
- BOM: UTF-8 BOM (EF BB BF) — prevents Korean character corruption in Korean-locale Windows Excel
- Line ending: CRLF (\r\n) for Windows Excel compatibility

[CSV → JSON]
- Delimiter auto-detection: the character (comma / semicolon / tab) that appears most often in the first row
- Quoted-cell parsing: everything between an opening and closing double-quote is treated as one field, including embedded newlines
- Number conversion: values matching /^-?\d+(\.\d+)?$/ are converted to the number type when the option is enabled
- BOM stripping: a leading UTF-8 BOM in the input is removed automatically
- Empty-line skipping: blank rows are ignored during parsing

items.title: Example 1: Convert an API JSON response to an Excel-ready CSV
items.body: Paste [{"name":"홍길동","age":30,"city":"서울"}, ...] into the input, select JSON → CSV, enable BOM and CRLF, then click 'Convert'. Downloading the result opens in Korean Windows Excel with all Hangul characters displayed correctly. Column order follows the key order of the first object.

items.title: Example 2: CSV with commas inside a cell — address data
items.body: If an address cell contains '서울특별시, 강남구' (a comma inside the value), Excel wraps it in double-quotes when exporting. This tool parses the quoted field as a single value per RFC 4180, so the JSON output will contain address: '서울특별시, 강남구' intact — the comma does not split the field.

items.title: Example 3: Number auto-conversion option
items.body: With 'Parse numbers' on, a CSV column value '30' becomes the JSON number 30 instead of the string '30'. This is convenient for downstream code that expects numeric types. Turn the option off if you need leading zeros preserved (e.g., postal codes like '01234' or phone numbers like '0101234567').

faq.question: Why does Korean text look garbled when opening a CSV in Excel?
faq.answer: Windows Excel interprets a UTF-8 file without a BOM using the system locale, which on Korean Windows is CP949 (EUC-KR). UTF-8-encoded Hangul read as EUC-KR appears as garbage characters. Enabling the 'UTF-8 BOM' option prepends the three bytes EF BB BF to the file, signalling to Excel that the encoding is UTF-8 and causing Hangul to render correctly.

faq.question: What JSON structure is supported?
faq.answer: JSON→CSV conversion requires the top-level value to be an array of objects: [ {...}, {...} ]. A single object { ... }, an array of primitives, or a nested array are not supported and will produce an error. Nested objects within a row (object-in-object) are serialized via JSON.stringify and placed in a single CSV cell.

faq.question: Can it handle semicolon-delimited CSV files?
faq.answer: Yes. In the CSV→JSON direction, the delimiter is detected automatically from the first row: whichever of comma, semicolon, or tab appears most often is used. European CSV files commonly use semicolons, and the auto-detection handles them correctly. If auto-detection picks the wrong delimiter, inspect the first row for ambiguity.

faq.question: What happens if a row has a different number of columns than the header?
faq.answer: A warning is shown for each mismatched row (row.N.colMismatch). Parsing continues: if the row has fewer cells than headers, missing values are filled with an empty string; extra cells beyond the header count are discarded. Conversion is not aborted.

faq.question: Is my data sent to any server?
faq.answer: No. All conversion logic runs entirely in your browser using JavaScript. Neither the input nor the output is transmitted over the network. You can safely use this tool with personal data or confidential business data.

faq.question: Can I choose the download file name?
faq.answer: The download button saves the file as 'worktool-[timestamp].csv' (or .json). You can rename the file in your file explorer after downloading. Some browsers show a 'Save as' dialog where you can change the name and location before saving.

cautions: JSON input must be an array of objects ([ {...}, {...} ]). A single object or a primitive array will cause an error.
Nested objects or arrays within a row are serialized as a JSON string into a single CSV cell. Flatten the structure first if you need them as separate columns.
EUC-KR (CP949) encoded CSV files are not directly supported. Convert the file to UTF-8 first using Notepad, VS Code, or similar tools before pasting here.
Very large files (tens of thousands of rows or more) may be slow or may crash the browser tab due to memory limits.

With 'Parse numbers' enabled, values with a leading zero — such as postal codes ('01234') or phone numbers ('0101234567') — will lose the leading zero when converted to a number. Disable the option to keep them as strings.

insurance-calc.overview: The 4 Major Insurances Calculator applies Korea's confirmed 2026 rates to split a single monthly salary into employee and employer shares across all five mandatory programs: National Pension, Health Insurance, Long-Term Care, Employment Insurance, and Workers' Compensation. From January 2026 the National Pension rate rose from 9% to 9.5% (4.75% each side), and Health Insurance rose from 7.09% to 7.19% (3.595% each). It automatically checks the pension income cap (6.37 million KRW) and shows both your net take-home pay and the employer's true labor cost. The Workers' Comp rate varies by industry, so you can adjust it yourself.
useCases: Employees: quickly verify that this month's payslip deductions are correct
HR/payroll staff: simulate a new hire's true labor cost (salary + employer-side insurance) before setting compensation
Freelancer-to-employee transition: compare offers by net pay after the 4 insurance deductions
Small business owners: budget total labor cost before deciding staff salaries
Gauge the impact of the 2026 National Pension and Health Insurance rate hikes

howTo.title: How to use (3 steps)
steps: Enter your monthly salary (standard remuneration) in KRW. Use pre-tax base pay plus fixed allowances. Irregular items such as bonuses or annual-leave pay may differ from actual deductions depending on whether they are included.

Check the Workers' Comp rate. The default is the all-industry average of 0.86%; for high-risk sectors such as construction or manufacturing, switch to the rate published by COMWEL (e.g. ~3.6% for construction) for accuracy.

Click Calculate to see a per-insurance table of employee and employer shares, your net take-home pay (salary − total employee share), and the employer's total labor cost (salary + total employer share).

formula.title: Formula based on 2026 rates
formula.body:
National Pension: standard income × 9.5% (employee 4.75% / employer 4.75%)
  - Income cap 6,590,000 KRW / floor 410,000 KRW (Jul 2026 – Jun 2027)
  - 2026.1 pension reform hike: 9% → 9.5%

Health Insurance: salary × 7.19% (employee 3.595% / employer 3.595%)
  - 2026 hike: 7.09% (frozen 2024–2025) → 7.19%

Long-Term Care: health premium (employee + employer) × 13.14%, split in half
  - 2026 hike: 12.95% → 13.14%

Employment Insurance (unemployment): salary × 1.8% (employee 0.9% / employer 0.9%)
Employment Insurance (job stability/skills): employer only, 0.25% for firms under 150 staff

Workers' Comp: salary × industry rate (employer pays 100%)
  - Average 0.86%, ranging 0.6%–38% by industry (COMWEL)

[Rounded down to the nearest 10 KRW — same as the agencies' actual billing]

items.title: Example 1: Employee earning 3M KRW/month (2026 rates)
items.body:
National Pension (employee): 3,000,000 × 4.75% = 142,500 KRW
Health (employee): 3,000,000 × 3.595% = 107,850 KRW
Long-Term Care: (107,850×2) × 13.14% ÷ 2 ≈ 14,140 KRW
Employment: 3,000,000 × 0.9% = 27,000 KRW
→ Total employee deduction ≈ 291,490 KRW / net pay ≈ 2,708,510 KRW
→ Employer adds ≈ 351,080 KRW / total labor cost ≈ 3,351,080 KRW

items.title: Example 2: 7M KRW/month — above the pension cap
items.body: The National Pension income cap of 6,590,000 KRW (from Jul 2026) applies. Instead of 7M × 4.75%, it is 6.59M × 4.75% = 313,025 KRW (313,020 after rounding down to 10 KRW). Health Insurance, however, applies to the full 7M salary (3.595% = 251,650 KRW). A cap warning is shown automatically.

items.title: Example 3: Small employer — annual cost for a 36M KRW salary
items.body: At 3M/month the employer share is ~351,080 KRW × 12 = ~4,212,960 KRW per year. True annual labor cost = 36M + ~4.21M = ~40.21M KRW. The 2026 pension and health hikes raised this versus the prior year.

faq.question: Why was more National Pension deducted in 2026?
faq.answer: A 2025 amendment to the National Pension Act raised the rate from 9% to 9.5% (a 0.5 point increase) effective January 2026. It then rises 0.5 points each year to 13% by 2033. At a 3M KRW salary the employee's annual share rises by about 18,000 KRW.

faq.question: How is the Long-Term Care premium calculated?
faq.answer: It is the health premium (employee + employer combined) multiplied by 13.14%, then split in half. In practice, the Long-Term Care line on your payslip ≈ your health premium (employee share) × 13.14%. It rose from 12.95% to 13.14% in 2026.

faq.question: When does the National Pension income cap change?
faq.answer: The National Pension Service adjusts it every July based on the change in the average insured income. The current cap is 6,590,000 KRW for July 2026 – June 2027 (up from 6,370,000) and is reset again in July 2027. High earners above the cap pay a fixed maximum premium.

faq.question: Does the job-stability/skills contribution vary by company size?
faq.answer: Yes. It is 0.25% for firms under 150 staff, 0.45% for priority-support firms of 150–under 1,000, 0.65% for other 150–1,000 firms, and 0.85% for firms of 1,000+ and government bodies. This tool defaults to 0.25% (under 150 staff).

faq.question: What industry does the 0.86% Workers' Comp average represent?
faq.answer: It is the all-industry average published yearly by COMWEL. Actual rates differ widely — roughly 0.6% for finance/insurance, 1.0–2.0% for manufacturing, and ~3.6% for construction. Change it to your own industry rate for accuracy.

faq.question: Can 3.3%-withholding freelancers use this calculator?
faq.answer: No — freelancers under 3.3% withholding (business-income earners) are not mandatorily enrolled in the four insurances, so this tool does not fit. Only 3% income tax + 0.3% local tax is withheld; the four insurances may instead require separate enrollment as a regional subscriber.

cautions: This tool uses confirmed 2026 rates; the National Pension income cap is 6.59M KRW (Jul 2026 – Jun 2027, adjusted every July).
Amounts are rounded down to the nearest 10 KRW, so they may differ from agency bills by under 1 KRW.
Whether irregular pay (bonuses, leave pay) counts toward the insurances depends on the employment contract and work rules; this tool treats the full input as standard remuneration.
The Employment Insurance job-stability/skills rate varies by firm size. The default (0.25%) assumes under 150 staff.
Results are for reference only. Confirm exact deductions via your payslip or the 4-Insurance EDI / your workplace administrator.

foreign-stock-tax.overview: The Foreign Stock Capital Gains Tax Calculator computes the tax for a Korean resident selling overseas shares (US, Japan, China, etc.). It converts the gain to KRW using the official exchange rate at both the purchase and sale dates, then applies the 2.5 million KRW annual deduction and the 22% rate (20% income tax + 2% local tax). If you already used part of the deduction on another holding this year, enter it in the 'deduction already used' field to apply only the remainder. Results are a preliminary simulation for the May Hometax filing; inputs are processed in your browser and never sent to a server.
useCases: Estimate tax before the May final filing after selling US stocks (S&P 500 ETFs or single names)
In a high-volatility year, simulate how the gap between the buy-date and sell-date exchange rates affects tax
Year-end loss-offset strategy: sell losing positions before winners to lower the taxable base
Plan sales within the 2.5M KRW deduction: back-calculate the gain that keeps tax at zero
Gauge the taxable gain on holdings before transferring proceeds to an ISA or pension account

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Enter the buy price (in foreign currency) and the exchange rate at purchase. Find the official rate at the Bank of Korea ECOS (ecos.bok.or.kr) or the 'applied rate' on your brokerage statement.
Enter the sell price (in foreign currency) and the exchange rate at sale. If you bought the same stock multiple times, use the weighted-average unit cost.
Enter transaction costs (fees + taxes, in KRW). For US stocks this includes brokerage commissions plus the SEC sell fee. Costs are subtracted from the gain.

If you already used part of the annual deduction on other foreign-stock gains this year, enter it under 'deduction already used' (0 if none). Click Calculate to see the taxable base, tax, and after-tax profit step by step.

formula.title: Formula (Income Tax Act §94·§104)
formula.body:
① KRW buy = buy price (foreign) × buy-date rate
② KRW sell = sell price (foreign) × sell-date rate
③ Capital gain = ② − ① − transaction cost (KRW)

④ Remaining deduction = 2,500,000 KRW − deduction already used this year
⑤ Taxable base = max(0, ③ − ④)
⑥ Capital gains tax = ⑤ × 22%
   (20% income tax + 2% local tax)
⑦ After-tax profit = ③ − ⑥

- The 22% rate is a flat rate regardless of income size
- The 2.5M KRW deduction resets once per year
- Exchange rate: official rate at the buy date and sell date — so FX moves also affect the gain

items.title: Example 1: 5M KRW gain on US stock — full deduction available
items.body:
Capital gain 5,000,000 − deduction 2,500,000 = taxable base 2,500,000 KRW
Tax = 2,500,000 × 22% = 550,000 KRW
After-tax profit = 5,000,000 − 550,000 = 4,450,000 KRW
→ Effective rate on the gain is 11%. The 2.5M deduction softens the burden.

items.title: Example 2: FX effect — dollar gain but the KRW gain vanishes
items.body:
Buy: $100 × 1,350 KRW = 135,000 KRW
Sell: $110 × 1,230 KRW = 135,300 KRW
A +10% gain in dollars, but only 300 KRW (+0.2%) in won
→ With a 300 KRW gain, it falls within the 2.5M deduction — zero tax
A classic case where the FX drop offset the price rise.

items.title: Example 3: Loss offset — sell the losing name first this year
items.body:
Stock A gain 6M KRW, Stock B loss 2M KRW (sold the same year)
Net gain = 6M − 2M = 4M KRW
Taxable base = 4M − 2.5M deduction = 1.5M KRW
Tax = 1.5M × 22% = 330,000 KRW
vs filing only the 6M gain without offset: (6M − 2.5M) × 22% = 770,000 KRW → ~440,000 KRW saved.

faq.question: When do I file foreign-stock capital gains tax?
faq.answer: You file the final return at Hometax (hometax.go.kr) from May 1 to May 31 each year, covering sales from January 1 to December 31 of the prior year. Failing to file triggers a non-filing penalty (20%) plus a late-payment penalty.

faq.question: How do I make the most of the 2.5M KRW deduction?
faq.answer: If your total annual foreign-stock gain (after loss offset) is 2.5M KRW or less, the tax is zero. Many investors time their realizations to stay at or under 2.5M ('tax harvesting'). The deduction is calculated separately from domestic stock and real-estate gains.

faq.question: Which exchange rate should I use?
faq.answer: Use the Bank of Korea base rate (the standard reference rate). Apply the rate for the actual transaction date — the acquisition date for the buy and the transfer date for the sell. You can back-calculate from the 'KRW-converted amount' on your brokerage statement or look it up directly at ECOS (ecos.bok.or.kr).

faq.question: Can I offset domestic-stock losses against foreign-stock gains?
faq.answer: Under current law, domestic listed-stock gains/losses and foreign-stock gains/losses cannot be offset against each other. Foreign stocks can offset each other within the same year. Even after the 2025 repeal of the financial-investment income tax, foreign stocks keep the separate 22% regime.

faq.question: Are foreign ETFs and single stocks taxed at the same rate?
faq.answer: If you buy an ETF listed on a foreign exchange through a Korean broker, it is taxed like a single stock at 22% on the gain. However, a foreign-index ETF listed on a Korean exchange (e.g. TIGER US S&P500) has its gains taxed as dividend income (15.4%) and is outside this tool's scope.

faq.question: What counts as transaction costs?
faq.answer: For US stocks, the main items are brokerage commissions (buy + sell) and the SEC sell fee (sale amount × about 0.00278%). Korea's securities transaction tax does not apply to foreign stocks. Transaction costs are subtracted from the gain, which lowers the tax.

cautions: This calculator simulates a single trade. If you bought the same stock multiple times, compute and enter the weighted-average cost yourself.
Compute your full annual taxable base by summing all foreign-stock trades and file accordingly; this tool handles only one trade.
A foreign-index ETF listed on a Korean exchange is subject to dividend income tax (15.4%), not capital gains tax, so it differs from this tool.
Exchange rates, tax rates, and deduction limits change when laws are amended. Confirm the exact filing via Hometax or a tax accountant.
Results are for reference only and have no legal force. For large amounts or complex trades, consult a licensed tax accountant.

labor-annual-leave.overview: The annual leave calculator applies the three-tier rule of Article 60 of the Korean Labor Standards Act automatically. Employees with under one year of service accrue 1 day per fully-worked month, up to 11 days; those with one year or more and at least 80% attendance receive 15 days; from the third year, 1 extra day is added every two years, capped at 25 days. Enter your hire date and reference date to instantly see accrued leave days and unused-leave compensation (based on monthly salary or daily ordinary wage).
useCases: New hires: see how many months in (including probation) leave starts to accrue
HR staff: compute leave entitlement per employee by length of service
Departing employees: convert remaining unused leave to cash and combine with severance
Contract and part-time workers: check monthly accrual rules in the first year
Dispute prevention: pinpoint the every-two-years increment at years 3, 5, 7, etc.

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Enter your hire date in YYYY-MM-DD format. The reference date defaults to today — change it to calculate as of a specific date.
Select whether attendance is 80% or higher. Below 80%, the monthly 1-day accrual rule (Article 60(3)) applies instead of 15 days.
Enter unused leave days and monthly salary (or daily ordinary wage) to auto-calculate compensation. Skip this if you don't need the cash value.
Review the step-by-step breakdown on the result screen. It also shows the leave you will accrue at your next anniversary (1st year, 2nd year, etc.).

formula.title: Formula (Labor Standards Act Article 60)
formula.body:
Under 1 year: accrued leave = completed months of service (max 11 days)

1 year or more: accrued leave = 15 days (attendance 80% or higher)

3 years or more: accrued leave = 15 + ⌊(years − 1) ÷ 2⌋ days (cap 25 days)
  e.g., year 3 = 16 days / year 5 = 17 days / year 9 = 19 days / year 21 = 25 days (cap)

Unused leave pay = daily ordinary wage × unused days
Daily ordinary wage = monthly ordinary wage ÷ 209 × 8

Basis: Labor Standards Act Article 60(1)(2)(4), MOEL administrative interpretation (209 hours)

items.title: Example 1: Employee at 7 months of service
items.body: Seven full months worked. Under 1 year, so 1 day accrues per month → 7 days of leave. After the first year ends, an additional 15 days accrue separately, granted independently of the 7 days earned in the first year (regardless of whether they were used).

items.title: Example 2: Employee at 5 years (monthly salary KRW 3.5M, 5 days unused)
items.body: Five years or more, so leave = 15 + ⌊(5−1)÷2⌋ = 15 + 2 = 17 days. Daily ordinary wage = 3,500,000 ÷ 209 × 8 ≈ KRW 133,971. Unused pay = 133,971 × 5 = about KRW 669,856.

items.title: Example 3: 21+ years of service (reaching the leave cap)
items.body: Year 21: 15 + ⌊(21−1)÷2⌋ = 15 + 10 = 25 days, reaching the statutory cap. Years 22 and 23 stay fixed at 25 days. A company may set a higher cap via work rules, but the statutory floor at this point is 25 days.

faq.question: Does the probation period count toward length of service?
faq.answer: Yes, probation counts as continuous service. Even if you are confirmed as a regular employee after 3 months of probation, accrual is counted from the hire date, so the leave accrual timing does not change.

faq.question: Is no leave accrued at all if attendance is below 80%?
faq.answer: No. In a year with attendance below 80%, the 15 days do not accrue, but 1 day still accrues per fully-worked month that year (Article 60(3)). For example, at 75% attendance in year 2, leave accrues for each fully-worked month (up to 11 days).

faq.question: Is the under-1-year leave (max 11 days) separate from the 15 days at 1 year?
faq.answer: Since the May 2018 amendment, yes — they are separate. In the first year you accrue up to 11 days (1 per month), and at the one-year anniversary an additional 15 days accrue independently. The 15 days are newly granted at the anniversary regardless of whether the first-year leave was used.

faq.question: When must unused leave pay be paid?
faq.answer: Workplaces that lawfully run a leave-usage promotion scheme are exempt from paying compensation for unused leave. Without such promotion, payment is due by the wage payment date of the month following the leave expiry (typically the one-year expiry date).

faq.question: Do fixed-term employees accrue leave?
faq.answer: Regardless of contract length, leave accrues if weekly contractual hours are 15 or more. A one-year fixed-term worker can be paid for the leave days accrued before the contract ends. However, very-short-time workers (under 15 hours per week) are excluded from leave entitlement.

faq.question: What if the company refuses to pay unused leave on departure?
faq.answer: Unused leave pay must be paid within 14 days of the departure date (Labor Standards Act Article 36). If unpaid, you can file a wage-arrears complaint with a regional MOEL office, and the claim must be exercised within 3 years.

cautions: This calculator computes statutory leave under the Labor Standards Act and does not reflect leave granted above the statutory standard via work rules or collective agreements.

Attendance calculation (actual days worked vs. scheduled working days) varies by workplace in its details (holiday treatment, whether parental leave is included, etc.), so confirm the actual figure with HR or a labor attorney.

The 'ordinary wage' used for leave pay includes regular, uniform allowances beyond base pay. Reimbursement-type allowances such as meal or transport allowances are usually excluded from ordinary wage, but this can vary by workplace.
Workplaces that run a leave-usage promotion scheme may be exempt from paying for unused leave — verify lawful promotion separately.
Results are for reference only; for precise leave calculation, consult your workplace work rules and a labor attorney.

labor-min-wage-monthly.overview: The minimum wage to monthly salary calculator instantly converts an hourly wage into a monthly salary and annual income including weekly holiday pay. It automatically applies Korea's standard 209-hour basis (40 weekly hours + 8 weekly holiday hours × 4.345 weeks per month), the key point in 'monthly conversion', and supports pro-rata calculation when you enter shorter weekly hours. The 2026 minimum hourly wage is KRW 10,320 (monthly equivalent KRW 2,156,880, MOEL Notice No. 2025-47); 2025 was KRW 10,030.
useCases: Job seekers: check whether an offered hourly wage violates the minimum by converting to monthly
Part-time and short-time workers: calculate expected monthly pay based on weekly contractual hours
Employers and HR: pre-simulate labor cost increases in monthly and annual terms when the minimum wage rises
Contract negotiation: compare the difference depending on whether weekly holiday pay is included
Foreign workers: verify whether your hourly wage meets Korea's legal minimum standard

howTo.title: How to use (3 steps)
steps: Enter your hourly wage. If left blank, the minimum hourly wage for the year (KRW 10,320 for 2026 · KRW 10,030 for 2025) is filled in automatically.
Set your weekly contractual hours. For full-time work (5 days, 8 hours), keep the default 40 hours; for short-time work (30, 25 hours, etc.), enter the actual hours.
Choose whether to include weekly holiday pay. When included, the monthly salary is computed using 209 hours (40-hour basis) or pro-rata hours; when excluded, only actual working hours are reflected.

formula.title: Formula (basis for 209 hours)
formula.body:
Monthly contractual hours (with weekly holiday, 40-hour basis)
  = (40 weekly hours + 8 weekly holiday hours) × average weeks per month
  = 48 hours × (52 weeks ÷ 12 months)
  = 48 × 4.345 ≈ 208.56 → 209 hours by administrative interpretation

Monthly salary = hourly wage × 209 hours
2026 minimum wage: KRW 10,320 / hour (2025: KRW 10,030)
Minimum monthly salary = 10,320 × 209 = KRW 2,156,880

Short-time work (N weekly hours):
  weekly holiday hours = (N ÷ 40) × 8 hours (cap 8 hours)
  monthly contractual hours = (N + weekly holiday hours) × 4.345
  monthly salary = hourly wage × monthly contractual hours

Basis: Labor Standards Act Article 55 (weekly holiday), Minimum Wage Act, MOEL administrative interpretation (209 hours)

items.title: Example 1: Full-time at minimum wage (40 weekly hours, 2025/2026 basis)
items.body: KRW 10,320 × 209 hours = KRW 2,156,880 / month. Annualized to KRW 25,882,560. Weekly holiday pay alone = 10,320 × about 34.8 monthly holiday hours = about KRW 359,136 / month.

items.title: Example 2: Short-time work, 30 weekly hours (hourly KRW 12,000)
items.body: Weekly holiday hours = (30 ÷ 40) × 8 = 6 hours. Monthly contractual hours = (30 + 6) × 4.345 ≈ 156.4 hours. Monthly salary = 12,000 × 156 = about KRW 1,876,800.

items.title: Example 3: Excluding weekly holiday pay (40 weekly hours, hourly KRW 13,000)
items.body: Monthly contractual hours excluding holiday = 40 × 4.345 ≈ 173.8 → 174 hours. Monthly salary = 13,000 × 174 = KRW 2,262,000. With weekly holiday pay, 13,000 × 209 = KRW 2,717,000, a difference of about KRW 455,000.

faq.question: Why is 209 hours the basis?
faq.answer: Adding 8 weekly holiday hours to 40 weekly working hours gives 48 hours per week; multiplied by the average weeks per month, 4.345 (52 weeks ÷ 12 months), this equals 208.56 hours. MOEL administrative interpretation and case law round this to 209 hours as the basis for ordinary wage calculation.

faq.question: What is the 2026 minimum hourly wage?
faq.answer: The minimum wage is decided and announced by the Minimum Wage Commission on August 5 each year and applies from January 1 of the following year. The 2026 minimum hourly wage is KRW 10,320 (+2.9%, MOEL Notice No. 2025-47), already reflected in this tool; 2025 was KRW 10,030.

faq.question: Does every worker receive weekly holiday pay?
faq.answer: It arises when a worker with at least 15 weekly contractual hours has perfect attendance on all scheduled working days that week. Very-short-time workers with under 15 weekly hours do not receive weekly holiday pay.

faq.question: Are meal and transport allowances included in the minimum wage?
faq.answer: Since 2024, meal and transport allowances paid regularly at least once a month are within the scope of the minimum wage. The phase-out of the previously applied exclusion ratios means most regularly paid amounts are now counted. Check MOEL standards for the detailed scope of inclusion.

faq.question: What if a part-time hourly wage is below the minimum?
faq.answer: A minimum wage violation is a criminal offense punishable by up to 3 years imprisonment or a fine of up to KRW 20 million. A contract with an hourly wage below the minimum is void for that portion, and the shortfall against the minimum wage can be claimed within 3 years.

faq.question: Are overtime, night, and holiday premiums reflected in this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator computes only ordinary wage based on contractual hours. Overtime (+50%), night (+50%), and holiday (+50% to 100%) premiums are separate; after confirming your work schedule, you can add them in the net salary calculator.

cautions: The tool reflects KRW 10,320 for 2026 and KRW 10,030 for 2025. The minimum wage is announced every August and this tool is updated as soon as it is official.
The 209 monthly hours are based on full-time 40-hour work; short-time workers have different pro-rata hours — enter your actual contractual hours for an accurate value.
Confirm details such as inclusion of meal/transport allowances and the probation period (3 months, where 90% of the minimum wage may apply) against your workplace rules and MOEL standards.
Results are for reference only; confirm the actual pay agreement with your employment contract and your employer or a labor attorney.

labor-severance.overview: The severance pay calculator precisely applies the formula 'daily average wage × 30 × (service days ÷ 365)' under Article 8 of the Korean Employee Retirement Benefit Security Act. It computes the average wage from the total wages of the three months before departure (base pay + fixed allowances) and supports pro-rata inclusion of 3/12 of regular bonuses and annual leave pay. It also automatically handles the rule that applies the ordinary wage when the average wage is lower than the ordinary wage.
useCases: Departing employees: pre-calculate the expected total severance to plan re-employment
HR staff: pre-compute severance provisions from each employee's service period and wage data
Contract enders: check whether fixed-term workers qualify for severance after one year or more
Bonus-inclusive calculation: understand the effect of annual regular bonuses on the average wage
DC-type retirement pension members: estimate the annual settlement amount to review funding adequacy

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Enter the hire date and resignation date. Service days are calculated automatically, and if under 365 days (1 year) it immediately notes that no severance accrues.
Enter total wages for the three months before departure. This is three months of base pay plus fixed allowances paid in the same period (position allowance, family allowance, etc.).
Optionally enter regular bonus (annual total) and annual leave pay (accrued in the prior year). Each is multiplied by 3/12 and included pro-rata in the average wage.

If you need the ordinary wage comparison, also enter the monthly ordinary wage. If it is higher than the average wage, the ordinary wage is applied automatically so severance is computed on the more favorable amount.

formula.title: Formula (Employee Retirement Benefit Security Act Article 8)
formula.body:
Severance = daily average wage × 30 × (service days ÷ 365)

Daily average wage
  = (3-month total wages + bonus × 3/12 + leave pay × 3/12) ÷ 90 days
  But if daily average wage < daily ordinary wage, the ordinary wage applies

Daily ordinary wage = monthly ordinary wage ÷ 209 × 8

e.g., 3-month wages KRW 9M, annual bonus KRW 2M, leave pay KRW 0.5M:
  = (9,000,000 + 500,000 + 125,000) ÷ 90
  = 9,625,000 ÷ 90 ≈ KRW 106,944 / day

Basis: Employee Retirement Benefit Security Act Article 8, Labor Standards Act Article 2 (definitions of average wage and ordinary wage)

items.title: Example 1: 2 years of service, monthly salary KRW 3M (no bonus or leave pay)
items.body: 3-month total wages = KRW 9,000,000. Daily average wage = 9,000,000 ÷ 90 = KRW 100,000. Service days = 730. Severance = 100,000 × 30 × (730 ÷ 365) = 100,000 × 30 × 2 = KRW 6,000,000.

items.title: Example 2: 3 years 6 months, monthly salary KRW 3.5M + annual bonus KRW 7M
Example 3: Ordinary wage applied because average wage < ordinary wage
items.body: Unpaid sick leave in the three months before departure reduced wages → average wage KRW 80,000 / day. Monthly ordinary wage KRW 2,090,000 → daily ordinary wage = 2,090,000 ÷ 209 × 8 ≈ KRW 80,000. The two values are equal, so the ordinary wage is used. In practice, when the ordinary wage exceeds the average wage, the ordinary wage must be applied automatically.

faq.question: Is there really no severance for under one year of continuous service?
faq.answer: Correct. Article 8 of the Employee Retirement Benefit Security Act specifies one year or more of continuous service as the condition for severance.
