If you leave after 364 days, no severance accrues. However, if short contracts under one year are repeated, whether the service is continuous must be assessed separately.

faq.question: How are bonuses included in the average wage?
faq.answer: For regularly paid bonuses, 3/12 (25%) of the annual total is added to the three-month wages of the average wage period. Irregular or one-off special bonuses are excluded from the average wage calculation.

faq.question: If I have a DC-type (defined contribution) pension, is there separate severance?
faq.answer: A DC-type retirement pension is one where the employer contributes at least 1/12 of annual wages to the worker's account each year. For DC members, the amount accumulated in the personal account is the retirement benefit, so they do not separately claim severance from the employer upon departure.

faq.question: Do parental leave and sick leave count toward service days?
faq.answer: Parental leave counts toward the service period (Equal Employment Act Article 19). However, the parental leave period is excluded from the average wage calculation, and the average wage period is extended by that duration. For sick leave (non-work injury or illness), treatment varies by workplace, so confirmation is needed.

faq.question: When must severance be paid?
faq.answer: As a rule it must be paid within 14 days of the departure date (Employee Retirement Benefit Security Act Article 9). The payment deadline can be extended by agreement, but delays without agreement incur 20% annual delay interest.

faq.question: Is severance taxed?
faq.answer: Severance is subject to retirement income tax. After applying the years-of-service deduction and the converted-wage deduction, it is taxed separately at progressive rates of 6 to 45%. Transferring it to a retirement pension (IRP) defers taxation until withdrawal, providing a tax-saving effect.

cautions: This calculator uses a fixed 90-day average wage period. If the exact number of days in the actual three months before departure (89 to 92) differs, the result may slightly differ from the actual payment.
For DC-type pension members, the actual amount received may vary with investment results and may differ from this calculator's result.
If there is a history of abnormal wage payment such as wage arrears, the average wage period must be adjusted, so a labor attorney's confirmation is needed.
If interim severance settlement was made, calculation starts from after the interim settlement, so it may differ from the total service days.
Results are for reference only; confirm precise severance settlement with your employer or a labor attorney.

labor-weekly-rest-pay.overview: The weekly holiday pay calculator precisely computes the pay due on the one paid weekly holiday under Article 55 of the Korean Labor Standards Act. It automatically verifies the two accrual conditions — at least 15 weekly contractual hours and perfect attendance on that week's scheduled working days — and calculates 8 hours' worth for full-time 40-hour workers and a pro-rata (weekly hours ÷ 40) × 8 hours for short-time workers. Enter an hourly wage or a monthly salary (reverse-derived from 209 hours) to see results in weekly, monthly, and annual terms.
useCases: Part-time and short-time workers: check how much weekly holiday pay you get from your hourly wage and weekly hours
Employers: pre-plan total labor cost including weekly holiday pay when hiring part-time staff
Workers near the 15-hour threshold: judge whether weekly holiday pay accrues and compute the amount if it does
Employment contract review: reverse-check whether weekly holiday pay is included in the contracted monthly salary
Wage dispute preparation: estimate the claim amount by figuring monthly and annual accumulated unpaid weekly holiday pay

howTo.title: How to use (3 steps)
steps: Enter your hourly wage. If you have a monthly salary, enter it and the hourly wage is auto-derived using the 209-hour basis.
Enter your weekly contractual hours. The default is 40 hours (5 days, 8 hours per day). Below 15 weekly hours, it automatically notes that no weekly holiday pay accrues.
From the results, check weekly holiday hours, one week's holiday pay, monthly holiday pay (converted over 4.345 weeks), and annual holiday pay. The step-by-step breakdown is shown as well.

formula.title: Formula (Labor Standards Act Article 55)
formula.body:
Accrual conditions: at least 15 weekly contractual hours + perfect attendance on scheduled working days

Weekly holiday hours = (weekly contractual hours ÷ 40) × 8 hours (cap 8 hours)
  - 40 weekly hours: weekly holiday hours = 8 hours
  - 30 weekly hours: weekly holiday hours = (30 ÷ 40) × 8 = 6 hours
  - 20 weekly hours: weekly holiday hours = (20 ÷ 40) × 8 = 4 hours

One week's holiday pay = weekly holiday hours × hourly wage
Monthly holiday pay = one week's holiday pay × 4.345 weeks
Annual holiday pay = monthly holiday pay × 12 months

Basis: Labor Standards Act Article 55 (paid weekly holiday), MOEL administrative interpretation (pro-rata for short-time workers)

items.title: Example 1: Full-time, 40 weekly hours (hourly KRW 12,000)
items.body: Weekly holiday hours = 8 hours. One week's holiday pay = 12,000 × 8 = KRW 96,000. Monthly holiday pay = 96,000 × 4.345 ≈ KRW 417,120. Annual holiday pay ≈ KRW 5,005,440. Under a 209-hour monthly salary, this pay is already included.

items.title: Example 2: Part-time, 20 weekly hours (hourly KRW 10,320, 2026 minimum wage)
items.body: Weekly holiday hours = (20 ÷ 40) × 8 = 4 hours. One week's holiday pay = 10,320 × 4 = KRW 41,280. Monthly holiday pay = 41,280 × 4.345 ≈ KRW 179,362. Pure weekly working wage (20 hours) = 10,320 × 20 × 4.345 ≈ KRW 896,808. Monthly total including holiday ≈ KRW 1,076,170.

items.title: Example 3: Very-short-time worker, 14 weekly hours (no weekly holiday pay)
items.body: Weekly contractual hours are 14, below 15, so no weekly holiday pay accrues. In this case monthly pay = hourly wage × 14 hours × 4.345 weeks only. Beware of the practice of contracting at 14 hours per week to avoid weekly holiday pay.

faq.question: Is there always no weekly holiday pay below 15 weekly hours?
faq.answer: Under Article 18 of the Labor Standards Act, workers whose average weekly contractual hours over four weeks are below 15 are excluded from weekly holiday pay. Because it is calculated as a four-week average, even if some week was below 15 hours, weekly holiday pay can still accrue if the four-week average is 15 hours or more.

faq.question: Does no weekly holiday pay accrue without perfect attendance?
faq.answer: No weekly holiday pay accrues in a week with an absence on a scheduled working day. However, lateness, early leave, and statutory public holidays are not treated as absences and do not affect weekly holiday pay. A day on which annual leave is used is treated as attendance, so weekly holiday pay accrues.

faq.question: Do monthly-paid workers receive weekly holiday pay separately?
faq.answer: For monthly-paid workers, weekly holiday pay is generally already included in the monthly salary. The 209-hour monthly salary is exactly such a case (40 weekly hours + 8 weekly holiday hours × 4.345 weeks = 209 hours). If the employment contract lists 'weekly holiday pay' as a separate item beyond 'base pay', beware of double counting.

faq.question: What if the employer does not pay weekly holiday pay?
faq.answer: Non-payment of weekly holiday pay is wage arrears and can be reported to a regional MOEL office. There is a 3-year claim right, and if wage arrears are confirmed, the employer faces criminal punishment and must pay the unpaid wages.

faq.question: Do daily and dispatched workers accrue weekly holiday pay?
faq.answer: Even daily workers accrue weekly holiday pay if, at the same workplace during one week, they complete at least 15 weekly contractual hours and have perfect attendance. Even with no scheduled work the next week, they can claim it if that week's conditions were met.

faq.question: Is weekly holiday pay included in the average wage used for severance?
faq.answer: Weekly holiday pay is included in the ordinary wage. So for monthly-paid workers, the entire 209-hour monthly salary becomes the ordinary wage, and the daily ordinary wage derived from it becomes the comparison basis for leave pay and severance.

cautions: Whether the 15-hour threshold is met is judged by the four-week average weekly contractual hours, so you cannot conclude there is no weekly holiday pay simply because 'one week was 14 hours' — check the four-week average.
Depending on absences, weekly holiday pay may not accrue for that week. Whether attendance is perfect follows the workplace work rules.
This calculator is a simple computation based on contractual hours and does not reflect actual attendance or the four-week average calculation.
Be sure to check the employment contract for the difference between a monthly salary that includes weekly holiday pay and a contract that pays weekly holiday pay separately.
Results are for reference only; for precise calculation in a wage dispute, consult a labor attorney or a MOEL consultation.

electric-voltage-drop.overview: The Voltage Drop Calculator takes conductor cross-section, current, run length, and phase type, then computes the voltage drop (V) and drop percentage (%) and compares it against the distance-based allowable limits in Korea's KEC wiring standard (formerly the Naeseon code). It supports both copper and aluminum conductors and applies the correct coefficient for single-phase 2-wire, single-phase 3-wire, 3-phase 3-wire, and 3-phase 4-wire circuits. Use it to confirm whether a wire gauge is adequate for the run distance and whether the voltage at the far end stays within equipment tolerance.
useCases: Check whether the drop exceeds the limit when a load is far from the distribution panel
Decide whether to step up to a larger wire gauge because of excessive drop
Compare voltage drop between single-phase and 3-phase wiring options
Assess the extra drop when using aluminum conductors instead of copper
Learn the voltage-drop formula for Korean electrician licensing exams

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Enter the line (or phase) voltage (V) and load current (A) — e.g., 220V single-phase, 380V 3-phase.
Enter the run length (m, one-way) — the actual wire length from the panel to the load.
Select the conductor cross-section (㎟) from the KS standard sizes, the conductor material (copper/aluminum), and the phase type (single-2, single-3, 3-phase-3, 3-phase-4).
Read the voltage drop (V), drop percentage (%), the distance-based limit, and the pass/fail verdict. If it fails, increase the cross-section or shorten the run.

formula.title: Formula & basis (KEC / former Naeseon code)
formula.body:
■ Voltage drop
  e = (coefficient × L × I) ÷ (1000 × A)
    L = run length (m, one-way), I = current (A), A = cross-section (㎟)

■ Coefficient by phase type (copper)
  Single-phase 2-wire        35.6
  3-phase 3-wire             30.8
  Single-phase 3-wire / 3-phase 4-wire  17.8
  Aluminum conductor: coefficient × 1.6

■ Drop percentage
  e(%) = (e ÷ V) × 100

■ Allowable drop by distance (combined feeder + branch)
  ≤ 60m — 3% / ≤ 120m — 5% / ≤ 200m — 6% / > 200m — 7%

items.title: Example 1: 220V single-phase, 20A, 50m, 4㎟ copper
items.body: e = (35.6 × 50 × 20) ÷ (1000 × 4) = 8.9V. Drop = 8.9 ÷ 220 × 100 ≈ 4.05%. The ≤60m limit is 3%, so this fails. Increasing to 6㎟ or larger brings it down to about 2.7% and passes.

items.title: Example 3: switching from copper to aluminum
items.body: Same as Example 1 but aluminum: coefficient 35.6 × 1.6 = 56.96 → e ≈ 14.24V, drop ≈ 6.47%. Aluminum's lower conductivity raises the drop by about 60% at the same gauge — you must size up at least one step.

faq.question: Is the run length one-way or round-trip?
faq.answer: Enter the one-way length (panel to load). The return current path is already accounted for in the phase coefficients (35.6, 30.8, 17.8), so do not double the distance.

faq.question: What are the 3% / 5% allowable limits based on?
faq.answer: They come from Korea's KEC wiring standard (formerly the Naeseon code): 3% for runs ≤60m, 5% for ≤120m, 6% for ≤200m, 7% over 200m. These are end-of-run values combining feeder and branch circuits, set to keep lighting and equipment within their normal operating voltage.

faq.question: What should I do if the drop exceeds the limit?
faq.answer: Either (1) step up the conductor cross-section, (2) shorten the run by relocating the panel, or (3) switch single-phase to 3-phase (lower coefficient). Increasing the wire gauge is the most common fix.

faq.question: Why does aluminum wire have a larger voltage drop?
faq.answer: Aluminum's conductivity is about 61% of copper's, so at the same cross-section its resistance — and voltage drop — is roughly 1.6× higher. This calculator applies a ×1.6 factor automatically when aluminum is selected. To match copper's drop, size aluminum one or two steps larger.

faq.question: Does it account for DC or power factor?
faq.answer: This is a simplified AC calculation assuming a power factor of 1, ignoring line reactance (inductance). Long, high-current, or low-power-factor circuits are more affected by reactance — use a licensed engineer or design software for precise work.

cautions: Simplified AC calculation assuming power factor 1 and ignoring reactance. Precise design needs full line-impedance calculation.
Enter the one-way length; the round-trip path is already in the phase coefficient.
Allowable limits follow the KEC distance bands; actual design depends on feeder/branch allocation and equipment characteristics.
Reference calculation only. Have an electrical safety manager or qualified engineer review actual installations.
Cross-section must be a KS standard size.

timber-concrete.overview: The Concrete Volume Calculator computes pour volume for two foundation types common in light-frame construction: Strip Footings and Mat Slabs. Enter width, depth (or thickness), and length to get net volume, waste-adjusted order volume (default 5% waste), a quick rebar estimate (default 90 kg/m³ for residential strip footings), and the number of ready-mix trucks based on the Korean standard 6 m³ per truck. It is designed to catch ordering errors before concrete is dispatched to site.
useCases: Pre-order check for ready-mix quantity and truck count before timber-frame house foundation work
Concrete volume takeoff for small sheds, farm buildings, or container pads
Side-by-side comparison of different slab thicknesses to balance cost vs strength
Cross-checking a contractor's concrete quote against your own calculation
Quick rebar weight estimate for preliminary steel budget

howTo.title: How to Use (4 Steps)
steps: Select foundation type. Choose 'Strip Footing' for a continuous wall footing, or 'Mat Slab' for a flat slab covering the entire footprint.
Enter width (m), depth or thickness (m), and length (m). For strip footings, depth is the vertical dimension below grade. For mat slabs, thickness is the slab depth.
Adjust waste factor (%). The default 5% covers typical pour loss and form leakage on straightforward sites. Increase to 10% for uneven terrain or complex formwork.

Check or adjust rebar density (kg/m³). The default 90 kg/m³ is a rule-of-thumb for residential strip footings with D10–D13 double-layer rebar. Review the results: adjusted volume (m³), rebar weight (kg), and ready-mix truck count.

formula.title: Formulas & References
formula.body:
■ Net Volume
  V_raw = width (m) × depth or thickness (m) × length (m)

■ Waste-Adjusted Volume
  V_adj = V_raw × (1 + waste % / 100)
  Default waste: 5% (max 30%)

■ Rebar Weight (quick estimate)
  W_rebar = V_adj × rebar density (default 90 kg/m³)
  Note: Rule-of-thumb for residential footings only. Use structural drawings for permitted buildings.

■ Ready-Mix Truck Count
  N_truck = V_adj ÷ 6 m³
  Korean standard drum mixer: 6 m³ capacity (KS F 4009 convention)
  Result is shown as a decimal — round up when placing order

items.title: Example 1: Farm cabin strip footing (10 m × 0.4 m × 0.3 m)
items.body: Width 0.4 m, depth 0.3 m, length 10 m → net volume 1.2 m³. With 5% waste: 1.26 m³. Truck count: 0.21 (order 1 truck — check minimum order with supplier). Rebar at 90 kg/m³: ≈ 113 kg.

items.title: Example 2: Timber-frame house mat slab (8 m × 10 m, 150 mm thick)
items.body: Width 8 m, thickness 0.15 m, length 10 m → net volume 12 m³. With 5% waste: 12.6 m³. Truck count: 2.1 (order 3 trucks to cover 12.6 m³ across two loads). Rebar at 90 kg/m³: ≈ 1,134 kg. Cross-check with structural engineer's rebar schedule before ordering.

items.title: Example 3: 4 isolated column pads (each 0.6 m × 0.4 m × 0.6 m)
items.body: Single pad: 0.6 × 0.4 × 0.6 = 0.144 m³. To total 4 pads, enter length as 2.4 m (0.6 × 4) or multiply result by 4 manually. Total with 5% waste: ≈ 0.605 m³. Minimum ready-mix order in Korea is typically 3 m³ — coordinate pour timing with other work.

faq.question: Should I use 5% or 10% waste?
faq.answer: 5% covers flat, simple sites with tight formwork. Use 7–10% for uneven ground, complex footing shapes, or whenever you are unsure. Running short after the truck leaves is expensive — when in doubt, add more waste factor.

faq.question: Is one Korean ready-mix truck exactly 6 m³?
faq.answer: The standard agitator truck drum holds 6 m³ of mixed concrete. Some larger trucks carry 7–8 m³. This tool uses 6 m³ as the baseline. Always confirm the actual truck capacity with your ready-mix supplier before placing the order.

faq.question: Is the calculation formula different for strip footings vs mat slabs?
faq.answer: The formula is identical: V = width × dimension × length. The difference is terminology — for strip footings the middle dimension is 'depth' (vertical below grade), for mat slabs it is 'thickness' (slab depth from top to bottom).

faq.question: What does the 90 kg/m³ rebar density represent?
faq.answer: It is a rule-of-thumb mid-point for residential strip footings reinforced with D10–D13 bars in a double grid (typical range 80–100 kg/m³). If you have structural drawings, use the rebar schedule instead — that will be far more accurate. Treat this tool's rebar output as a budget placeholder only.

faq.question: Does this calculator handle concrete mix design (strength grade)?
faq.answer: No. This tool focuses on volume and rebar weight only. Specify concrete compressive strength (e.g., 21 MPa, 24 MPa) separately when ordering from a ready-mix plant. For most residential foundations in Korea, 21–24 MPa (fck) is standard. Check the structural general notes on your drawings.

faq.question: How do I calculate L-shaped or T-shaped footings?
faq.answer: Split the cross-section into rectangular segments, calculate each segment separately, and add the volumes together. For isolated pads, enter one pad's dimensions, note the volume, and multiply by the number of pads. Complex shapes benefit from a structural drawing takeoff rather than this tool alone.

cautions: Rebar weight (90 kg/m³) is a quick estimate without structural drawings. For permitted buildings, always follow the rebar schedule from your structural engineer.
Truck count is for reference. Confirm minimum order volume (usually 3 m³), delivery time limits, and pour sequencing with your ready-mix supplier.
Waste factor varies by site conditions. The default 5% suits straightforward sites; complex or uneven sites may need 10%.
This calculator does not assess structural adequacy. Any building requiring a permit must be reviewed by a licensed structural engineer.
Concrete mix design variables (water-cement ratio, slump, admixtures) are outside the scope of this tool.

timber-lumber.overview: The Lumber Board Feet Calculator converts width, thickness, length, and quantity into total volume (m³), Japanese/Korean Sai (才), North American Board Feet (BF), and weight (kg) by species — all in a single calculation. It is built for mixed-unit job sites where import SPF or Douglas Fir invoices are quoted in board feet while domestic lumber dealers still price in Sai, eliminating the unit conversion errors that inflate material costs.
useCases: Total cost check for SPF 2×4 or 2×6 rafter stock purchased at a board-foot price
Sai-unit quantity and cost for domestic larch or pine sourced from a Korean lumber yard
Pre-delivery weight check to stay within truck payload limits
Comparing cost-per-volume between Douglas Fir and SPF at current market prices
Verifying a supplier's quoted board-foot count against your own dimensions

howTo.title: How to Use (3 Steps)
steps: Enter width (mm), thickness (mm), length (mm), and quantity. Note that nominal dimensions differ from actual (dressed) dimensions — a 2×4 is actually 38 mm × 89 mm after drying and planing.
Select wood species: SPF (Spruce-Pine-Fir mix), Larch, Pine, Cedar, Douglas Fir, or Oak. The density value for your chosen species (at 12% moisture content) is applied to calculate weight.
Read the results: total volume (m³), Sai, Board Feet, and weight (kg). Multiply by your unit price (per Sai or per BF) to get a material cost estimate.

formula.title: Formulas & References
formula.body:
■ Volume per piece
  V_per = (width mm × thickness mm × length mm) ÷ 1,000,000,000  [m³]

■ Total volume
  V_total = V_per × quantity

■ Sai (才) conversion
  Sai = V_total ÷ 0.003342
  (1 才 = 1 Sun × 1 Sun × 12 Shaku ≈ 30.303 mm × 30.303 mm × 3,636.4 mm ≈ 0.003342 m³)

■ Board Feet (BF) conversion
  BF = V_total ÷ 0.002359737
  (1 BF = 1 ft × 1 ft × 1 in = 144 in³ ≈ 2,359.737 cm³)

■ Weight (air-dry, 12% moisture content)
  W = V_total × species density [kg/m³]
  SPF: 460 / Pine: 500 / Larch: 540 / Cedar: 380 / Douglas Fir: 530 / Oak: 750

items.title: Example 1: 30 pieces of 2×6 SPF rafter stock (3,600 mm)
items.body: Width 140 mm, thickness 38 mm, length 3,600 mm, qty 30, species SPF. Volume per piece ≈ 0.019152 m³. Total volume ≈ 0.5746 m³. Sai ≈ 172 才. Board Feet ≈ 243.5 BF. Weight ≈ 264 kg (460 kg/m³ × 0.5746).

items.title: Example 2: 10 pieces of larch 4×4 post (3,000 mm)
items.body: Width 89 mm, thickness 89 mm, length 3,000 mm, qty 10, species Larch. Total volume ≈ 0.2376 m³. Sai ≈ 71 才. BF ≈ 100.7. Larch density 540 kg/m³ → weight ≈ 128 kg. Comfortably fits on a 1-ton pickup.

items.title: Example 3: 5 oak slabs for a dining table (400 × 50 × 2,000 mm)
items.body: Total volume = (400 × 50 × 2000 / 1,000,000,000) × 5 = 0.2 m³. Sai ≈ 60 才. Weight ≈ 150 kg (750 kg/m³). Oak is dense — 50 pieces of this size would weigh 1.5 tonnes. Plan lifting equipment accordingly.

faq.question: Why is a 2×4 actually 38 mm × 89 mm?
faq.answer: North American dimensional lumber is sold by its nominal (rough-sawn) size of 2 in × 4 in (50.8 × 101.6 mm). After kiln drying and planing (S4S — Surfaced Four Sides), the lumber shrinks to its actual dressed size of 38 mm × 89 mm. Korea's KS F 3020 adopts these same North American dressed dimensions. Always input the actual (dressed) dimension, not the nominal label.

faq.question: What is the difference between Sai (才) and Board Feet (BF)?
faq.answer: Sai is the traditional Korean and Japanese lumber unit: 1 才 ≈ 0.003342 m³ (one Sun square by 12 Shaku long). Board Feet is the North American unit: 1 BF ≈ 0.002360 m³ (one foot square by one inch thick). For the same volume: 1 m³ ≈ 299 Sai ≈ 424 BF. Korean lumber yards typically quote per Sai; imported lumber invoices use BF.

faq.question: Does moisture content affect the weight result?
faq.answer: Yes. This tool uses air-dry density at 12% moisture content (MC), which is typical of kiln-dried (KD) structural lumber. Green lumber (fresh-sawn, MC 30–60%) is 30–60% heavier. If you are unsure of the actual MC, weigh a sample board and scale proportionally.

faq.question: What exactly is SPF?
faq.answer: SPF stands for Spruce-Pine-Fir, a combined species designation for Canadian structural lumber that groups White Spruce, Red Pine, and Balsam Fir. It is the dominant import species for light-frame (stick-frame) construction due to its low density (≈ 460 kg/m³), ease of working, and wide availability. All three species have similar mechanical properties and are grouped for grading and pricing.

faq.question: How do I enter dimensions in Shaku (尺) or feet?
faq.answer: This tool uses millimetres. Conversion: 1 Shaku (尺) = 303.03 mm. A standard 12-Shaku board (≈ 4.8 m) = 3,636 mm. One foot = 304.8 mm, so 12 ft = 3,658 mm. Enter the converted value in the length field.

cautions: Weight is calculated at 12% moisture content (air-dry). Green or high-MC lumber can be 30–60% heavier in practice.
Always enter actual (dressed) dimensions, not nominal dimensions. A 2×4 is 38 × 89 mm, not 50 × 100 mm.
This tool outputs quantities only — it does not provide market prices per Sai or per BF.
Volume and weight calculations are for procurement planning only and do not assess structural load capacity or span adequacy.

timber-plywood.overview: The Plywood Sheet Calculator takes a wall, floor, or roof area (m²) and returns the number of standard 4×8 ft (1,220×2,440 mm = 2.9768 m²) 18 mm plywood sheets required, the waste-adjusted order area, total weight (33 kg/sheet), and an estimated nail count based on IRC R602.3 edge (150 mm) and field (300 mm) spacing. It is calibrated to Korean distribution-standard sheet sizes so quantities translate directly to a purchase order.
useCases: Wall sheathing sheet count before framing a timber house exterior
Subfloor plywood quantity for floor joist decking
Roof deck plywood takeoff including slope-adjusted actual area
Replacement quantity for damaged wall or floor sheathing panels
Material budget based on sheet count before getting supplier quotes

howTo.title: How to Use (3 Steps)
steps: Enter the area (m²). For walls, use perimeter × wall height. For floors, use room width × room length. For roofs, enter the actual slope surface area, not the plan footprint.
Set the waste factor (%). The default 10% covers cutting loss and breakage. For simple rectangular areas use 10%; for areas with many openings or complex angles, use 15–20%.
Read the results: sheet count, adjusted area (m²), total weight (kg), and estimated nail count. Uncheck 'Estimate Fasteners' to skip the nail count.

formula.title: Formulas & References
formula.body:
■ Sheet Specification
  Plywood 18 mm: 1,220 mm × 2,440 mm = 2.9768 m² / sheet, 33 kg / sheet

■ Waste-Adjusted Area
  A_adj = area (m²) × (1 + waste % / 100)
  Default waste: 10% (max 30%)

■ Sheet Count (ceiling)
  N = ceil(A_adj ÷ 2.9768)

■ Total Weight
  W = N × 33 kg

■ Nail Count Estimate (per IRC R602.3)
  Edge nails: perimeter (mm) ÷ 150 mm + 4 (end-point correction)
  Field nails: interior stud rows × (height ÷ 300 mm + 1)
    Stud spacing assumed 600 mm → interior rows = floor(1220 ÷ 600) − 1 = 1 row
  Total nails = nails per sheet × sheet count

items.title: Example 1: Exterior wall sheathing, 4 walls (12 m × 3 m = 36 m²)
items.body: Area 36 m², 10% waste → adjusted 39.6 m². Sheets = ceil(39.6 ÷ 2.9768) = 14. Total weight: 14 × 33 = 462 kg. Nails: ≈ 59 per sheet × 14 = 826 nails. Order 900 nails (2 boxes of 500).

items.title: Example 2: Subfloor over floor joists (6 m × 8 m = 48 m²)
items.body: Area 48 m², 10% waste → 52.8 m². Sheets = ceil(52.8 ÷ 2.9768) = 18 sheets. Weight: 594 kg. For a second-floor installation, factor this load into the floor joist design. Joist spacing of 400 mm increases stiffness versus 600 mm.

items.title: Example 3: Sloped roof deck (slope-adjusted area 25 m²)
items.body: Roof slope must be converted to actual surface area before entering. At 30° slope, actual = plan area ÷ cos(30°) ≈ plan × 1.155. Enter actual 25 m², 15% waste (complex cuts) → 28.75 m². Sheets = ceil(28.75 ÷ 2.9768) = 10. Weight: 330 kg. Use structural-grade plywood (KS F 3113) for roof decking.

faq.question: Why is the sheet listed as 1,220 × 2,440 mm when 4×8 ft should be 1,219 × 2,438 mm?
faq.answer: Nominally, 4 ft × 8 ft = 1,219.2 mm × 2,438.4 mm, but Korean distribution rounds this to 1,220 × 2,440 mm (sometimes sold as '4×8 자' referencing the Shaku unit). This tool uses the actual Korean market size of 1,220 × 2,440 mm = 2.9768 m² per sheet.

faq.question: When should I use 12 mm vs 18 mm plywood?
faq.answer: This page is for 18 mm only. For 12 mm, use the Plywood 12 mm Calculator. General guidelines: exterior wall sheathing 12–18 mm, subfloor 18–24 mm, roof deck 12–18 mm. Canada Wood Korea and NDS-based practice recommend 18 mm or thicker for structural sheathing on 600 mm stud spacing.

faq.question: How should I handle window and door openings?
faq.answer: Option 1 (precise): subtract opening area from total, then use 10–15% waste. Option 2 (practical): leave openings in and use a lower 5–8% waste factor, since cut-off pieces from openings are often reused elsewhere on site. Option 2 is more common in practice.

faq.question: What is the nail spacing based on?
faq.answer: IRC R602.3 (International Residential Code, structural sheathing fastening schedule): 6-inch (150 mm) edge spacing and 12-inch (300 mm) field spacing, assuming 600 mm stud spacing. Apply KDS 41 33 02 or your structural drawings if they specify different spacing.

faq.question: What is the difference between structural and non-structural plywood?
faq.answer: Structural plywood (KS F 3113) has tighter veneer defect limits and a waterproof adhesive bond meeting interior exposure standards. Use it for load-bearing sheathing, subfloors, and roof decking. Non-structural plywood is for cabinetry, non-load-bearing partitions, and furniture where moisture and load demands are low.

cautions: This tool covers 18 mm plywood only. For 12 mm, use the separate Plywood 12 mm Calculator.
For sloped roofs, enter the actual slope surface area, not the plan footprint.
Nail count is an estimate based on IRC R602.3 with assumed 600 mm stud spacing. Follow your structural drawings or KDS 41 33 02 if they specify otherwise.
Structural adequacy (load capacity, deflection) cannot be assessed by this tool. Permitted structures must be reviewed by a licensed structural engineer.
Total weight (33 kg/sheet) is a reference value for transport and lifting planning — use it to check vehicle payload and hoist capacity.

timber-osb.overview: The OSB Sheathing Calculator converts a wall or roof area (m²) into the number of standard 4×8 ft (1,220×2,440 mm = 2.9768 m²) 18 mm OSB (Oriented Strand Board) sheets required, the waste-adjusted order area, total weight (31 kg/sheet), and an IRC R602.3-based nail count estimate. OSB is the dominant structural sheathing panel in light-frame construction worldwide, priced lower than plywood while meeting equivalent strength and stiffness requirements for wall and roof diaphragms.
useCases: Wall sheathing sheet count for a light-frame timber house exterior
Roof deck OSB quantity including slope-area correction
Material budget for container house, barn, or addition project
Quantity estimate for replacing damaged sheathing panels
Weight comparison against 18 mm plywood (31 kg vs 33 kg/sheet) to choose between materials

howTo.title: How to Use (3 Steps)
steps: Enter the area (m²). For exterior walls, use total wall perimeter × wall height. For roofs, convert plan footprint to actual slope area (plan area ÷ cos(pitch angle)) before entering.
Set the waste factor (%). OSB tends to crumble slightly at cut edges compared to plywood, so the default 10% is recommended. Use 15% for roofs with hip ridges, valleys, or many openings.
Review results: sheet count, adjusted area (m²), total weight (kg), and estimated nail count. Uncheck 'Estimate Fasteners' to skip the nail count if you plan to use screws or an adhesive system.

formula.title: Formulas & References
formula.body:
■ Sheet Specification
  OSB 18 mm: 1,220 mm × 2,440 mm = 2.9768 m² / sheet, 31 kg / sheet
  (vs Plywood 18 mm at 33 kg/sheet — OSB is ≈ 2 kg lighter per sheet)

■ Waste-Adjusted Area
  A_adj = area (m²) × (1 + waste % / 100)
  Default waste: 10% (max 30%)

■ Sheet Count (ceiling)
  N = ceil(A_adj ÷ 2.9768)

■ Total Weight
  W = N × 31 kg

■ Nail Count Estimate (per IRC R602.3, same as plywood)
  Edge nails: perimeter (mm) ÷ 150 mm + 4 end-point correction
  Field nails: 1 interior row × (2440 ÷ 300 + 1) ≈ 9 nails
    (stud spacing 600 mm assumed → interior rows = floor(1220 ÷ 600) − 1 = 1)
  Total nails = nails per sheet × sheet count

items.title: Example 1: Single-story house exterior walls (perimeter 32 m × height 2.7 m)
items.body: Area 32 × 2.7 = 86.4 m². 10% waste → 95.04 m². Sheets = ceil(95.04 ÷ 2.9768) = 32. Weight: 32 × 31 = 992 kg. Nails ≈ 32 × 59 = 1,888. Order 2,000 nails (a little extra for rim and header nailing).

items.title: Example 2: Gable roof deck (30° pitch, plan area 72 m²)
items.body: Slope area = 72 ÷ cos(30°) ≈ 83.1 m². 10% waste → 91.4 m². Sheets = ceil(91.4 ÷ 2.9768) = 31. Weight: 961 kg. If your drawings specify 11 mm OSB for roof deck, use the OSB 11 mm Calculator instead.

items.title: Example 3: Storage shed addition, 3 walls (4 m × 2.5 m each = 30 m²)
items.body: Area 30 m², 10% waste → 33 m². Sheets = ceil(33 ÷ 2.9768) = 12. Weight: 372 kg. Nails ≈ 708. For a low-budget shed with no lateral-load requirement, consider OSB 11 mm — but verify minimum sheathing thickness requirements in your local code or Canada Wood Korea detail.

faq.question: What is the difference between OSB and plywood?
faq.answer: OSB (Oriented Strand Board) is engineered wood made by compressing wood strands in alternating cross directions under adhesive and heat. Plywood uses thin peeled veneers cross-laminated. Structural performance (stiffness, shear strength) is comparable, but OSB is typically 10–20% cheaper. Plywood edges are slightly more resistant to moisture swelling. Both are accepted as structural sheathing in light-frame codes (NDS, IRC, KDS 41 33 02).

faq.question: When should I use 11 mm vs 18 mm OSB?
faq.answer: This page is for 18 mm only. For 11 mm, use the separate OSB 11 mm Calculator. General guidance: minimum exterior wall sheathing thickness at 600 mm stud spacing is typically 11–12 mm; roof decking and floors with heavier loads use 18 mm. Verify with your structural drawings or Canada Wood Korea standard details.

faq.question: Is OSB suitable for exterior walls in wet climates?
faq.answer: Structural OSB rated Exposure 1 or Exposure 2 withstands limited, short-term moisture exposure during construction. For long-term exterior use, OSB wall sheathing must be covered with a Weather-Resistant Barrier (WRB / housewrap) immediately. Prolonged direct rain exposure causes OSB edges to swell, delaminate, and lose structural integrity.

faq.question: How do I convert roof pitch to slope area?
faq.answer: Slope area = plan area ÷ cos(pitch angle in degrees). Common conversions: 6/12 pitch (26.6°) → multiply plan area by 1.118; 8/12 (33.7°) → 1.202; 12/12 (45°) → 1.414. Hip roofs require calculating each face separately.

faq.question: Can I use screws instead of nails for OSB sheathing?
faq.answer: Structural sheathing standards (IRC R602.3, NDS) are based on nails (ring-shank or common) because nails provide better lateral shear capacity in dynamic loading (wind, seismic). Screws may be inferior in shear transfer for diaphragm applications. The nail count in this tool is based on IRC nailing schedules. Follow your structural engineer's fastener specification.

cautions: This tool covers 18 mm OSB only. For 11 mm OSB, use the separate OSB 11 mm Calculator.
For sloped roofs, always enter the actual slope surface area, not the plan footprint.
OSB edges crumble more than plywood when cut — plan for 10–15% waste on complex shapes.
Protect OSB from prolonged moisture exposure. Apply WRB (housewrap) immediately after sheathing is nailed. Do not leave OSB exposed to rain for more than a few days.
Structural adequacy (shear wall, diaphragm design) cannot be assessed by this tool. Permitted structures must be reviewed by a licensed structural engineer.

timber-drywall.overview: The drywall sheet calculator gives you sheet count, fastener (screw) count, and total weight instantly from wall or ceiling area plus a waste factor. It uses the Korean standard distribution size of 900×1800mm (1.62㎡/sheet) at 12.5mm thickness, and estimates screws with edge spacing 200mm and field spacing 300mm. Know your material quantities before pricing a job to avoid over-ordering.
useCases: New build or renovation interior wall and ceiling drywall sheet count
Pre-price material quantities and weight for interior fit-out quotes
Estimate screw quantity for site procurement
Adjust waste factor for walls with many openings (doors, windows)
Plan material delivery for high-ceiling commercial or office spaces

howTo.title: How to use (3 steps)
steps: Enter the installation area (m²) — you can combine wall and ceiling areas.
Set the waste factor (%). The default 10% suits straight walls; bump up to 15-20% for angled ceilings or many openings.
Click Calculate to see sheet count (rounded up), fastener count, and total weight with step-by-step workings.

formula.title: Formula & reference
formula.body:
Sheet spec: 900mm × 1800mm = 1.62㎡/sheet, 12.5mm thick, 16 kg/sheet

Adjusted area = installation area × (1 + waste% / 100)
Sheet count = ceil(adjusted area ÷ 1.62)
Total weight = sheets × 16 kg

Fastener estimate (per sheet):
  Edge screws: ceil(perimeter ÷ 200mm) + 4
  Field screws: interior stud rows (stud spacing 600mm) × ceil(sheet length ÷ 300mm)
  Total fasteners = per-sheet count × sheet count

Recommended waste: straight walls 10% / walls with many openings 15% / angled ceilings 20%

items.title: Example 1: Studio renovation (42㎡ walls)
items.body: Adjusted area = 42 × 1.10 = 46.2㎡. Sheets = ceil(46.2 ÷ 1.62) = 29 sheets. Weight = 29 × 16 = 464kg. Approx 580 screws — one 1,000-screw box covers it.

items.title: Example 2: Meeting room ceiling (24㎡, 15% waste)
items.body: Adjusted area = 27.6㎡. Sheets = ceil(27.6 ÷ 1.62) = 18 sheets. Weight 288kg. 15% used to absorb extra cutting at ceiling perimeter and light fittings.

items.title: Example 3: Full apartment interior walls (130㎡)
items.body: Adjusted area = 143㎡. Sheets = ceil(143 ÷ 1.62) = 89 sheets. Weight ~1,424kg — plan 2-3 elevator runs for delivery in multi-story buildings.

faq.question: Which size is standard — 900×1800mm or 1220×2440mm (4×8)?
faq.answer: 900×1800mm (1.62㎡) is the primary distribution size in Korea. North American standard is 4×8 ft (1220×2440mm, 2.976㎡). Always check the project specification. This calculator uses the Korean 900×1800mm size.

faq.question: How do I choose between 9.5mm and 12.5mm thickness?
faq.answer: 9.5mm (12kg/sheet) is used for lightweight partitions and ceilings. 12.5mm (16kg/sheet) suits load-bearing walls, fire-rated assemblies, and walls adjacent to wet areas. Regulatory fire-compartment requirements often specify 12.5mm minimum.

faq.question: Is 10% waste enough?
faq.answer: Yes for straight walls with few openings. Use 15% where doors and windows are frequent, and 20% for angled or irregular ceilings. Under-ordering risks a colour-lot mismatch if you reorder later.

faq.question: What spacing rules are used for fastener estimates?
faq.answer: Edge screws at 200mm intervals, field screws at 300mm intervals, stud spacing 600mm assumed. This gives roughly 20 screws per sheet; actual count varies with real stud layout.

faq.question: Why does the result include weight?
faq.answer: Weight matters for load assessments, elevator capacity planning, and selecting lightweight steel framing. At 89 sheets of 12.5mm drywall, you are moving about 1,400kg — typically 2-3 trips in a small elevator.

faq.question: Can I add wall and ceiling areas together?
faq.answer: Yes, but waste rates differ — walls typically 10%, ceilings 15-20%. For the most accurate result, calculate walls and ceilings separately and add the sheet counts.

cautions: Based on Korean 900×1800mm distribution size. Imported or non-standard sizes need separate conversion.
Fastener count assumes 600mm stud spacing — verify against actual framing layout on site.
Wet areas (bathrooms, kitchens) require moisture-resistant drywall (green board) or cement board, not standard gypsum.
Fire-rated assemblies require specific thickness and fire rating confirmation beyond this calculator.
Sheet count is always rounded up, so expect a small surplus.

timber-siding.overview: The siding calculator computes fiber-cement siding board count and total weight from exterior wall area and a waste factor. The reference spec is the Korean standard distribution size of 200×3600mm (0.72㎡/board, 9kg/board). A default waste of 10% covers cutting loss; increase to 15% when eave overhangs and window frames have not been deducted from the input area.
useCases: Timber-frame house exterior siding replacement or new installation
Pricing materials for rural or multi-unit residential exterior cladding
Optimise order quantity after deducting openings for windows and doors
Compare material quantities before requesting contractor quotes
Plan delivery logistics given the 3.6m board length and weight

howTo.title: How to use (3 steps)
steps: Enter the exterior wall area (m²). If you have already subtracted window and door openings, use a lower waste factor; if you input gross wall area, use 15-20%.
Set the waste factor (%). Default 10% applies when openings are already deducted. Use 15% for gross area input or lap-siding overlap.
Click Calculate to get board count (rounded up) and total weight with step-by-step workings.

formula.title: Formula & reference
formula.body:
Board spec: 200mm × 3600mm = 0.72㎡/board, 9 kg/board

Adjusted area = installation area × (1 + waste% / 100)
Board count = ceil(adjusted area ÷ 0.72)
Total weight = boards × 9 kg

Fiber-cement lap siding notes:
  - Typical lap overlap 30-50mm → exposed face ~150-170mm of 200mm width
  - Effective coverage per board ≈ 0.54-0.61㎡ when lapped
  - For lap installation, use waste factor 15%+ to account for overlap loss
  - Corner trim and J-channel material calculated separately

Recommended waste: straight wall (openings deducted) 10% / gross area input 15% / complex shapes 20%

items.title: Example 1: Single-storey house exterior (80㎡ net wall, 10% waste)
items.body: Adjusted area = 80 × 1.10 = 88㎡. Boards = ceil(88 ÷ 0.72) = 123 boards. Weight = 123 × 9 = 1,107kg. Plan one truck delivery.

items.title: Example 2: Lap installation with 30mm overlap (60㎡, 15% waste)
items.body: Effective coverage with overlap = ~0.612㎡/board. Adjusted area = 69㎡. Boards = ceil(69 ÷ 0.72) = 96 boards. 15% waste absorbs overlap and cutting loss.

items.title: Example 3: Multi-unit residential (200㎡ gross wall, 40㎡ openings)
items.body: Net wall = 160㎡. 10% waste → adjusted 176㎡. Boards = ceil(176 ÷ 0.72) = 245 boards. Weight 2,205kg — confirm heavy-lift equipment availability.

faq.question: What is the difference between fiber-cement and vinyl siding?
faq.answer: Fiber-cement (cement, sand, and cellulose fibre) is fire-resistant, durable, and can replicate a wood-grain finish. Vinyl (PVC) is lighter and cheaper but can deform in heat and fade over time. This calculator uses the fiber-cement spec.

faq.question: Does lap installation require more boards than vertical?
faq.answer: Yes. Horizontal lap siding overlaps 30-50mm per course, reducing effective coverage per board to about 0.60-0.66㎡ versus the nominal 0.72㎡. Expect to order 10-15% more boards than a straight-coverage calculation would suggest.

faq.question: Are corner trim and J-channel included?
faq.answer: No. This calculator covers siding board count only. Corner trim, J-channel, starter strip, and window flashing must be calculated separately.

faq.question: When should I use 10% vs 15% waste?
faq.answer: Use 10% if you have already subtracted opening areas from your input. Use 15-20% if you are entering gross wall area (openings not deducted), or if you are accounting for lap-siding overlap.

faq.question: Why does weight matter for siding?
faq.answer: Fiber-cement at 9kg/board is more than twice the weight of vinyl (4kg). At 100+ boards you exceed half a tonne, which affects vehicle selection and site handling. For upper-storey work, scaffold loading also needs checking.

faq.question: How do I measure exterior wall area?
faq.answer: Measure each wall face as width × height, then sum. Decide whether to subtract window and door areas — if not, raise the waste factor. For gable-end walls, treat the triangular section as average height × width.

cautions: Based on 200×3600mm fiber-cement siding spec. Other siding types or sizes need separate calculations.
Lap installation reduces effective board coverage — adjust waste factor upward to 15% or more.
Corner trim, J-channel, starter strip, and flashing are not included in this calculation.
Board weight is substantial; confirm vehicle capacity and site handling equipment before ordering.
House wrap (weather-resistant barrier) behind the siding is a separate material not covered here.

timber-tile.overview: The tile calculator outputs tile count, adhesive (kg), and grout (kg) in one step from installation area, tile size, grout joint width, and waste factor. The core formula computes each tile's footprint including the grout joint — (tile width + grout) × (tile height + grout) — then divides the waste-adjusted area by that footprint. Adhesive is fixed at 4kg/m². Grout consumption uses an empirical formula scaled by tile size and joint width.
useCases: Bathroom floor and wall tile count and adhesive quantity
Kitchen floor and backsplash tile materials estimate
Large-format living room or entryway tiles (600×600, 800×800)
Comparing waste factor across different tile sizes or joint widths
Pre-ordering adhesive and grout to avoid mid-project shortages

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Enter the installation area (m²) — you can combine bathroom floor and wall areas.
Choose a tile size preset (100, 200, 300, 400, 600, 800mm) or enter custom width and height.
Set the grout joint width (mm). Porcelain tiles typically use 2mm; ceramic and exterior tiles 3-5mm.
Click Calculate to get tile count (rounded up), adhesive kg, and grout kg with step-by-step workings.

formula.title: Formula & reference
formula.body:
Tile footprint (m²) = (tile width + grout joint, in m) × (tile height + grout joint, in m)
Adjusted area = installation area × (1 + waste% / 100)  ← default 10%
Tile count = ceil(adjusted area ÷ footprint)

Adhesive = installation area × 4 kg/m²

Grout empirical formula:
  rate (kg/m²) = grout_mm × 4 ÷ avg_tile_size_mm × 1.6
  Example: 300×300 tile, 2mm joint → 2×4÷300×1.6 ≈ 0.043 kg/m²
  Example: 100×100 tile, 3mm joint → 3×4÷100×1.6 = 0.192 kg/m²
Grout quantity = installation area × rate

Recommended waste: straight-lay floor 10% / diagonal (45°) 15-20% / complex shape 20%

items.title: Example 1: Bathroom floor 300×300mm (4㎡, 2mm grout)
items.body: Footprint = 0.302 × 0.302 = 0.09120㎡. Adjusted area = 4 × 1.10 = 4.4㎡. Count = ceil(4.4 ÷ 0.09120) = 49 tiles. Adhesive = 4 × 4 = 16kg. Grout ≈ 4 × 0.043 = 0.17kg.

items.title: Example 2: Living room floor 600×600mm (30㎡, 2mm grout)
items.body: Footprint = 0.602 × 0.602 = 0.36240㎡. Count = ceil(33 ÷ 0.36240) = 92 tiles. Adhesive = 30 × 4 = 120kg. Grout ≈ 30 × 0.021 = 0.64kg — large tiles consume very little grout.

items.title: Example 3: Kitchen mosaic backsplash 100×100mm (3㎡, 3mm grout)
items.body: Footprint = 0.103 × 0.103 = 0.01061㎡. Count = ceil(3.3 ÷ 0.01061) = 312 tiles. Adhesive 12kg. Grout ≈ 3 × 0.192 = 0.58kg — small tiles consume comparatively more grout.

faq.question: How do I choose a grout joint width?
faq.answer: Porcelain tiles (bathroom wall/floor) typically use 2mm joints. Ceramic and outdoor tiles use 3-5mm. Large-format tiles (600mm+) laid diagonally benefit from 3mm or wider for levelling. Narrower joints require more precise tile manufacturing tolerances.

faq.question: Is 10% waste enough?
faq.answer: Yes for rectangular rooms with straight-lay installation. Use 15-20% for diagonal (45°) installation, 15% for irregular bathroom shapes, and up to 20% for small mosaic tiles with heavy field cutting.

faq.question: What does the 4kg/m² adhesive rate assume?
faq.answer: It is the average for ceramic/porcelain tile adhesive based on KS L 1001 tile installation practice. Thick tiles (10mm+) or exterior installation may need 5kg/m²; thin interior tiles may use 3kg/m². The calculator uses 4kg/m² as the mid-range value.

faq.question: Is grout the same as cement grout?
faq.answer: Grout (joint filler) comes in cementitious and epoxy types. Wet areas like bathrooms and kitchens benefit from epoxy grout for water resistance. This calculator's grout kg estimate is based on cementitious grout density.

faq.question: How are cut tiles at room edges handled?
faq.answer: The waste factor includes edge-cut loss. For large tiles, edge cuts can represent a significant proportion of total tile area, which is why a higher waste factor matters more with large-format tiles.

faq.question: Can I calculate rectangular tiles (e.g. 300×600mm)?
faq.answer: Yes — enter width 300mm and height 600mm separately. The calculator handles non-square tiles using the full footprint formula.

cautions: Adhesive at 4kg/m² is an average. Confirm the manufacturer's coverage rate for your specific product.
Grout quantities are empirical estimates. Always check the manufacturer's specification for the actual product.
Diagonal (45°) or irregular-shape installation needs 15-20% waste factor.
Wet areas require waterproofing membrane application before tiling.
Large-format tiles (600mm+) require a flat substrate — verify floor flatness (±3mm per 2m) before starting.

timber-insulation-batt.overview: The insulation batt calculator gives roll/batt count and total weight from wall or ceiling area plus a waste factor. The reference spec is R-19 fiberglass batt at 600×2400mm (1.44㎡/batt, 5kg/batt), sized for 2×6 stud walls at 600mm spacing. The default 10% waste covers cutting around pipes, wiring, and openings. Mineral wool (rock wool) is dimensionally compatible but heavier — note that this calculator uses fiberglass weight.
useCases: R-19 batt count for 2×6 timber-frame exterior walls
Ceiling and attic insulation batt quantity and weight planning
Retrofit insulation replacement quantity for renovation projects
Checking area requirements for Korean Energy Code Annex 1 R-value compliance
Pre-ordering insulation to plan truck delivery by total weight

howTo.title: How to use (3 steps)
steps: Enter the installation area (m²) — full stud-bay wall area including stud framing is acceptable.
Set the waste factor (%). The default 10% covers pipe and wire penetrations and openings. Use 15% in conduit-heavy zones.
Click Calculate to get batt count (rounded up) and total weight with step-by-step workings.

formula.title: Formula & reference
formula.body:
Batt spec: 600mm × 2400mm = 1.44㎡/batt, R-19, 5 kg/batt
(Optimised for 2×6 stud walls at 600mm spacing)

Adjusted area = installation area × (1 + waste% / 100)
Batt count = ceil(adjusted area ÷ 1.44)
Total weight = batt count × 5 kg

R-19 thermal performance:
  RSI ≈ 3.35 m²·K/W  (1 R-unit = 0.176 m²·K/W)
  Check against Korean Energy Code (별표1) for your climate zone

Recommended waste: straight walls 10% / high pipe/wire density 15% / irregular shapes 20%

items.title: Example 1: Single-storey timber house exterior walls (90㎡, 10% waste)
items.body: Adjusted area = 90 × 1.10 = 99㎡. Batts = ceil(99 ÷ 1.44) = 69 batts. Weight = 69 × 5 = 345kg. Fits on a small truck.

items.title: Example 2: Ceiling with attic (60㎡, 15% waste)
items.body: Higher waste for recessed lighting and wiring penetrations. Adjusted area = 69㎡. Batts = ceil(69 ÷ 1.44) = 48 batts. Weight 240kg.

items.title: Example 3: Two-storey house all exterior walls (160㎡, 10% waste)
items.body: Adjusted area = 176㎡. Batts = ceil(176 ÷ 1.44) = 123 batts. Weight 615kg — single small truck delivery.

faq.question: What thermal performance does R-19 represent?
faq.answer: R-19 equals approximately RSI 3.35 (m²·K/W). Korea's Energy Code Annex 1 sets wall U-values by climate zone (e.g., 0.210 W/m²K for central zone 2 exterior walls). Whether R-19 batts alone meet the requirement depends on the full wall assembly — confirm with your designer.

faq.question: Can 600mm-wide batts be used in 400mm stud spacing walls?
faq.answer: R-19 batts at 600mm width are designed for 600mm stud spacing. For 400mm stud bays you need 400mm-wide batts (a separate product). Forcing a 600mm batt into a 400mm bay creates significant cutting waste, so raise the waste factor accordingly.

faq.question: What is the difference between fiberglass and mineral wool (rock wool)?
faq.answer: Fiberglass (glasswool) is lighter and easier to handle — this is the spec used here. Mineral wool (rock wool) has superior fire resistance and sound absorption but is 1.5-2× heavier. If you switch to mineral wool, the batt count stays the same but the weight figure will understate actual delivery weight.

faq.question: What does the 10% waste factor cover?
faq.answer: Cutting around pipe and cable penetrations, fitting around window and door openings, imperfect fill at joist ends, and breakage allowance. In zones with dense conduit runs or complex ceiling framing, use 15%.

faq.question: Is the vapour barrier (vapour retarder) included?
faq.answer: No. Vapour barrier (poly sheet) on the warm side of the insulation is a separate installation step and separate material. This calculator covers batts only. Vapour control is essential for condensation prevention in cold climates.

faq.question: What is the difference between a batt and a roll?
faq.answer: A batt is a pre-cut panel of fixed width and length — this calculator's reference format (600×2400mm). A roll is continuous insulation wound on a spool, commonly used for horizontal attic coverage. The calculator uses the batt (panel) format.

cautions: Based on R-19 600×2400mm fiberglass batt spec. Other R-values or sizes need separate calculations.
Compliance with Korean Energy Code Annex 1 requires full wall assembly assessment by a qualified designer.
Always wear safety glasses, dust mask, and gloves when handling fiberglass insulation — fine fibres are irritants.
Vapour barrier installation is not covered by this calculator and must be planned separately.
Ensure insulation fills stud bays without gaps to prevent thermal bridging.

timber-rafter.overview: The rafter length calculator uses the Pythagorean formula (√(run²+rise²)) to find the base rafter length from the horizontal span (run) and vertical height (rise), then adds the eave overhang corrected for slope (eave × sec θ) to give the full cut length. Angle, slope percentage, pitch-in-12, and area correction factor (sec θ = 1/cos θ) are shown simultaneously, making the results immediately useful for material ordering and roof area conversion.
useCases: Lumber ordering: multiply cut length by rafter count to calculate total board footage needed
Light-frame roof design: find accurate rafter length including eave overhang
Roof area conversion: use sec θ to convert horizontal projection area to actual sloped area
Cut angle reference: read upper plumb-cut and lower bird's-mouth cut angles directly from the result
Pitch notation conversion: see slope %, angle in degrees, and x/12 rise all at once

howTo.title: How to use (3 steps)
steps: Enter the horizontal run — the horizontal distance from the ridge to the top of the exterior wall plate. For a gable roof with a 6 m east-west span, the run for each side is 3 m (half the total width).
Enter the rise — the vertical height from the top plate to the ridge. For example, run 3,000 mm and rise 900 mm gives a 30% slope and a 3.6/12 pitch.

Enter the eave overhang length in mm if applicable. The calculator adds the slope-corrected eave diagonal (eave × sec θ) to give the total cut length. Leave it at 0 if you only need the base rafter length.

formula.title: Formula & basis
formula.body:
Base rafter length = √(run² + rise²)

Angle θ = atan(rise / run)
Slope (%) = (rise / run) × 100
Pitch (x/12) = (rise / run) × 12

Area correction factor (sec θ) = 1 / cos θ
  → multiply projected area by sec θ to get actual sloped area

Eave diagonal = eave overhang (mm) × sec θ
Total cut length = base rafter length + eave diagonal

Example: run=3,000 mm · rise=900 mm · eave=500 mm
  Base = √(9,000,000 + 810,000) = 3,132 mm
  θ = atan(0.30) = 16.7°,  sec θ = 1.044
  Eave diagonal = 500 × 1.044 = 522 mm
  Total cut length = 3,132 + 522 = 3,654 mm

items.title: Example 1: run 3,000 mm · rise 900 mm · eave 500 mm (30% slope)
items.body: Base rafter = √(3,000²+900²) = 3,132 mm. Angle 16.7°. sec θ = 1.044. Eave diagonal = 522 mm. Total cut length = 3,654 mm. Area correction 1.044 → multiply projected floor area by 1.044 to get actual roof area.

items.title: Example 2: run 4,000 mm · rise 2,000 mm · eave 600 mm (6/12 pitch)
items.body: Base rafter = √(16,000,000+4,000,000) = 4,472 mm. Angle 26.6°. sec θ = 1.118. Eave diagonal = 671 mm. Total cut length = 5,143 mm. At 6/12 the actual roof area is about 11.8% larger than the projected area.

items.title: Example 3: run 2,500 mm · rise 1,250 mm · no eave (45°)
items.body: Base rafter = √(6,250,000+1,562,500) = 2,795 mm. Angle 26.6°. With 0 mm eave, total cut length equals base length = 2,795 mm. Area correction factor 1.118. Useful when only the plain rafter length is needed.

faq.question: Where exactly is the 'run' measured?
faq.answer: The run is the horizontal distance from the ridge (peak) to the top of the exterior wall's top plate. For a symmetrical gable roof with a 6 m total width, each side has a run of 3 m. The eave overhang beyond the wall is entered separately.

faq.question: Where is the area correction factor (sec θ) used?
faq.answer: Multiply the horizontal projected area by sec θ to get the actual sloped roof area. For example, sec θ = 1.118 means 100 m² projected becomes 111.8 m² actual — the figure needed for shingle or tile quantity takeoffs, waterproofing, and solar panel layout.

faq.question: What does the x/12 pitch notation mean?
faq.answer: It is the North American light-frame convention: the number of inches of vertical rise per 12 inches of horizontal run. A 4/12 pitch rises 4 inches for every 12 horizontal inches. This calculator converts rise/run × 12 automatically.

faq.question: Why is the eave not simply added to the rafter length?
faq.answer: The eave overhang is measured horizontally, but the rafter runs along the slope. To convert a horizontal eave distance to a sloped rafter distance you must multiply by sec θ (= 1/cos θ). Example: 500 mm horizontal eave at 16.7° becomes 500 × 1.044 = 522 mm along the rafter.

faq.question: How do I use the cut angle in the workshop?
faq.answer: The angle result (°) is the cut angle for both ends of the rafter. Use it as the bevel setting on a circular saw or miter saw for the upper plumb cut (at the ridge) and the lower bird's-mouth seat cut (at the wall plate).

faq.question: Can this calculator find the structural span limit of a rafter?
faq.answer: No — this tool handles geometry only (length and angle). To check whether a rafter size can safely span a given distance under bending stress and deflection criteria, use the separate Span Calculator, which applies NDS allowable stress design.

cautions: This calculator covers rafter geometry only. Structural adequacy (allowable span, deflection, member size) requires the Span Calculator and review by a licensed structural engineer.
The run is measured to the top plate, not including the eave. Entering eave length in the run field will double-count the overhang.
Results assume a simple gable or shed roof with a single straight slope. For hip roofs or complex geometries, calculate each face separately.
In practice, rafter length may need a small adjustment (1–2 mm) to account for ridge board thickness at the peak.
Always have a licensed structural professional review calculations before construction.

timber-roof-pitch.overview: The roof pitch converter instantly converts between three pitch notations — degrees (°), slope percentage (%), and x/12 rise — in either direction. Enter any one value and the other two, the area correction factor (sec θ), and the pitch category (flat / low / medium / steep / extreme slope) are displayed immediately. It resolves the notation mismatches that arise when architects, framers, and roofers each use a different convention on the same project.
useCases: Drawing conversion: translate a drawing's '26.6°' into the framer's 6/12 or the roofer's 50% in one step
Material takeoff: use sec θ to convert projected floor area to actual roof area for shingle or tile quantities
On-site communication: resolve notation conflicts between designers (degrees), carpenters (x/12), and waterproofers (percentage)
Roofing material selection: confirm pitch category (low-slope vs. steep-slope) to choose the correct material and installation method
Light-frame education: understand the relationship between angle, slope ratio, and rise-per-foot

howTo.title: How to use (2 steps)
steps: Select the notation you already know: 'Angle (°)' for drawing or inclinometer readings, 'Slope (%)' for Korean construction drawings and civil engineering documents, or 'x/12 Rise' for North American light-frame notation.

Enter the value. The other two notations, area correction factor (sec θ), and pitch category appear instantly. Categories: flat (< 10°), low slope (10–30°), medium slope (30–45°), steep slope (45–60°), extreme slope (≥ 60°).

formula.title: Formula & basis
formula.body:
Angle (°) → slope % and x/12:
  Slope (%) = tan(θ) × 100
  x/12 rise = tan(θ) × 12
  Area correction factor (sec θ) = 1 / cos(θ)

Slope (%) → angle and x/12:
  θ = atan(slope / 100)
  x/12 = (slope / 100) × 12

x/12 rise → angle and slope:
  θ = atan(rise / 12)
  Slope (%) = (rise / 12) × 100

Pitch categories (Korean practice / AIK):
  Flat        : θ < 10°
  Low slope   : 10° ≤ θ < 30°
  Medium slope: 30° ≤ θ < 45°
  Steep slope : 45° ≤ θ < 60°
  Extreme     : θ ≥ 60°

Key reference values:
  4/12  ≈ 18.4° ≈ 33.3%  (sec 1.054)
  5/12  ≈ 22.6° ≈ 41.7%  (sec 1.083)
  6/12  = 26.6° = 50.0%  (sec 1.118)
  8/12  ≈ 33.7° ≈ 66.7%  (sec 1.202)
  12/12 = 45.0° = 100.0% (sec 1.414)

items.title: Example 1: Drawing shows '26.6°' — convert to construction notations
items.body: θ = 26.6°. Slope = tan(26.6°)×100 = 50.0%. x/12 = tan(26.6°)×12 = 6.0 → 6/12. sec θ = 1/cos(26.6°) = 1.118. Category: low slope. 100 m² projected → 111.8 m² actual roof area.

items.title: Example 2: Korean drawing '45%' — convert to degrees and x/12
items.body: Slope 45% → θ = atan(0.45) = 24.2°. x/12 = 0.45×12 = 5.4/12. sec θ = 1.098. Category: low slope. Sometimes written as '4.5-sun' in traditional Korean notation.

items.title: Example 3: 8/12 steep roof — area correction
items.body: 8/12 rise → θ = atan(8/12) = 33.7°. Slope = 66.7%. sec θ = 1.202. Category: medium slope. Roofing material requirement is 20.2% greater than the projected area; specialized safety equipment is recommended at this pitch.

faq.question: How does Korean 'sun (寸)' notation differ from x/12?
faq.answer: Korean 'x-sun' means x units of rise per 10 units of run (ratio x/10). North American x/12 means x inches of rise per 12 inches of run. Example: Korean 5-sun = 50% slope = atan(0.5) = 26.6° = 6.0/12. The 'Slope (%)' input mode in this calculator is equivalent to the x/10 Korean notation.

faq.question: Why does pitch category matter for roofing materials?
faq.answer: Each pitch range requires different waterproofing: below 10° (flat) needs membrane waterproofing (asphalt, urethane coating); 10–30° (low slope) suits TPO, metal standing seam, or fixed-panel systems; above 30° (medium/steep) drains fast enough for shingles, tiles, or metal panels.

faq.question: Why is the area correction factor always greater than 1?
faq.answer: At 0° the factor is sec(0°) = 1/cos(0°) = 1 (flat roof = projected area). As the angle increases, cos θ decreases so sec θ grows above 1. A factor of 1.118 at 26.6° means the sloped surface is 11.8% larger than what you see from directly above.

faq.question: What happens if I enter 0°?
faq.answer: 0° is a perfectly flat roof: slope 0%, x/12 = 0, sec θ = 1.000, category 'flat'. In practice even 'flat' roofs need a minimum 1–2% slope for drainage.

faq.question: Are pitches steeper than 45° used in residential construction?
faq.answer: Not commonly in Korea. Slopes above 45° (steep) appear mainly in European chalet-style or religious buildings. Above 60° (extreme), safe installation requires specialist equipment and rigging — it is rarely seen in standard residential work.

cautions: This tool covers pitch notation conversion and area correction factor only. Structural load, rafter sizing, and span limits require separate tools.
The x/12 rise input accepts values from 0 to 60 (angles up to 89.99°). Near-vertical values produce very large area correction factors and have no practical meaning in standard construction.
The area correction factor assumes a flat inclined plane. Curved surfaces (domes, barrel vaults) require a different approach.
Pitch categories follow Korean architectural practice and AIK guidelines; classification thresholds may differ by country or application.

timber-span.overview: The span calculator uses NDS 2018 (National Design Specification for Wood Construction) allowable stress design to find the maximum safe span for floor joists, ceiling joists, rafters, and headers. It checks both the bending stress limit (M = wL²/8, σ ≤ Fb) and the deflection limit (δ = 5wL⁴/384EI ≤ L/k) simultaneously, reports whichever governs, and applies the repetitive member factor (Cr = 1.15) automatically for closely spaced joists and rafters. Regional snow loads per KDS 41 10 15 are built in for Korean rafter design.
useCases: Floor joist design: check how far 2×8 or 2×10 SPF #2 can span for a 2 kN/m² residential live load
Rafter span check: factor in regional snow load to plan roof rafter spacing and size
Ceiling joist planning: size members for attic storage (0.5 kN/m² live load) vs. inaccessible attic
Header sizing: pre-check maximum span for door or window opening headers before framing
Species comparison: see how Douglas Fir or Larch spans compare with SPF for the same member size

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Select member type: floor joist, ceiling joist, rafter, or header. Default live load, dead load, and deflection limit (L/k) are set automatically based on member type.

Choose lumber size (2×4 through 2×12) and species/grade. SPF #2 is the most common imported species in Korean light-frame construction. Actual dressed dimensions per KS F 3020 (e.g., 2×8 = 38×184 mm) are applied automatically.

Enter spacing in mm. Standards are 400, 450, and 600 mm. Narrower spacing reduces the load per member and increases the allowable span. Spacing ≤ 600 mm with 3 or more members triggers the NDS repetitive member factor Cr = 1.15 automatically.
For rafters, select the snow region. Gangwon Yeongdong (east coast) and Ulleung Island carry 3.0 kN/m² vs. 0.5 kN/m² elsewhere (KDS 41 10 15), which substantially shortens the allowable rafter span.

formula.title: Formula & basis (NDS Allowable Stress Design)
formula.body:
1. Load assembly
   Total load (kN/m²) = live load + dead load + snow load (rafters only)
   Line load w (N/mm) = total load × (spacing mm / 1000)

2. Section properties — KS F 3020 actual dimensions
   A = b × d
   I = b × d³ / 12
   S = b × d² / 6
   (b = 38 mm thickness; d = width — load direction for joists/rafters)

3. Repetitive member factor (NDS Cr)
   Spacing ≤ 600 mm + floor/ceiling joist or rafter + ≥ 3 members
   → Fb_eff = Fb × 1.15

4. Bending check: L_b = √(8 × Fb_eff × S / w)
   (derived from M = wL²/8 ≤ Fb_eff × S)

5. Deflection check: L_d = ∛(384 × E × I / (5 × w × k))
   (from δ = 5wL⁴/384EI ≤ L/k)
   k = 360 (floor) · 240 (ceiling, header) · 180 (rafter)

6. Max span = min(L_b, L_d)

Reference values for SPF #2: Fb = 7.6 MPa, E = 9,500 MPa
Douglas Fir-Larch #2:       Fb = 12.4 MPa, E = 12,400 MPa

items.title: Example 1: 2×8 SPF #2 floor joist, 400 mm spacing
Example 2: 2×10 SPF #2 rafter, Seoul region, 600 mm spacing
items.body: Actual section 38×235 mm. Total load = 0 (live) + 0.5 (dead) + 0.5 (snow Seoul) = 1.0 kN/m². Line load = 1.0×0.6 = 0.6 N/mm. Cr = 1.15, Fb_eff = 8.74 MPa. I = 41,342,729 mm⁴. L_b = 4,985 mm. Deflection k = 180. L_d = ∛(384×9,500×41,342,729/(5×0.6×180)) = 5,801 mm. Max span 4,985 mm — bending governs.

items.title: Example 3: 2×6 SPF #2 ceiling joist, 600 mm spacing
items.body: Actual section 38×140 mm. Total load 0.8 kN/m² (live 0.5 + dead 0.3). Line load = 0.8×0.6 = 0.48 N/mm. Cr = 1.15, Fb_eff = 8.74 MPa. S = 124,600 mm³, I = 8,722,000 mm⁴. L_b = √(8×8.74×124,600/0.48) = 4,258 mm. Deflection k = 240. L_d = ∛(384×9,500×8,722,000/(5×0.48×240)) = 3,681 mm. Max span 3,681 mm — deflection governs.

faq.question: What does 'bending governs' vs. 'deflection governs' mean?
faq.answer: Bending governs when the member would reach its stress limit (Fb exceeded) at a shorter span than the deflection limit allows. Deflection governs when the floor or roof would sag beyond L/360 (or L/180 for rafters) before the wood reaches its bending stress limit. The smaller of the two span limits is always the controlling value.

faq.question: What does the repetitive member factor (Cr = 1.15) do?
faq.answer: When joists or rafters are spaced at 600 mm or less in groups of three or more, adjacent members share load when one is overloaded. NDS recognizes this load-sharing by allowing a 15% increase in the allowable bending stress (Fb). It does not apply to single members such as headers or posts.

faq.question: This tool uses NDS — does it apply in Korea?
faq.answer: The underlying design approach (allowable stress, Fb, E, deflection limits) closely parallels KDS 41 33 02. Snow loads use Korean regional values from KDS 41 10 15. Allowable stress values are from NDS for Canadian/US imported species (SPF, DF, Hem-Fir); for domestic Korean larch, consult KS F 3019.

faq.question: Which NDS adjustment factors does this tool NOT apply?
faq.answer: NDS includes CD (load duration), CM (wet service), Ct (temperature), CL (beam stability), CF (size factor), and Ci (incising). Only Cr (repetitive member) is applied here. Actual field conditions — green lumber, high humidity, sustained loads — can reduce the allowable span. A licensed structural engineer must account for these factors in final design.

faq.question: Why is the Gangwon Yeongdong snow load so much higher?
faq.answer: KDS 41 10 15 Appendix B sets the ground snow load at 3.0 kN/m² for the Gangwon east-coast region (Goseong, Sokcho, Gangneung, Samcheok) and Ulleung Island due to frequent heavy snowfall driven by cold air crossing the East Sea. This is six times the 0.5 kN/m² used for Seoul and most other regions, significantly shortening the allowable rafter span.

faq.question: Can I use this tool for engineered lumber (LVL, PSL, Glulam)?
faq.answer: No. Engineered wood products have proprietary allowable stress values that differ from sawn lumber and must be taken from the manufacturer's span tables or ICC evaluation reports. This calculator covers dimension lumber (2×4 through 2×12) in sawn species grades only.

cautions: Based on NDS 2018. Only the Cr repetitive member factor (1.15) is applied; CD, CM, Ct, CL, CF, and Ci are not. Results may be unconservative if lumber is wet, temperatures are extreme, or loads are sustained.
Shear and bearing (compression perpendicular to grain) are not checked. Short, heavily loaded headers may be shear-critical — a structural engineer must verify.

Default live loads (floor 2.0 kN/m², ceiling 0.5 kN/m²) are for standard residential occupancy. Warehouses, offices, and assembly areas require higher values per KDS 41 10 15 — enter them manually using the override fields.
Spacing outside 400/450/600 mm triggers a warning. The repetitive member factor (Cr = 1.15) is not applied when spacing exceeds 600 mm, which reduces the allowable span.
Results are for planning and preliminary review only. Final structural design and construction must be reviewed by a licensed structural engineer (Korean PE or equivalent).

timber-stairs.overview: The stair calculator computes step count, actual riser height, tread depth, stair length, angle, and Blondel value from total rise (floor-to-floor height) and preferred riser. It simultaneously checks the Korean Building Act Enforcement Decree Article 48 limits (residential: riser ≤ 200 mm, tread ≥ 240 mm; public: riser ≤ 180 mm, tread ≥ 280 mm) and the ergonomic Blondel formula (2R + T = 600–640 mm), displaying warnings when values fall outside safe ranges. Providing the optional total run enables measured tread instead of the Blondel-recommended default.
useCases: New timber-frame homes: draft stair layout (step count, riser, tread) once floor-to-floor height is fixed
Renovation: re-plan stair division when ceiling height changes
Compact housing / loft stairs: verify tread depth and pitch angle meet safety criteria in limited horizontal space
Pre-permit check: spot riser/tread violations before drawing submission
Deck and exterior stairs: calculate slope angle and stringer length for outdoor construction

howTo.title: How to Use (4 Steps)
steps: Enter total rise in mm — the floor-to-floor structural height including finish materials. Example: 2,700 mm between level 1 and level 2.
Enter preferred riser height. Residential stairs typically target 170–200 mm. The tool rounds to the nearest whole step count and divides evenly.
(Optional) Enter total run if you know the horizontal stair footprint. Leave blank to get the Blondel-formula tread recommendation (T = 620 − 2R).
Choose use type (Residential / Public). The building-code thresholds change accordingly. Review the Blondel pass/fail indicator and any riser or tread warnings in the results.

formula.title: Formulas & Standards
formula.body:
Step count = round(total rise ÷ preferred riser)  [min 2]
Actual riser (R) = total rise ÷ step count

Tread depth (T):
  With total run → T = total run ÷ (step count − 1)
  Without total run → T = 620 − 2 × R  (Blondel recommendation)

Stair length = √(total rise² + (T × (step count − 1))²)
Pitch angle = atan(R ÷ T)  [degrees]
Blondel value = 2R + T  → 600–640 mm is ergonomically safe

Korean Building Act Enforcement Decree Art. 48:
  Residential: R ≤ 200 mm, T ≥ 240 mm, width ≥ 750 mm
  Public: R ≤ 180 mm, T ≥ 280 mm, width ≥ 1,200 mm
  Headroom: ≥ 2,100 mm (both types)

items.title: Example 1: Total rise 2,700 mm, preferred riser 175 mm
items.body: Step count = round(2700 ÷ 175) = 15. Actual R = 2700 ÷ 15 = 180 mm. T = 620 − 2×180 = 260 mm (Blondel). Blondel = 2×180 + 260 = 620 mm → pass. Angle = atan(180÷260) = 34.7°. Stair length = √(2700² + (260×14)²) ≈ 4,534 mm.

items.title: Example 2: Total rise 3,000 mm, total run 3,640 mm
items.body: Preferred R 180 mm → step count = round(3000÷180) = 17. Actual R = 3000÷17 ≈ 176.5 mm. T = 3640÷(17−1) = 227.5 mm. Blondel = 2×176.5 + 227.5 = 580.5 mm → below 600 mm warning. Reduce step count or widen run to fix.

items.title: Example 3: Loft stair, total rise 2,000 mm (public threshold applied)
items.body: Public: R ≤ 180 mm. round(2000÷175) = 11 steps. R = 2000÷11 ≈ 181.8 mm → exceeds 180 mm warning. Increasing to 12 steps: R = 166.7 mm, T = 620−2×166.7 = 286.6 mm → both limits met. Angle = 30.2°.

faq.question: What is the Blondel formula?
faq.answer: The Blondel formula (2R + T = 600–640 mm) was proposed by 17th-century French architect François Blondel to describe the ergonomic stride length on stairs. When this range is satisfied, climbing feels natural without overstretching or shuffling. This calculator uses 620 mm as the target and warns when the value falls outside 600–640 mm.

faq.question: Why does the actual riser differ from the preferred value I entered?
faq.answer: The tool rounds total rise ÷ preferred riser to the nearest integer to ensure every step is equal height. The actual riser (total rise ÷ rounded step count) will therefore differ slightly from your preferred value. Always verify the displayed 'actual riser' before drafting construction drawings.

faq.question: Why is the tread auto-calculated when I leave total run blank?
faq.answer: Without a measured run, the Blondel formula T = 620 − 2R provides a recommended starting tread depth. This is useful in early design when only the floor-to-floor height is known. Enter the actual total run once the stair footprint is fixed to get precise tread dimensions.

faq.question: Where are the Korean building-code stair dimensions specified?
faq.answer: Building Act Enforcement Decree Article 48 and the Rules on Evacuation and Fire-Protection Structures Article 15 set stair dimensional requirements in Korea. This tool applies: residential (within dwelling unit) R ≤ 200 mm / T ≥ 240 mm; public (common areas / public buildings) R ≤ 180 mm / T ≥ 280 mm. Always verify current local authority requirements before permit submission.

faq.question: How do I design stair width (clear width)?
faq.answer: Korean code minimums: residential ≥ 750 mm, public ≥ 1,200 mm clear width. This calculator covers riser, tread, and step count only — stair width design should be confirmed with a licensed architect. Handrail requirements (height ≥ 900 mm) should also be reviewed.

faq.question: Can this calculator be used for outdoor stairs?
faq.answer: The geometry formulas apply equally to indoor and outdoor stairs, but outdoor stairs benefit from wider treads (to shed rainwater) and anti-slip finishes. In freeze-thaw climates, keeping risers at 150 mm or below reduces ice-accumulation risk. Structural footings for exterior stair stringers are a separate design consideration.

cautions: This tool references Korean Building Act Enforcement Decree Art. 48 as a guide. Final design and permit approval must be confirmed with a licensed architect and the local authority having jurisdiction.
Stair width (clear width), headroom (≥ 2,100 mm), and handrail requirements are not calculated here.
Total rise must reflect finished-floor-to-finished-floor height, including floor finish and stair tread thickness. Structural shell height will differ.
Step count is determined by rounding, so actual riser may differ from the preferred value. Use the displayed actual riser for all drawings.
Cutting a stair opening through floor joists requires double headers and trimmer/rim joists — consult a structural engineer.

timber-studs.overview: The stud framing calculator outputs 2×4 SPF stud count, single stud length, double top plate total length, sole plate total length, header count, and nail count from wall length, ceiling height, stud spacing (16" or 24" OC), and opening count. It adds 4 extra studs per opening (2 jack + 2 king) on top of the base layout (ceil(wall length ÷ spacing) + 1), then applies a configurable waste factor (default 10%) to give a ready-to-order quantity.
useCases: New timber-frame construction: calculate 2×4 stud order quantity for each wall line
Addition and renovation: determine extra studs and plates for new partition walls
Opening layout: quantify jack, king, and header lumber for windows and doors
Budget planning: fix stud count before pricing labor and material deliveries
Site management: tune waste factor to minimize over-ordering on the job

howTo.title: How to Use (5 Steps)
steps: Enter wall length in metres — total linear length of all walls combined. Example: 10 + 8 + 10 + 8 = 36 m for a rectangular floor plan.
