Enter ceiling height in mm (default 2,400 mm). Single stud length = ceiling height − 3 × 38 mm (double top plate + sole plate = 114 mm).
Select stud spacing: 16" (406 mm) OC for load-bearing and exterior walls; 24" (610 mm) OC for non-load-bearing interior partitions.
Enter the number of openings (windows and doors). Each opening adds 2 jack studs + 2 king studs = 4 studs.
Adjust the waste factor (default 10%) and click Calculate. Use 15% for complex framing or first-time builders.

formula.title: Formulas & Standards
formula.body:
Base stud count = ceil(wall length(mm) ÷ spacing(mm)) + 1  [includes both end studs]
Opening extra studs = openings × 4  [2 jack + 2 king per opening]
With waste = ceil((base + opening extra) × (1 + waste%/100))

Single stud length = ceiling height − 38 mm × 3  [2 top plates + 1 sole plate]
  Example: 2,400 mm ceiling → 2,400 − 114 = 2,286 mm per stud

Double top plate total length = wall length × 2
Sole plate total length = wall length

Header count = openings × 2  [double 2×10 header per opening]
Nail count = stud count × 8  [4 toenails per end, 2 ends]

Spacing reference (IRC practice, adopted by Korean timber-frame industry):
  16" OC = 406 mm — load-bearing and exterior walls
  24" OC = 610 mm — non-load-bearing interior partitions

items.title: Example 1: 10 m wall, 2,400 mm ceiling, 16" spacing, 2 openings
items.body: Base studs = ceil(10,000÷406)+1 = 26. Opening extra = 2×4 = 8. Subtotal 34 × 1.10 → ceil = 38 studs. Length = 2,400−114 = 2,286 mm each. Top plates = 20 m, sole plate = 10 m. Headers = 4. Nails = 38×8 = 304.

items.title: Example 2: 36 m perimeter, 2,700 mm ceiling, 24" spacing, 6 openings
items.body: Base = ceil(36,000÷610)+1 = 60. Opening extra = 24. Subtotal 84 × 1.10 = 93 studs. Stud length = 2,700−114 = 2,586 mm. Top plates = 72 m, sole plate = 36 m. Headers = 12.

items.title: Example 3: Interior partition 5 m, 2,400 mm ceiling, 16" spacing, 1 door
items.body: Base = ceil(5,000÷406)+1 = 14. Opening extra = 4. Subtotal 18 × 1.10 = 20 studs. Top plates = 10 m, sole plate = 5 m. Headers = 2.

faq.question: How do I choose between 16" (406 mm) and 24" (610 mm) spacing?
faq.answer: 16" OC is the standard for exterior walls and any wall that carries vertical load from floors or roof above. 24" OC is acceptable for non-load-bearing interior partitions and saves material cost. In high snow-load or seismic zones, verify the spacing through a structural calculation — do not assume 24" is permissible without engineering review.

faq.question: What are jack studs and king studs?
faq.answer: King studs (also called full studs) run the full height of the wall on each side of an opening. Jack studs (trimmer studs) are shorter members that sit inside the king studs and directly support the header beam above the opening. Each opening therefore requires 2 king studs + 2 jack studs = 4 additional studs beyond the regular layout.

faq.question: Why are there three plates (double top + single sole)?
faq.answer: The sole plate anchors the bottom of studs to the floor. The first top plate ties the stud tops together; the second (double) top plate laps over wall intersections and corners to create a continuous structural tie and to carry roof rafters or upper-floor joists. Each plate is a 2×4 (38 mm thick), so three plates total 114 mm deducted from ceiling height to give net stud length.

faq.question: Why does 2×4 lumber actually measure 38×89 mm?
faq.answer: Nominal dimensions describe rough-sawn size before kiln drying and planing. After processing, a nominal 2×4" (50.8×101.6 mm) becomes 1.5"×3.5" (38×89 mm) actual. Always use actual dimensions in design calculations — nominal sizes are only for ordering at the lumber yard.

faq.question: What does the 10% waste factor cover?
faq.answer: It accounts for off-cuts when studs are trimmed to exact stud length, pieces ruined by splitting or mis-cuts, and small damage during delivery. Simple straight walls: 8–10%. Complex framing or first-time builders: 12–15%. Experienced crews on clean layouts can sometimes target 5–8%. Adjust the field in this calculator to match your job.

faq.question: Is there a Korean code standard for stud spacing?
faq.answer: The Korean Building Act and KS standards do not currently prescribe explicit stud-spacing rules for light timber framing. Korean timber-frame practice borrows from the US IRC (International Residential Code) Chapter 6 and NDS (National Design Specification), which is also the basis used by the Korean Wood Construction Association guidance.

cautions: This calculator assumes simple straight walls. Corners (L-corner: 3 studs), wall intersections (T-wall: extra channel studs), and wall-to-ceiling blocking require additional studs not included here.
Structural load analysis (snow, wind, seismic) is outside the scope of this tool. Multi-story builds or high-hazard zones require an engineer-stamped structural calculation.
Header size scales with opening width. This tool assumes 2-ply 2×10 headers; openings wider than ~3 m may need engineered lumber or a structural steel header.
Stud length is net length after plate deduction. On-site, re-measure actual floor-to-ceiling dimension and adjust for any out-of-level conditions before cutting.
Fire-resistance, air-sealing, and acoustic requirements may mandate specific stud spacing or additional blocking — check applicable codes.

timber-material-quantity.overview: The material quantity calculator determines sheets (or bundles/batts) required, waste-adjusted area, total weight, and fastener count for 11 common timber-frame materials: OSB (11 mm, 18 mm), plywood (12 mm, 18 mm), gypsum board (9.5 mm, 12.5 mm), cement board, fiber-cement siding, vinyl siding, asphalt shingles, and R-19 glass-wool batt insulation. Standard sheet sizes shipped in the Korean market are built in (1,220×2,440 mm for OSB/plywood; 900×1,800 mm for gypsum/cement board), and fastener estimates follow IRC R602.3 nail-spacing rules.
useCases: Roof sheathing: compute OSB sheet count plus nail quantity for structural deck
Interior finish: calculate gypsum-board sheets and screw count for walls and ceilings
Exterior cladding: determine fiber-cement or vinyl siding linear pieces for façade
Roofing: find asphalt shingle bundle count from actual roof area
Insulation: count R-19 glass-wool batts for 2×6 stud wall cavities

howTo.title: How to Use (4 Steps)
steps: Select the material type: structural (OSB, plywood), interior finish (gypsum board), exterior cladding (cement board, siding), roofing (shingles), or insulation (glass-wool batt).

Enter the installation area in m². For walls, multiply width × height × number of faces. For roofs, enter the actual sloped area — use the Roof Area Calculator first if you only know the plan footprint.
Adjust the waste factor (default 10%). Simple rectangles: 5–10%; many cut-outs or diagonal patterns: 15–20%.
Click Calculate to see sheet count (ceiling-rounded), adjusted area, total weight, and fastener count (nail or screw) based on standard edge/field spacing.

formula.title: Formulas & Standards
formula.body:
Adjusted area = installation area × (1 + waste%/100)
Sheets required = ceil(adjusted area ÷ area per sheet)
Total weight = sheets × weight per sheet (kg)

Built-in sheet sizes (Korean market standard):
  OSB / Plywood: 1,220 × 2,440 mm = 2.9768 m²/sheet (19–33 kg)
  Gypsum board: 900 × 1,800 mm = 1.62 m²/sheet (12–16 kg)
  Cement board: 900 × 1,800 mm = 1.62 m²/sheet (22 kg)
  Fiber-cement siding: 200 × 3,600 mm = 0.72 m²/piece (9 kg)
  Vinyl siding: 250 × 3,700 mm = 0.93 m²/piece (4 kg)
  Asphalt shingle: 1 bundle = 3.1 m² (33 sq. ft., 27 kg)
  Glass-wool R-19 batt: 600 × 2,400 mm = 1.44 m²/batt (5 kg)

Fastener spacing (IRC R602.3):
  Structural sheathing (OSB/plywood): edge 150 mm OC / field 300 mm OC → nails
  Gypsum board: edge 200 mm OC / field 300 mm OC → screws

items.title: Example 1: OSB 11 mm roof sheathing, 80 m² area, 10% waste
items.body: Adjusted area = 80 × 1.10 = 88 m². Sheets = ceil(88 ÷ 2.9768) = ceil(29.6) = 30 sheets. Total weight = 30 × 19 kg = 570 kg. Nails estimated from perimeter ÷ 150 mm + interior rows × (2,440 ÷ 300). Plan a 570 kg cargo delivery.

items.title: Example 2: Gypsum board 12.5 mm, 45 m² walls, 15% waste
items.body: Adjusted area = 45 × 1.15 = 51.75 m². Sheets = ceil(51.75 ÷ 1.62) = 32 sheets. Weight = 32 × 16 = 512 kg. Screws estimated from edge 200 mm + field 300 mm spacing per sheet.

items.title: Example 3: Asphalt shingles, actual roof area 120 m², 12% waste
items.body: Adjusted area = 120 × 1.12 = 134.4 m². Bundles = ceil(134.4 ÷ 3.1) = 44 bundles. Weight = 44 × 27 = 1,188 kg. Shingles are excluded from fastener estimation — refer to manufacturer's nailing schedule.

faq.question: What is the difference between OSB and plywood?
faq.answer: OSB (Oriented Strand Board) is manufactured by compressing wood strands in alternating directions under heat and adhesive. Plywood uses thin veneer layers cross-laminated with adhesive. OSB is typically less expensive with uniform structural strength; plywood has a smoother face, slightly better moisture edge resistance, and is preferred for finish applications. Both are standard for roof and wall sheathing in timber-frame construction.

faq.question: Why is one asphalt shingle bundle equal to 3.1 m²?
faq.answer: In US roofing convention, 1 'square' covers 100 sq. ft. (9.29 m²) of roof. Most shingle products use 3 bundles per square, so 1 bundle = 33.3 sq. ft. ≈ 3.1 m². Coverage accounts for the overlap (exposure) required between shingle courses. This calculator uses 3.1 m²/bundle as the built-in coverage constant.

faq.question: What wall type is R-19 glass-wool batt designed for?
faq.answer: R-19 (thermal resistance roughly 3.35 m²·K/W) is sized for 2×6 stud wall cavities (140 mm deep). For 2×4 walls (89 mm cavity), R-11 or R-13 batts are the typical match. Korean energy regulations (Building Energy-Saving Design Standards) specify minimum U-values by climate zone — verify the required insulation thickness separately with those tables.

faq.question: How should I set the waste factor?
faq.answer: Simple rectangular wall or roof panels: 10% is a reasonable default for the standard 1,220×2,440 mm sheet format. Many windows/doors or non-rectangular shapes: 15–20%. Diagonal siding patterns or multi-faceted roofs: up to 25%. Over-ordering raises cost, so refine the factor as you gain on-site experience with a particular material.

faq.question: Why does total weight matter for ordering?
faq.answer: Weight determines vehicle payload (1-tonne pickup vs. 5-tonne flatbed), hoist or crane capacity for multi-story lifting, and temporary floor loading on scaffolding. Gypsum board and cement board (16–22 kg per sheet) are particularly heavy and typically require two workers per sheet plus plan for upper-floor deliveries.

faq.question: Fiber-cement siding vs. vinyl siding — which should I choose?
faq.answer: Fiber-cement is durable (20–30+ year lifespan), fire-resistant, and paintable, but heavier (9 kg per piece) and labor-intensive to install. Vinyl siding is lightweight (4 kg), low-cost, and easy to install, but has lower fire resistance and can distort under extreme heat. In Korea's climate with wide seasonal temperature swings, allow adequate expansion joints in vinyl siding to prevent buckling.

cautions: Sheet count is the minimum order quantity after rounding up. Add buffer for site-specific complexity beyond the stated waste factor.
Fastener estimates use IRC R602.3 standard spacing. Seismic or high-wind zones may require closer nail schedules or structural clips — check local requirements.
Asphalt shingles, vinyl siding, fiber-cement siding, and glass-wool batts are excluded from fastener estimation. Follow the manufacturer's installation instructions for nailing patterns.
Weight values are dry-condition references. Use code-prescribed unit weights (Korean Building Structural Standard KBC) for structural load calculations — do not use the tool weights for that purpose.
Gypsum board thickness selection for fire-rated assemblies (e.g., 9.5 mm vs. 12.5 mm for 30-minute or 60-minute ratings) depends on the fire-resistance design. Confirm with your architect.

deposit-risk.overview: An 'underwater jeonse' (깡통전세) is a lease where the deposit plus the landlord's senior debt approaches or exceeds the property's value — so if the home is auctioned, the tenant can't get the deposit back. This calculator turns that risk into numbers. It computes the jeonse ratio (your deposit ÷ market price) and the debt ratio ((senior mortgages + your deposit) ÷ market price), then simulates the worst case: how much you'd actually recover at a foreclosure auction, and how much you could lose. Everything runs in your browser — no data is sent anywhere.
useCases: Deciding whether a specific jeonse or high-deposit wolse listing is financially safe
Seeing how a landlord's existing mortgage (근저당) eats into what you'd recover
Stress-testing a deposit against a lower auction price before you commit
Foreigners assessing risk on their first Korean lease
Checking if a home is near the ~80% deposit-to-value danger line before viewing

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Enter the property's market price — use real transaction data (실거래가), not the asking price.
Enter the senior debt: mortgage amounts (근저당 설정액) from the property register's 을구 section, which are repaid before you at auction.
Enter your deposit, then set an assumed auction recovery rate (default 80%) — lower it to be conservative.
Read the debt ratio and risk band, plus the estimated recovery and loss if the home were auctioned.

formula.title: How the risk is measured
formula.body:
Jeonse ratio = your deposit ÷ market price
Debt ratio  = (senior debt + your deposit) ÷ market price   ← the key metric

Worst-case (auction) simulation:
  Expected auction proceeds = market price × recovery rate
  You recover = max(0, proceeds − senior debt)     (senior creditors are paid first)
  Estimated loss = max(0, your deposit − you recover)

Rule-of-thumb bands (industry / HUG usage, NOT law):
  ≤ 70%  relatively safe
  70–80% caution
  80–90% risky
  > 90%  very risky (classic underwater jeonse)

items.title: Borderline case — ₩300M home
items.body: Market price ₩300M, senior mortgage ₩50M, your deposit ₩200M. Debt ratio = (50 + 200) ÷ 300 = 83% → 'risky'. At an 80% auction (₩240M), senior debt takes ₩50M first, leaving ₩190M — ₩10M short of your ₩200M deposit. You'd likely lose about ₩10M without deposit insurance.

items.title: Safe case — no senior debt
items.body: Market price ₩400M, no mortgage, your deposit ₩200M. Debt ratio = 50% → 'safe'. Even at an 80% auction (₩320M) you'd recover the full ₩200M. Still verify the register on balance-payment day — a new mortgage can appear between signing and move-in.

faq.question: What counts as 'senior debt'?
faq.answer: Anything repaid before your deposit at a foreclosure auction — most commonly the landlord's mortgage (근저당권) shown in the 을구 section of the property register, and unpaid taxes with an earlier statutory date. A deposit registered later ranks behind these.

faq.question: Why does the auction recovery rate matter so much?
faq.answer: Foreclosed homes rarely sell for full market value. The winning bid (낙찰가율) varies widely by property and market — often 70–90% of appraised value. Because senior debt is paid from that reduced amount first, a lower recovery rate can wipe out a deposit that looked safe on the debt ratio alone. Simulate conservatively.

faq.question: Is the 80% line an official rule?
faq.answer: No. The 70–80% deposit-to-value and ~90% debt-ratio lines are widely-used rules of thumb from HUG guidance and industry practice, not statutory limits. HUG/SGI deposit-insurance eligibility uses its own criteria that change over time — confirm current requirements directly with them.

faq.question: The tool says 'safe' — am I protected?
faq.answer: It's only a screen based on the numbers you enter. It does not check the actual register, the landlord's taxes, trust registration (신탁), or whether move-in reports are allowed. Treat a 'safe' result as 'worth proceeding to full due diligence', not as a guarantee.

faq.question: How do I lower my risk if the ratio is high?
faq.answer: Prefer wolse or ban-jeonse (smaller deposit), require the landlord to clear the mortgage before the balance payment, get HUG/SGI deposit-return insurance, and complete your move-in report + fixed date immediately. See the full jeonse-scam checklist linked below.

cautions: Reference and educational tool, not legal, tax, or investment advice — verify the property register (등기부등본), HUG eligibility, and consult a licensed agent and lawyer before signing.
The 70/80/90% bands are industry rules of thumb, not law; your real risk depends on facts this tool cannot see.
Auction recovery rate is an assumption you set — real foreclosure outcomes vary widely by property.
Market price should come from actual transaction data, not the asking price, or the result is misleading.
A 'safe' score is a starting screen, not a guarantee — always complete full due diligence.

cost-of-living.overview: This is a budgeting tool, not a claim about what Korea 'costs'. Pick a region and household and it prefills typical monthly lines — rent, utilities, food, transport, mobile, health insurance, and other spending — with representative 2026 mid-range estimates. Then you edit every line to your own situation, and the total is simply the sum of your numbers, with annual and per-person figures. It's most useful for planning a move or comparing cities before you commit to a lease. Everything runs in your browser.
useCases: Planning a move to Korea and sizing how much to save first
Comparing central Seoul against Gyeonggi or a smaller city
Turning a job offer's salary into a realistic monthly surplus
Students and single expats setting a lean baseline budget
Families estimating the jump in rent, food, and utilities for 3–4 people

howTo.title: How to use (3 steps)
steps: Pick your region and household — the seven lines fill with typical mid-range estimates.
Edit each line to your reality: your actual rent, your phone plan, whether you cook or eat out.
Read the monthly total, the annualized figure, and the per-person cost. Adjust and compare regions freely.

formula.title: How the estimate is built
formula.body:
Monthly total = rent + utilities + food + transport + mobile + health insurance + other
Annual = monthly × 12
Per person = monthly ÷ household size (single 1, couple 2, family 4)

The region/household selection only prefills editable defaults. Rent scales by region and by household (more people → more space); the other lines scale by household size. Nothing here is fixed — the total always reflects the numbers you leave in the fields.

items.title: Single, central Seoul
items.body: Prefilled to roughly ₩2.5M/month: rent ₩1.3M, utilities ₩130k, food ₩500k, transport ₩80k, mobile ₩40k, health insurance ₩150k, other ₩300k. That sits between a lean (~₩1.5–2M) and comfortable (~₩3–4M) lifestyle — move the lines to match yours.

items.title: Single, smaller city
items.body: The same profile outside the capital starts near ₩1.6–1.8M, mostly because rent drops to about ₩450k. Food, mobile, and insurance barely change with location — so region mainly moves the rent line.

faq.question: Are these numbers official?
faq.answer: No. They're representative mid-points of commonly-cited ranges for 2026 (aggregated public and private cost data), meant as a starting point. Real costs vary widely by lifestyle and contract, which is exactly why every line is editable and the total only reflects your inputs.

faq.question: Why is there no US dollar figure?
faq.answer: Exchange rates move daily, so a fixed USD number would quickly become misleading. The tool shows Korean won only; convert with a live rate when you need dollars.

faq.question: How is health insurance estimated?
faq.answer: The default is a rough monthly figure. If you're employed, National Health Insurance is a percentage of your salary (about 8.135% in 2026, split with the employer); if you're a local subscriber it's based on income and assets. Replace the default with your own once you know your scheme.

faq.question: Does 'rent' mean jeonse or monthly rent?
faq.answer: Monthly rent (wolse). Jeonse replaces most monthly rent with a large lump-sum deposit instead, so its monthly cash cost is different — use the jeonse tools to compare that model.

faq.question: Does this include one-time move-in costs?
faq.answer: No — it's a recurring monthly budget. Deposits (key money), agent fees, furniture, and visa/relocation costs are separate one-time amounts you should budget on top.

cautions: Estimates only — representative mid-range values for 2026, not a guarantee or an official figure.
Region and household just prefill defaults; the total always equals the numbers you leave in the fields.
Rent and health insurance are the most variable lines — replace them with your real figures first.
One-time costs (deposit, agent fee, furniture, flights) are not included.
Confirm actual amounts with real listings, bills, and your insurance scheme before relying on the total.

remittance.overview: Sending money out of Korea costs more than the fee on the receipt. The bigger, hidden cost is usually the FX margin — the gap between the mid-market exchange rate and the rate your provider actually applies. This tool adds both together so you can see the true effective cost of a transfer and compare methods fairly. It doesn't use live rates or name providers; you enter the margin and fee, and it shows the total cost, effective rate, and value delivered. It also summarizes Korea's outbound-remittance rules so you know what documentation to expect.
useCases: Foreign workers sending part of their salary home each month
Comparing a bank transfer against a specialist service on true cost, not just the fee
Seeing how much a 'low fee, bad rate' option really costs on a large transfer
Understanding why small transfers cost proportionally more (fixed fee weight)
Checking what reporting or proof a larger remittance from Korea may need

howTo.title: How to use (3 steps)
steps: Pick a method — it prefills a representative margin and fixed fee that you should overwrite with real figures.
Enter the amount you want to send, then your provider's actual FX margin (spread) and fixed fee.
Read the total cost, the effective cost rate, and the value delivered. Change the numbers to compare options.

formula.title: How the cost is measured
formula.body:
FX margin cost = amount × margin %
Total cost     = FX margin cost + fixed fee
Effective rate = total cost ÷ amount
Value delivered = amount − total cost   (in KRW-value terms)

To find a provider's real margin, compare their quoted rate to the mid-market rate (e.g. from a neutral source) on the same day: margin % = (mid − applied) ÷ mid × 100. A '0 fee' offer can still be expensive if the margin is wide.

items.title: ₩1,000,000 via a bank
items.body: At a 2.5% margin + ₩25,000 fixed fee: FX cost ₩25,000 + fee ₩25,000 = ₩50,000 total, an effective 5.0%. Only about ₩950,000 of value reaches the recipient.

items.title: Same amount via a specialist
items.body: At a 0.7% margin + ₩5,000 fee: ₩7,000 + ₩5,000 = ₩12,000, an effective 1.2%. The gap is almost entirely the hidden margin, not the visible fee — which is why comparing fees alone is misleading.

faq.question: What is the FX margin (spread)?
faq.answer: It's the difference between the mid-market rate (the 'real' rate you see on a neutral source) and the rate your provider gives you. Providers earn on this gap, so a transfer advertised as 'no fee' can still be costly. Always compare the applied rate to the mid-market rate.

faq.question: Are there limits on sending money out of Korea?
faq.answer: Yes. Under the Foreign Exchange Transactions rules, banks apply per-transaction and annual thresholds above which extra documentation and reporting to the Bank of Korea / tax authority apply. The exact figures change over time — confirm the current thresholds with your bank before a large transfer.

faq.question: As a foreign worker, can I send my salary home?
faq.answer: Generally yes, but banks typically ask for proof — your employment, income, and that tax was paid (e.g. withholding receipt). Requirements vary by bank and amount; ask your bank what documents they need for salary remittances.

faq.question: Does sending or receiving money trigger tax?
faq.answer: The transfer itself isn't income, but large gifts can be subject to gift tax, and cross-border transfers may be reported to the National Tax Service. If money is a gift or business income rather than your own funds moving, check the tax treatment with a professional.

faq.question: Why does a small transfer cost a higher percentage?
faq.answer: Because the fixed fee is a bigger share of a small amount. ₩5,000 on ₩200,000 is 2.5% before any margin; the same fee on ₩5,000,000 is 0.1%. For small, frequent transfers the fixed fee matters most; for large ones, the margin dominates.

cautions: Reference and educational tool — not financial advice and not a live quote. The real cost is set by the rate and fee at the moment you send.
No live exchange rates or specific provider fees are used; margin and fee are assumptions you enter.
Method choice only prefills representative ranges — it does not recommend or rank any provider.
Remittance limits, reporting thresholds, and required proof change over time — confirm current rules with your bank and the Bank of Korea.
Large gifts or cross-border transfers may have tax and reporting consequences — consult a professional.

income-tax.overview: The Korean income tax calculator applies the 8-bracket progressive rate (6% / 15% / 24% / 35% / 38% / 40% / 42% / 45%) as of 2026 to compute calculated tax, final tax, and local income tax from a given taxable base. It supports the wage earner credit (Income Tax Act §59) and shows the effective rate alongside the current bracket. All inputs stay in the browser — nothing is sent to a server.
useCases: Freelancers and sole proprietors: simulate the May income tax filing
Employees: estimate refund or additional payment after year-end settlement
Landlords: see when adding rental income pushes you into a higher bracket
Investors: compare separate vs comprehensive taxation on dividends and interest
Year-end planning: estimate tax reduction from pension or IRP contributions

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Enter your taxable base — gross income minus deductions (personal, pension, special). Employees can read this directly from the year-end settlement document.
If you are an employee, check 'Apply wage earner credit'. The calculator applies the Income Tax Act §59 credit (up to KRW 740,000) automatically.
Click Calculate. You will see the marginal rate, progressive deduction, calculated tax, wage earner credit, final tax, 10% local income tax, and the effective rate.
The 8-bracket table highlights your current bracket. Adjust the taxable base to see how close you are to the next bracket boundary.

formula.title: Formula (Korean Income Tax Act §55)
formula.body:
Calculated tax = Taxable base × Marginal rate − Progressive deduction

2026 brackets:
  up to KRW 14M       : 6%   (deduction 0)
  up to KRW 50M       : 15%  (deduction 1,260,000)
  up to KRW 88M       : 24%  (deduction 5,760,000)
  up to KRW 150M      : 35%  (deduction 15,440,000)
  up to KRW 300M      : 38%  (deduction 19,940,000)
  up to KRW 500M      : 40%  (deduction 25,940,000)
  up to KRW 1B        : 42%  (deduction 35,940,000)
  over KRW 1B         : 45%  (deduction 65,940,000)

Final tax = Calculated tax − tax credits (wage earner, child, etc.)
Local income tax = Final tax × 10%
Total tax = Final + Local

items.title: Example 1: Employee, gross KRW 50M (taxable ~KRW 30M)
items.body: Bracket: 15%. Calculated = 30M × 0.15 − 1.26M = KRW 3.24M. After wage earner credit (~KRW 1.78M) and local tax (~KRW 146K), total roughly KRW 1.6M.

items.title: Example 2: Freelancer, taxable KRW 80M
items.body: Bracket: 24%. Calculated = 80M × 0.24 − 5.76M = KRW 13.44M. No wage earner credit. Plus local 10% = KRW 14.78M total.

items.title: Example 3: Crossing into 35% bracket at KRW 90M
items.body: At KRW 88M you are still at 24%. Adding KRW 2M of income jumps you to 35%: 90M × 0.35 − 15.44M = KRW 16.06M, a jump of about KRW 2.5M for the extra KRW 2M earned. Plan year-end deductions to avoid crossing.

faq.question: When is the Korean income tax filing period?
faq.answer: May 1 to May 31 each year. Sincere-filing-confirmation taxpayers get an extension to June 30. Payment is due in the same window, with optional installment to 2 months later.

faq.question: Do employees need to file?
faq.answer: If you only have wage income, the year-end settlement handles it. If you have side income, freelance income, rental income, or financial income above KRW 20M, you must file in May.

faq.question: What is the progressive deduction?
faq.answer: A shortcut for the staircase tax formula. Instead of computing tax bracket by bracket, you multiply the entire base by the top marginal rate and subtract the progressive deduction for that bracket — the result is identical.

faq.question: Where do I file local income tax?
faq.answer: Local income tax is 10% of the final tax and is filed alongside income tax via Hometax. A separate payment notice is issued and paid to your city or district office.

faq.question: What is the wage earner credit cap?
faq.answer: Up to KRW 740K for gross income below KRW 33M; the cap decreases for higher incomes (KRW 660K / 500K / 200K floor at high incomes). 55% credit on calculated tax up to KRW 1.3M, then 30%, capped.

faq.question: Does this calculator handle child or pension credits?
faq.answer: Currently only the wage earner credit is applied. Child credit (KRW 150K per child), pension savings credit (up to 16.5%), and medical/donation credits must be subtracted manually from the final tax.

faq.question: Who are sincere-filing-confirmation taxpayers?
faq.answer: Business owners exceeding a revenue threshold (e.g., KRW 1.5B for wholesale/retail, KRW 750M for restaurants) must attach a tax accountant's confirmation and file by June 30. Missing this triggers a penalty.

cautions: This is a simplified simulation focused on the main calculation. Real filings include detailed credits for children, donations, medical expenses, etc.
Separate-taxation items (financial income under KRW 20M, daily-wage income, etc.) are excluded from the taxable base.
Sincere-filing-confirmation taxpayers have a June 30 deadline.
Results are for reference. Verify with Hometax's 'Tax Filing Assistant' or a tax accountant.
Rates and caps may change with future law revisions. Current as of January 2026.

checklist.title: Year-end settlement & income-tax checklist for foreigners

groups.heading: When & who (year-end settlement)
items: Year-end tax settlement (연말정산) is done in February for the previous calendar year's wage & salary income.
It applies to employees — your employer reconciles the tax already withheld against your final liability.
Finalized tax minus tax already withheld = a refund (if negative) or an extra payment (if positive).
Use the NTS year-end auto-calculation service to estimate your result (not available for non-residents); see the foreigner guides on nts.go.kr/english.

groups.heading: 19% flat tax vs progressive — choosing
items: Foreign employees may choose each year: global taxation at progressive rates, or a flat 19% on gross wage & salary income.
Elect the flat rate by submitting the flat-tax-rate application during settlement.
Critical trade-off: choosing the 19% flat rate means NO exemptions, deductions, reductions, or credits apply at all.
Rule of thumb: high income with few deductions → flat 19% often wins; lower income with many deductions → progressive often wins (verify with the NTS calculator).
Flat-rate eligibility is limited to a set number of years from your first Korean employment start date — confirm you are still in the window (verify).

groups.heading: Deductions — available vs restricted
items: Under progressive taxation, residents can claim personal, medical, and credit-card deductions; non-residents (generally under 183 days) get only the personal deduction and the wage-tax credit.
The credit-card deduction applies only on spending above 25% of your gross annual wage.
A spouse or dependent earning more than ₩1,000,000 in the year is not eligible for the basic deduction.
Some housing-related deductions are restricted or unavailable to foreigners — verify each against the NTS foreigner guide (verify).

groups.heading: Documents, tax treaty & special cases
items: Gather: the wage & salary withholding receipt, the income-deduction/tax-credit report, and receipts for medical expenses, donations, etc.
Teachers/professors (often E-1/E-2) from treaty countries may be exempt on teaching/research income for a set period — but some countries grant none (verify your country's treaty).
To claim treaty exemption, submit the tax-treaty non-taxation application through your employer to the tax office.
Early departure: if you leave Korea permanently before year-end, complete a final settlement before departure rather than waiting for February (verify the procedure).
A comprehensive (global) income tax return is filed in May if you are a freelancer, have multiple income sources, or have income beyond a single employer's wages.

foreign-flat-tax.overview: Foreign workers in Korea can elect a 19% flat tax (20.9% including the 1.9% local income tax) on earned income instead of the regular progressive rates of 6–45%, under Article 18-2 of the Special Tax Treatment Control Act. This calculator compares both methods side by side from your gross salary, applying the earned-income deduction, personal deduction, and wage-earner credit on the progressive side. It reveals the real breakeven — which, contrary to the common '80 million won' claim, sits around 130–170 million won depending on your deductions. All inputs stay in your browser.
useCases: Foreign professionals (E-7 and other work visas) deciding whether to elect the flat tax at year-end settlement
High earners checking whether the 19% flat rate beats progressive taxation
Families comparing — each dependent shifts the breakeven upward
New arrivals confirming eligibility before the 2026-12-31 sunset
HR and payroll teams advising foreign employees

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Enter your annual gross salary (before tax, including bonuses).
Enter the number of dependents excluding yourself (spouse, children). Each adds a KRW 1.5M personal deduction on the progressive side.
Click Compare. You will see total tax under each method, the effective rate, and how much the cheaper option saves per year.
Review the progressive breakdown (taxable base, earned-income deduction, wage-earner credit) to understand why one method wins.

formula.title: Formula (Special Tax Act §18-2 vs Income Tax Act §55)
formula.body:
Flat tax = Gross salary × 19% + local 1.9% = Gross × 20.9%
  (all deductions and credits waived)

Progressive (resident basis):
  Earned-income deduction (§47, capped at KRW 20M)
  Personal deduction = KRW 1.5M × (1 + dependents)
  Taxable base = Gross − earned-income deduction − personal deduction
  Calculated tax = base × bracket rate − progressive deduction
  − Wage-earner credit (§59)
  + Local income tax 10%

Better method = whichever total is lower.

Breakeven (single filer): around KRW 137M. More dependents and more special deductions push it higher.

items.title: Mid income — gross KRW 50M
items.body: Flat: 50M × 20.9% = KRW 10.45M. Progressive: taxable base ~KRW 36.25M after deductions → about KRW 3.87M total. Progressive wins by a wide margin (~KRW 6.6M). At mid incomes the flat rate's loss of all deductions makes it far more expensive.

items.title: Breakeven — gross KRW 140M
items.body: Flat: KRW 29.26M. Progressive: about KRW 29.91M. Flat wins by only ~KRW 650K. This is the real crossover point for a single filer — far above the '80 million won' figure repeated in many guides.

items.title: High income — gross KRW 200M
items.body: Flat: KRW 41.8M. Progressive: about KRW 53.8M. Flat saves ~KRW 12M because it caps your rate at 20.9% while progressive reaches the 38% bracket.

faq.question: Who can use the 19% flat tax?
faq.answer: Foreign workers (not Korean nationals) on earned income, electing under Special Tax Act §18-2. Employees of special-related companies are excluded. It is available whether you are a tax resident or non-resident.

faq.question: Is the rate really 19%?
faq.answer: The income tax is 19%, plus a local income tax of 1.9% (10% of the income tax), for an effective 20.9%. This calculator shows both lines.

faq.question: Until when can I start using it?
faq.answer: You must begin your first employment in Korea on or before 2026-12-31. Once you qualify, the flat tax applies for 20 years from your first work day (unless the law is extended).

faq.question: Why is the breakeven so high (around KRW 130–170M)?
faq.answer: The progressive method allows the earned-income deduction, personal deductions, and the wage-earner credit, which keep effective rates low. So the flat tax only wins at high salaries with few deductions — much higher than the commonly cited KRW 80M.

faq.question: What do I give up by electing the flat tax?
faq.answer: All exemptions, deductions, and credits are waived, and even normally non-taxable allowances become taxable. If you have large deductions (medical, education, mortgage, pension), progressive is usually better.

faq.question: How do I elect the flat tax?
faq.answer: Submit a flat-tax application to your employer at year-end settlement, or to the district tax office when filing your return. You may switch your election each year.

faq.question: Does this include the 4 social insurances?
faq.answer: No. This compares income tax plus local income tax only — not national pension, health, employment, or industrial-accident insurance. It is not your net take-home pay.

cautions: Excludes the 4 social insurances and special credits (medical, education, donations, card spending) — more deductions favor progressive and raise the breakeven.
On the flat-tax side, employer-paid insurance and normally non-taxable allowances become taxable; this simplified model does not add them, so real flat-tax burden can be higher.
Resident basis. Non-residents face restricted deductions and different results.
Flat-tax new entry sunsets on 2026-12-31 unless extended by law.
Reference estimate only. Confirm with the NTS Hometax or a tax professional.

checklist.title: NHIS enrollment & usage checklist for foreigners

groups.heading: Mandatory enrollment & eligibility
items: Any foreigner who has stayed 6+ months is auto-enrolled as a local (self-employed) subscriber the day after the 6-month mark — mandatory since 16 Jul 2019.
Covered statuses include D-2, D-4, E-1–E-10, H-1, H-2, F-1–F-4, G-1, and overseas Koreans.
Use the NHIS 'expected local-subscriber enrollment date' simulator on nhis.or.kr to estimate your date in advance.
You can apply for exemption only with proof of equivalent coverage under a foreign law, private insurance, or employer contract.
Handle all foreigner matters through the NHIS Center for Foreign Residents (☎ 1577-1000, English support).

groups.heading: Employee vs local subscriber & premiums
items: If you work for an insured company you are 'employee insured' — premium is wage × 7.19% (2026), split 50/50 with your employer; this overrides local status.
If not employed you are 'local insured' — premium is based on household income, property, and vehicles.
D-2/D-4 students and F-4 overseas-Korean students get a local-premium reduction (commonly cited as 50% — verify the current rate).
Set up auto-debit (CMS) — local premiums are due by the 25th of the preceding month.
Extra reductions exist for remote islands/areas (up to 50%) and farming/fishing villages (22%).

groups.heading: Non-payment consequences (critical)
items: Unpaid premiums suspend ALL benefits from the arrears date until paid in full — you pay full price at hospitals.
Arrears can block your visa extension and other immigration procedures — clear them before any appointment.
Auto-debit is the safest way to protect both your benefits and your immigration standing.
Check your balance and payment history on the NHIS website or mobile app to catch arrears early.

groups.heading: Card, dependents & hospital use
items: Request your insurance card / certificate through the NHIS Center for Foreign Residents.
Register dependents with an ARC plus an apostilled family-relationship document (dated within 9 months) and a notarized Korean translation.
Go to a primary clinic (1차) first; you need a referral (의뢰서) to use a tertiary general hospital with coverage.
Co-payment rates differ by institution level (clinic vs general vs tertiary) and inpatient/outpatient.
NHIS does not cover non-covered (비급여) items — consider private indemnity (실손) insurance for those gaps.

foreign-health-insurance.overview: If you live in Korea for six months or more, enrollment in the National Health Insurance Service (NHIS) is mandatory. Employees pay based on salary (7.19% split with the employer, plus long-term care), while the self-employed, unemployed, and students pay under a foreigner rule: the higher of income/asset scoring or the average premium — about ₩158,640 per month in 2026. Students on a D-2 or D-4 visa get a 50% reduction. This calculator estimates your monthly and annual premium by enrollment type. All inputs stay in your browser.
useCases: New arrivals estimating their premium before the 6-month mandatory enrollment
Employees checking the health + long-term care deduction on their payslip
Self-employed and freelancers budgeting for the regional premium
International students confirming the 50% reduced rate
Anyone comparing employee vs self-employed enrollment cost

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Choose your enrollment type: employee (working at a company) or self-employed/student.
If employed, enter your monthly salary. The calculator applies your 3.595% share plus 13.14% long-term care.
If self-employed, check the student box if you hold a D-2/D-4 visa and meet the income/asset limits for the 50% reduction.
Click Calculate to see your monthly and annual premium, with the employer's matching share shown for reference.

formula.title: Formula (NHIS 2026)
formula.body:
Employee (your share):
  Health = monthly salary × 3.595% (capped between ₩10,080 and ₩4,591,740)
  Long-term care = Health × 13.14%
  Monthly = Health + Long-term care
  (employer pays the same amount)

Self-employed (foreigner rule):
  Monthly = max(income/asset score, average premium ₩158,640)
  Student (D-2/D-4) = ₩158,640 × 50% = ₩79,320

items.title: Employee earning ₩3M/month
items.body: Health = 3M × 3.595% = ₩107,850. Long-term care = ₩14,171. Monthly = about ₩122,021 (your share); the employer pays the same amount on top.

items.title: Self-employed foreigner
items.body: Most pay the average premium — ₩158,640/month (₩1,903,680/year) — even with low reported income, because of the foreigner floor rule.

items.title: International student (D-2)
items.body: With the 50% reduction, ₩79,320/month, provided annual income is ₩3.6M or less and assets ₩135M or less.

faq.question: Do I have to join NHIS as a foreigner?
faq.answer: Yes. After six months in Korea you are automatically enrolled as a regional subscriber. Employees join immediately through their company, and students (D-2/D-4) join upon foreign registration.

faq.question: Why is my premium fixed even though I earn little?
faq.answer: Foreign regional subscribers pay at least the average premium (about ₩158,640 in 2026). Income/asset scoring only raises it above that floor — it cannot go below.

faq.question: How much is the student discount?
faq.answer: 50% off the average premium, about ₩79,320/month, if your annual income is ₩3.6M or less and assets are ₩135M or less.

faq.question: What happens if I don't pay?
faq.answer: Unpaid premiums can block your visa extension — immigration checks tax and NHIS arrears. Late fees and asset seizure are also possible.

faq.question: Is long-term care insurance included?
faq.answer: For employees, yes — it is 13.14% of the health premium, shown separately. The regional average premium already reflects the total payment.

faq.question: Does this include income/asset scoring for the self-employed?
faq.answer: No. It uses the average-premium floor that most foreigners actually pay. High-income or high-asset foreigners may pay more.

faq.question: Can I be exempted?
faq.answer: If you have equivalent coverage through foreign insurance or an employer contract, you can apply for an exemption with NHIS.

cautions: Regional income/asset scoring is not modeled — the calculator uses the average-premium floor most foreigners pay. High earners or asset holders may pay more.
The average premium (~₩158,640) is set yearly by NHIS each November — this is a 2026 estimate, verify annually.
Employee figures apply the health-premium cap; very high or low salaries differ from a simple percentage.
Reduction eligibility depends on individual income/asset review — confirm with NHIS (1577-1000).
Reference estimate only; official assessment follows NHIS rules.

groups.heading: Area terms — don't get fooled by price-per-pyeong
items: Distinguish 전용면적 (exclusive area — your actual living space) from 공급면적 (supply area — exclusive + shared hallways, stairs, lobby).
Korean listings usually advertise the larger 공급면적, so the unit feels smaller than the headline number — always ask which figure is quoted.
For officetels, watch for 계약면적 (contract area), which adds parking and facility areas on top, inflating the size further.
Compute price-per-pyeong (평당가) on a consistent area basis — comparing one unit's 전용 to another's 공급 is misleading.
Cross-check the stated area against the free 건축물대장 (building register) from gov.kr (1 pyeong ≈ 3.3㎡).

groups.heading: Documents to verify before paying anything
items: Pull a fresh 등기부등본 (property register) yourself from IROS (iros.go.kr, ~₩1,000) — don't trust the landlord's copy.
Confirm the registered owner matches the landlord's ID and the contract; review the 을구 section for mortgages/liens (근저당권).
Get the free 건축물대장 from gov.kr to confirm legal use and catch any illegal extension (위반건축물).
Check 실거래가 (reported transaction prices) on rt.molit.go.kr to judge whether the price or deposit is fair.
Red flags: an unusually cheap price, a 'friend/agent' signing instead of the owner, or a deposit sent to a third-party account.

groups.heading: Physical inspection during the viewing
items: Check the orientation (방향) — 남향 (south-facing) gets the most sunlight; confirm which way the main windows face.
Inspect for 결로 (condensation) and 곰팡이 (mold) on window frames, low walls, behind furniture, and in the bathroom.
Test 수압 (water pressure) on taps and the shower, especially in older or high-rise buildings.
Check the floor, daylight (채광) at the time you'd actually be home, heating (보일러) in every room, and the hallway/elevator.
Document existing defects (하자) with photos and have them noted in the contract before move-in.

groups.heading: Costs, move-in & practical confirmations
items: Get a written breakdown of 관리비 (maintenance fee, ~₩70,000–250,000/month) and what it includes versus separate utilities.
Confirm parking (주차), the move-in date (입주 가능일), and a written list of built-in/option items (빌트인/옵션 — fridge, washer, A/C).
Photograph the gas and electricity meter readings at handover so you aren't charged for the previous occupant's usage.
Bring a Korean speaker or a translation app (Papago) — listings and contracts are usually Korean-only.

apartment-area.overview: A Korean property listing's headline area — say '84㎡' — is the supply area, which includes shared space like stairs and hallways. Your actual living space is the smaller exclusive area. This tool converts both to pyeong, shows the exclusive ratio, and computes price per pyeong on both bases. It decodes the exclusive-vs-supply trap that causes foreign renters and buyers to overestimate how much space they actually get.
useCases: Renters and buyers checking how much living space a listing really offers
Comparing apartments with different exclusive ratios at the same supply area
Calculating the true price per pyeong (exclusive basis) before negotiating
Understanding why an 84㎡ apartment 'reads' as 25 pyeong but lives like 18
Converting between ㎡ and pyeong for any Korean property document

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Enter the supply area in ㎡ — the figure in the listing (e.g. 84).
Enter the exclusive area in ㎡ — found on the deed or floor plan (e.g. 59).
Optionally enter the sale or jeonse price to get price per pyeong.
Click Calculate to see pyeong conversions, the exclusive ratio, and per-pyeong prices on both supply and exclusive bases.

formula.title: Formula
formula.body:
1 pyeong = 3.3058㎡ (1㎡ = 0.3025 pyeong)
Supply pyeong = supply ㎡ × 0.3025
Exclusive pyeong = exclusive ㎡ × 0.3025
Exclusive ratio = exclusive ÷ supply
Price per pyeong (supply) = price ÷ supply pyeong
Price per pyeong (exclusive) = price ÷ exclusive pyeong

items.title: National size — 84㎡ supply / 59㎡ exclusive
items.body: Supply 25.4 pyeong, exclusive 17.8 pyeong, exclusive ratio 70%. This 84/59 combo is Korea's most common apartment size, the 'national size' (국민평형).

items.title: Price per pyeong — ₩1B for the 84㎡
items.body: Supply basis ≈ ₩39.4M/pyeong; exclusive basis ≈ ₩56.0M/pyeong. Agents quote the lower supply figure, but you actually live in the exclusive area.

items.title: Officetel — 50㎡ supply / 28㎡ exclusive
items.body: Exclusive ratio just 56%. Officetels have far lower ratios than apartments, so the same supply area gives much less living space.

faq.question: What's the difference between supply and exclusive area?
faq.answer: Supply area = exclusive area + shared residential space (stairs, hallways, elevators). Exclusive area is the usable space inside your unit. Listings usually show supply area.

faq.question: What is a good exclusive ratio?
faq.answer: Apartments are typically 70–80%; officetels 50–60%. A higher ratio means more of the advertised area is actually yours to live in.

faq.question: Why is 84㎡ called 25 pyeong?
faq.answer: 84㎡ × 0.3025 = 25.4 pyeong (supply). The exclusive area of about 59㎡ ≈ 17.8 pyeong is what you live in. The 84/59 pairing is Korea's 'national size'.

faq.question: Which price per pyeong should I use?
faq.answer: Agents quote the supply basis, which looks cheaper. For honest value comparison, the exclusive basis is better because it reflects the space you actually use.

faq.question: Is there a contract area too?
faq.answer: Yes — contract area adds other shared space (underground parking, management offices). It is the largest figure and used mainly for officetels and commercial units.

faq.question: Does this match the deed?
faq.answer: The deed (등기부등본) lists the exact exclusive area. Always verify against the deed and contract, not just the listing.

cautions: Listings usually show supply area; the exclusive area on the deed is smaller — confirm both.
Officetels and some mixed-use buildings have much lower exclusive ratios than apartments.
Price per pyeong on a supply basis looks lower; the exclusive basis reflects true usable cost.
1 pyeong = 3.3058㎡ exactly; rounded figures in ads can differ slightly.
Reference only — verify actual areas on the deed and contract.

checklist.title: F-2-7 residence visa — application & document checklist

groups.heading: Confirm your eligibility category
items: Professionals (E-1–E-7 / D-5–D-9) with 3+ years of continuous lawful residence (the 3-year rule is waived if annual income exceeds ₩40M).
Study-talent: a Korean master's degree or higher, employed in an eligible role within 5 years of graduation.
Listed-company employees (KOSPI/KOSDAQ) — the 3-year residence rule is waived.
Promising-industry workers (IT, bio, nano, etc.) at or above the GNI income threshold (verify — the multiple changes yearly).
Baseline: reach 80 of 170 points and have 1+ year of legal stay before applying (verify the current threshold).

groups.heading: Gather point-evidence & screening documents
items: Income proof: income-amount certificate (소득금액증명) and/or withholding receipt (원천징수영수증).
Employment proof: work contract or certificate of employment; degree certificate (apostilled if issued abroad).
Korean-ability proof: a TOPIK certificate or KIIP (Social Integration Program) completion certificate.
Submit only the documents for the points you actually claimed — full documentation of every category is not required.
National criminal-background check with apostille (issued within 6 months), plus a TB screening if you are from a designated country (verify).

groups.heading: Core packet & filing
items: Integrated application form (통합신청서), passport, ARC, a 3.5×4.5 cm photo, and your completed F-2-7 score table.
Proof of residence: a lease, a residence-confirmation form, or a dormitory certificate.
Apply online via HiKorea (status change → F-2-7) or in person at the immigration office for your address; HiKorea is often 1–2 weeks faster.
Fee around ₩130,000 for a status change (verify on HiKorea before paying); processing ~4–8 weeks, longer in peak months.
Track status on HiKorea, then collect the new Residence Card in person; call 1345 for case-specific rules.

groups.heading: Maintain status & the F-5 path
items: At each extension, keep your score at 80+ and your income at or above the GNI threshold.
Hold F-2-7 continuously for 3 years to become eligible for F-5 permanent residence.
Spouse and children can hold dependent F-2 status and may work after approval (subject to your income meeting the condition — verify).
Keep updated income, tax, and residence records each year, and confirm current F-5 requirements separately (they change) (verify).

f2-residence-visa.overview: F-2-7 is Korea's points-based residence visa — a common step toward permanent residence (F-5). To apply you must (1) fall under one of five eligible categories, (2) meet core requirements (a 3-year continuous legal stay or ₩40M+ annual income, good conduct, public health), and (3) reach 80 of 170 points on a scoring test. This tool checks the categories and core requirements. It deliberately does NOT compute a point total, because the detailed scoring is an internal, unpublished Ministry of Justice manual that varies by source and changes without notice.
useCases: Professionals (E-7, etc.) on a 3+ year stay checking if they can move to F-2-7
Graduates of Korean master's programs exploring the residence track
Anyone mapping the path from work visa → F-2-7 → F-5 permanent residence
Confirming core requirements before booking a hikorea consultation
Understanding what the 80-point test actually weighs

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Pick the applicant category that matches you (listed-company employee, growth-industry worker, 3-year professional, domestic master's graduate, or STEM talent).
Check the core requirements: 3-year continuous legal stay or ₩40M+ income, good conduct, and the public-health requirement.
See whether your core requirements are met and what is still missing.
Then confirm your actual point total and eligibility at hikorea.go.kr or 1345 — this tool won't guess your score.

formula.title: How the 80-point test works (informational)
formula.body:
Pass mark: 80 of 170 points (130 core + up to 40 bonus).
Scored items: age, education level, Korean ability (TOPIK or KIIP), annual income, work/stay experience, plus bonuses (Korean degree, government recommendation, KIIP completion, volunteering) and deductions (immigration violations, criminal record).

⚠️ Exact sub-scores are NOT published — they live in an internal MOJ manual and differ between unofficial sources, so this tool does not calculate a number. Income carries the most weight. Verify your real score at hikorea.

items.title: Professional on a 3-year E-7
items.body: Meets the professional category and the 3-year requirement. Still needs 80 points (income and Korean ability usually decide it) plus good conduct. Check the score at hikorea.

items.title: Master's graduate from a Korean university
items.body: Qualifies under the study-talent category; the 3-year stay can be waived with ₩40M+ income. Education and Korean ability tend to score well.

items.title: High earner in a growth industry
items.body: The growth-industry category plus high income (the heaviest-weighted item) makes 80 points reachable — but the category itself requires income of at least 1.5× GNI.

faq.question: What is the F-2-7 visa?
faq.answer: A points-based residence visa for skilled foreigners with broad work and residence rights. After holding it for 3 years in good standing you can pursue points-based permanent residence (F-5).

faq.question: What score do I need?
faq.answer: 80 out of 170. But 80 alone isn't enough — you must also fit one of the eligible categories and pass the conduct and public-health checks.

faq.question: Why doesn't this tool calculate my points?
faq.answer: The detailed scoring table is an unpublished internal MOJ manual, and unofficial sources disagree on the numbers (especially income and education). Asserting a wrong score could mislead your application, so we check the verifiable requirements and send you to the official source for the score.

faq.question: Can the 3-year stay requirement be waived?
faq.answer: Yes — with ₩40M+ annual income, or via an approved STEM talent-attraction program with a government recommendation.

faq.question: Does student (D-2) or job-seeker (D-10) time count toward the 3 years?
faq.answer: For the professional category, prior D-2/D-10 stay generally does not count (per an official advisory). Korean-degree graduates instead apply under the study-talent category.

faq.question: What comes after F-2-7?
faq.answer: Hold it for 3+ years in good standing and you can apply for F-5 (permanent residence) on the points track.

cautions: Sub-scores are unofficial and change without notice — this is a requirements checklist, not a score calculator.
Meeting 80 points does not guarantee approval; category, stay/income, conduct, and health must all be met.
Eligible categories and income thresholds (GNI-linked) shift yearly.
Decisions are case-by-case at officer discretion.
Always verify at hikorea.go.kr or the Immigration Contact Center (1345).

groups.heading: Pick your D-8 track
items: D-8-1 (corporate investor): invest ₩100M+ in a Korean corporation and hold 10%+ of voting shares; grants up to 5 years.
D-8-3 (sole-proprietorship investor): invest ₩100M+ in a business with a Korean co-representative, holding 10%+ of capital; up to 5 years.
D-8-4 (technology startup): a founder with a degree plus intellectual property, via the OASIS program and a points test; 1 year, renewable.
Confirm a real, independent office — shared/virtual offices are not accepted for D-8-1/3 (a coworking desk can suffice for D-8-4).

groups.heading: D-8-1/3 — four-step incorporation
items: Step 1 — file a Foreign Investment Declaration (외국인투자신고) at a designated foreign-exchange bank or KOTRA.
Step 2 — remit ₩100M+ in foreign currency; the sender name must match the shareholder, with an MT103 SWIFT note 'sent on behalf of [investor]'.
Step 3 — complete corporation establishment registration (법인설립등기), then obtain the Business Registration Certificate (사업자등록증).
Step 4 — complete Foreign-Invested Company Registration (외국인투자기업 등록) at the bank/KOTRA.
Keep the full money trail: corporate bank-account history, proof of source of funds, and the ₩100M remittance record.

groups.heading: D-8-4 — technology-startup prerequisites (OASIS)
items: Hold a bachelor's degree or higher; a foreign degree must be apostilled.
Secure IP tied to the business — a patent, utility model, or design application/registration (at least a filing is mandatory).
Establish a NEW Korean corporation with you as founder/key executive in a technology field.
Enroll in OASIS and collect course-completion certificates toward your points total.
Reach the required points (cited as 60, though some sources say 80 — verify) and secure a real office or a leased coworking desk.

groups.heading: Documents, filing & the D-10-2 path
items: Prepare a detailed business plan (authenticity is closely reviewed), plus the application form, passport, photo, and fee.
D-8-1/3 add: investment proof, corporate registry extract, business registration certificate, and proof of fund use.
D-8-4 add: apostilled degree, OASIS certificate(s), IP filing certificate, points-calculation form, and a residence agreement.
Establish and register the company and pay in capital BEFORE applying; convert your entry to an ARC within 90 days of arrival.
Recommended route: enter on D-10-2 (startup-prep, up to 2 years), complete OASIS + file IP + register the company, then change to D-8-4.

d8-startup-visa.overview: The D-8 is Korea's business-investment visa for foreigners who found or invest in a Korean company. It splits into three common types: D-8-1 (set up or invest in a corporation), D-8-3 (invest in a Korean-run company), and D-8-4 (technology startup — found a new company with your own intellectual property). This tool checks the verifiable core requirements for each type. For D-8-4 it deliberately does NOT compute an OASIS score: the official minimum is 60 of 300, but sources disagree (80–120) and the item-by-item table is an internal, unpublished Ministry of Justice manual that changes without notice.
useCases: Foreign founders deciding between the investment route (D-8-1) and the startup route (D-8-4)
Entrepreneurs with a patent checking the D-8-4 technology-startup requirements
Investors confirming the ₩100M / 10%-share rule before wiring funds
D-10-2 (startup-prep) holders mapping the jump to D-8-4
Anyone preparing documents before a hikorea or KOTRA consultation

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Pick your D-8 type: D-8-1 corporate investment, D-8-3 individual investment, or D-8-4 technology startup.
Tick the core requirements shown for that type (investment & shares & corp registration, or degree & IP & new corp & an OASIS mandatory item).
Add the common conduct requirement: no criminal record, fewer than 4 immigration violations, no unpaid taxes.
See what's met and what's missing — then verify your exact eligibility (and, for D-8-4, your OASIS points) at hikorea.go.kr or the Global Startup Immigration Center.

formula.title: How the OASIS points test works (informational)
formula.body:
OASIS (Overall Assistance for Startup Immigration System) scores D-8-4 applicants.
Official pass mark: 60 of 300 — but unofficial sources cite 80–120 as the realistic bar.
Mandatory items (need at least one): a registered/pending IP right, government startup funding or OASIS-6/9 participation, ₩100M+ investment raised, or 3+ years as a professor (E-1)/researcher (E-3).
Optional items add points: OASIS coursework (OASIS-1/2/4/5/8), TOPIK level, and master's/PhD degrees.

⚠️ The full table is an internal, unpublished MOJ manual that changes yearly, so this tool checks the verifiable requirements only and does NOT assert a score.

items.title: Founder with a pending patent (D-8-4)
items.body: Has an overseas bachelor's, a pending patent, and is forming a new corp — three core items met. Still needs at least one OASIS mandatory item and a clean conduct record. Confirm the points at the Global Startup Immigration Center.

items.title: Investor setting up a Korean corp (D-8-1)
items.body: Wires ₩100M+, takes 10%+ of voting shares, and completes corp + foreign-investment-enterprise registration. Immigration still examines business substance and source of funds — small investors face closer scrutiny.

items.title: Investing in a Korean-run company (D-8-3)
items.body: Same ₩100M / 10% rule plus an executive contract. D-8-3 grants the longest single stay among D-8 types (up to 5 years), but the minimum investment is still ₩100M, not ₩300M.

faq.question: What is the D-8 visa?
faq.answer: Korea's business-investment visa for foreigners who run or invest in a Korean company. D-8-1/D-8-3 are investment-based; D-8-4 is for technology startups founded on your own IP.

faq.question: How much do I need to invest?
faq.answer: For D-8-1 and D-8-3, the legal minimum is ₩100M (about US$73K) with 10%+ voting shares, under the Foreign Investment Promotion Act. D-8-4 has no fixed minimum capital in law, but immigration still expects realistic funding.

faq.question: Why doesn't this tool calculate my OASIS score?
faq.answer: The official minimum is 60, but unofficial sources say 80–120, and the detailed point table is an unpublished MOJ manual that changes yearly. Asserting a wrong score could mislead your application, so we check the verifiable requirements and send you to the official source.

faq.question: Can I buy an existing company and get a D-8-4?
faq.answer: No. D-8-4 requires founding a NEW corporation. Acquiring an existing company doesn't qualify for the technology-startup route.

faq.question: Does a patent application (not yet granted) count?
faq.answer: Yes — a pending patent, utility model, or design right counts for D-8-4. You don't have to wait for registration to be completed.

faq.question: What's the recommended path for founders?
faq.answer: Start on D-10-2 (startup-prep, 6 months up to 2 years), join the OASIS program and set up your corporation, then switch to D-8-4 (issued for 12 months at a time).

cautions: OASIS scores and the point table are unofficial/unpublished and change yearly — this is a requirements checklist, not a score calculator.
Meeting the requirements does not guarantee approval; business substance and source of funds are examined at officer discretion.
D-8-4 needs a NEW corporation; a virtual-office address is effectively not accepted — a real office is expected.
The ₩100M minimum is set by law; the ₩300M figure some sources cite is a remittance-rule threshold, not a higher minimum.
Always verify at hikorea.go.kr, the Immigration Contact Center (1345), or the KOTRA Foreign Investment Support Center.

jeonse-wolse.overview: Korea has two rental models: jeonse (a large lump-sum deposit, no monthly rent) and wolse (a smaller deposit plus monthly rent). This tool converts between them using the conversion rate — legally capped at 4.5% (base rate 2.5% + 2%). It also confronts what dominated headlines in 2022–2023: a wave of jeonse fraud (deposit scams) that cost thousands of tenants — foreigners included — their entire deposits. Convert first, then run the prevention checklist below before you sign anything.
useCases: Comparing a jeonse listing against a wolse one at the same value
Checking whether a landlord's jeonse→monthly rate is within the legal cap
Seeing what a deposit is 'worth' in monthly-rent terms
Protecting your deposit from the scams that hit thousands of tenants
Foreigners learning the protections — and the traps unique to them

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Pick a direction: jeonse → monthly, or monthly → jeonse.
Enter the deposit (plus the deposit you'd keep, for jeonse→monthly) or the monthly deposit + rent.
The rate defaults to the 4.5% legal cap — edit it to your landlord's proposed rate.
See the converted amount and whether the rate exceeds the legal cap.

formula.title: Formula · conversion rate
formula.body:
Jeonse → monthly:  rent = (jeonse − kept deposit) × rate ÷ 12
Monthly → jeonse:  jeonse = monthly deposit + (rent × 12) ÷ rate

Legal cap = min(10%, base rate + 2%) = 4.5% (base rate 2.5%, since May 2025).
The cap applies to jeonse→monthly conversion during a lease or at renewal — NOT to a brand-new monthly contract.

items.title: Before you sign
items.body: Check the property register (등기부등본) for the owner's name and any senior mortgages (근저당). Compare market price vs deposit — if the deposit exceeds ~70–80% of the property value, it's an 'underwater jeonse' red flag. Check the landlord's unpaid taxes (allowed without consent for deposits over ₩10M). Verify the agent is licensed. Beware trust-registered (신탁) properties — only the trustee, not the old owner, can lease them.

items.title: At signing & right after
items.body: File your move-in report (전입신고) and get a fixed date (확정일자) — together they give opposing power + priority repayment. Note opposing power starts the day AFTER your report; scammers exploit that one-day gap. Re-check the register on balance-payment day for any new mortgage. Get deposit-return insurance (HUG / SGI).

items.title: Foreigner-specific traps (critical)
items.body: A simple 'residence registration' is NOT enough — as a foreigner you must file a 'change of residence report' under the Immigration Act to get the same opposing power a Korean gets from a move-in report. Many foreigners think they're protected when they aren't. You can get a fixed date with your ARC. Confirm the unit allows move-in reports before signing — some officetels don't.

items.title: If the deposit isn't returned
items.body: Do NOT move out and transfer your registration first. File a 'lease registration order' (임차권등기명령) to keep your opposing power and priority even after you leave. Moving out first can forfeit your protection.

faq.question: What are jeonse and wolse?
faq.answer: Jeonse is a uniquely Korean lease: a large deposit (often 50–80% of the property value) and no monthly rent — the landlord keeps the interest. Wolse is a smaller deposit plus monthly rent. The conversion rate links the two.

faq.question: What was the 2022–2023 jeonse fraud crisis?
faq.answer: Scammers (the 'villa king', 'construction king') bought hundreds of homes with almost no money down using tenants' deposits, then couldn't return them. Tens of thousands were affected and a special victim-relief law runs to 2027. Foreigners were hit too.

faq.question: What is 'underwater jeonse' (깡통전세)?
faq.answer: When the deposit plus senior debt approaches or exceeds the property's value — so if the home is auctioned, you can't recover your deposit. Keep the deposit-to-value ratio under roughly 70–80%.

faq.question: What are opposing power and priority repayment?
faq.answer: Move-in report + occupancy = opposing power (your lease survives a change of owner). Add a fixed date = priority repayment (paid before junior creditors at auction). You need all three: report, occupancy, fixed date.

faq.question: Does my residence registration protect me as a foreigner?
faq.answer: Not by itself. You must file a 'change of residence report' under the Immigration Act — a plain residence registration does NOT create opposing power. This is the #1 foreigner trap, leaving people exposed when they think they're safe.

faq.question: What is HUG deposit-return insurance?
faq.answer: A guarantee that pays your deposit if the landlord can't, then recovers from them. You can join via the Anbsim Jeonse app even if you're not the head of household. Eligibility and limits depend on property value and debt ratio — confirm with HUG.

cautions: Reference tool and educational guide, not legal or tax advice — verify with the property register, HUG, the tax office, a licensed agent, and a lawyer before signing.
The conversion-rate cap applies only to jeonse→monthly during a lease or at renewal, not to new contracts or monthly→jeonse.
Base rate and legal figures change; the 70–80% deposit-to-value line is an industry rule of thumb, not law.
Foreigners: a residence registration alone gives no opposing power — file a change-of-residence report.
Deposit-return insurance eligibility and limits must be confirmed with HUG / SGI.

checklist.title: Before you leave Korea — exit & settlement checklist

groups.heading: Claim your money BEFORE cancelling your ARC
items: Apply for your National Pension (NPS) lump-sum refund — possible if you held an E-8/E-9/H-2 visa, or your country has a reciprocity / social-security agreement (US, Canada, Germany, etc.); China and Japan nationals generally cannot (verify your country).
File at an NPS office within 1 month of departure with your passport, ARC, flight ticket, and a bankbook copy — or use the Incheon Airport NPS desk (Terminal 1) on departure day.
Collect severance pay (퇴직금) if you worked 1+ year — due within 14 days of your last working day.
Ask your employer for a leaver's year-end tax settlement (연말정산) and your withholding receipt (원천징수영수증).
A tax (~6.75%, lower under a treaty) is withheld from the refund, paid in foreign currency (verify the current rate).

groups.heading: Get your housing deposit back
items: Give written notice to end the lease according to your contract's notice period.
If the deposit return is delayed, send a 내용증명 (certified mail); if you must move out unpaid, file a Leasehold Registration Order (임차권등기명령) BEFORE leaving the property.
Settle and close utilities — electricity (KEPCO), gas, water, building fees — and cancel their auto-debits.
Take dated move-out photos; only tenant-caused damage is deductible, not normal wear and tear.

groups.heading: Close accounts & contracts
items: Settle National Health Insurance — leaving a job triggers a final premium adjustment to pay before departure.
Cancel mobile and internet contracts; check early-termination fees and device-installment balances.
Keep one Korean bank account open for ~6 months to receive the pension/tax refunds and final settlements.
Cancel all auto-debits, arrange international shipping, and claim the airport VAT / tax-free refund.

groups.heading: Immigration & departure day
items: Do NOT surrender your ARC until severance is paid and your NPS refund is filed — you need it for both.
Hand your ARC to the immigration officer at the airport on permanent departure.
If you may return within 1–2 years, apply for a Re-Entry Permit via HiKorea instead of surrendering your status.
Call Immigration 1345 or NPS 1355 to confirm any country-specific steps before your flight.

pension-refund.overview: If you worked in Korea and paid into the National Pension, you may be able to reclaim it as a lump-sum refund when you leave the country for good. This tool estimates that amount from your monthly income base and the number of months you contributed. For workplace subscribers the refund includes your employer's half too (the full ~9%), not just the 4.5% you saw deducted. The exact figure is computed month-by-month by NPS with yearly deposit-rate interest, so this is a ballpark — and eligibility depends on your nationality and visa, which the guide explains.
useCases: Foreign workers (E-7, E-9, etc.) leaving Korea wanting a 'how much will I get back' estimate
E-9 / H-2 employment-permit workers, who can claim regardless of nationality
Anyone from a reciprocity or social-security-agreement country checking the payout
Deciding between taking the refund now vs. totalizing the period with a home-country pension
Budgeting before booking a one-way ticket out of Korea

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Enter your monthly income base (your reported salary, capped at ₩6,590,000 from Jul 2026).
Enter the total months you contributed to the National Pension.
Keep the rate at 9% (1998–2025) or set 9.5% for months in 2026+; adjust the interest rate if you know it.
See the estimated principal + interest — then confirm your eligibility and exact amount with NPS (call 1355).

formula.title: How the refund is built (informational)
formula.body:
Estimate used here:
Principal = min(income base, ₩6,590,000) × rate × months
Interest ≈ principal × deposit rate × (months ÷ 12 ÷ 2)

Workplace subscribers get the full 9% back — your 4.5% plus the employer's 4.5% (National Pension Act §77). The contribution rate is 9% through 2025 and rises to 9.5% from January 2026 (then +0.5pp a year to 13% by 2033).

⚠️ NPS calculates the real refund by compounding each month's payment at that year's 3-year deposit rate (about 2.6% in 2025, 3.0% in 2024), so the interest here is only an approximation.

items.title: By visa: E-9 / H-2 (any nationality)
items.body: If you held a Non-Professional Employment (E-9) or Visiting Employment (H-2) visa, you can claim the refund regardless of your country — even if there's no agreement with Korea. (The newer E-8 seasonal-work visa is excluded.)

items.title: By reciprocity or agreement
items.body: Citizens of countries that grant Koreans a similar benefit, or whose social-security agreement has a lump-sum clause, can claim. Examples that can claim include the US, Canada, the Philippines, India, Thailand, Indonesia, Sri Lanka. Some agreement countries (e.g. Vietnam, Sweden, Denmark, Norway) cannot get a lump sum.

items.title: Totalize instead of refunding
items.body: From agreement countries you can often choose to add your Korean contribution period to your home pension instead of cashing out. If you contributed 10+ years, the monthly old-age pension may be worth more long-term than the lump sum.

faq.question: Can every foreigner get a refund?
faq.answer: No. By default foreigners are not entitled to it. You qualify only through (1) reciprocity, (2) a social-security agreement with a lump-sum clause, or (3) an E-9 / H-2 employment-permit visa. Check your case with NPS.

faq.question: Do I get the employer's contribution too?
faq.answer: Yes, for workplace subscribers. Even though only 4.5% was deducted from your pay, the refund returns the full ~9% including the employer's matching half (National Pension Act §77).

faq.question: When and how do I claim?
faq.answer: You can apply from about a month before departure (with proof like a flight ticket), at the airport NPS desk on the day you leave, or from your home country afterward. The money can be wired to an overseas account. The claim expires 5 years after eligibility arises.

faq.question: Is the refund taxed?
faq.answer: Contributions made since 2002 (and their interest) are treated as retirement income and NPS withholds tax at payout; pre-2002 portions and death payouts are tax-free. Your home country may also tax it depending on the tax treaty.

faq.question: What's the 2026 rate change?
faq.answer: The contribution rate rose from 9% to 9.5% on 1 January 2026 and increases 0.5 percentage points each year to 13% by 2033. Use 9% for months before 2026 and 9.5% for 2026 months.

faq.question: Refund now or keep the period?
faq.answer: Taking the refund erases that contribution period — you can't combine it later. If you may return to Korea, or your home country has a totalization agreement, keeping the period can be the better choice.

cautions: Estimate only — NPS computes the exact amount month-by-month with yearly deposit-rate interest.
Eligibility depends on nationality and visa; E-9/H-2 holders qualify regardless of country, but many nationalities otherwise cannot claim.
Taking the lump sum permanently erases that contribution period (no later totalization).
Contributions since 2002 are subject to retirement-income withholding tax.
Confirm your eligibility and exact figure at nps.or.kr or by calling 1355.

loan-calc.overview: The Korean loan calculator compares the three most common Korean repayment types — equal payment (same monthly amount), equal principal (high to low), and balloon (interest only with principal at maturity) — in one screen, with a monthly amortization schedule. The same standard formulas apply to mortgages, credit loans, jeonse-deposit loans, and auto installments. Prepayment fees, stamp duty, and DSR limits are not included.
